> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Basic Structure of a Computer

A **computer** is an electronic, programmable, data-processing machine that accepts input (data and instructions), stores them, processes them according to a program, and produces output (results). The **basic structure** of a computer, as standardized in computer architecture, consists of four fundamental subsystems: the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, the **Memory Unit**, the **Input/Output (I/O) Subsystem**, and the **System Interconnection (Bus)** that links them.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A computer is a fast electronic calculating machine that accepts digitized input information, processes it according to a list of internally stored instructions (the program), and produces the resulting output information.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Human Body
Think of a computer like a human body working in a kitchen:
- The **CPU** is the **chef's brain** — it thinks, decides, and cooks.
- The **Memory** is the **kitchen counter and refrigerator** — it holds ingredients (data) and recipe cards (instructions) close at hand.
- The **Input devices** are the **grocery delivery** — they bring in raw ingredients (data) and recipes (programs).
- The **Output devices** are the **served plate** — the final cooked dish delivered to the customer.
- The **Bus** is the **kitchen corridor** — the pathway along which ingredients, recipes, and finished plates travel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Principle (Von Neumann Architecture)**
> Both **data** and **instructions** of a program are stored in the **same memory unit**. The CPU fetches them one at a time using a single bus. This is the foundation of almost all modern general-purpose computers.

---

## 1.2 Functional Units of a Computer

The computer system is logically divided into **five functional units**, each performing a specific role:

### (a) Input Unit
- Accepts **raw data and instructions** from the user or external environment.
- Converts human-readable information into a binary form the computer can process.
- **Examples**: Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner, Microphone, Touchscreen.

### (b) Memory Unit (Storage Unit)
- Stores **data, instructions, and intermediate results** before, during, and after processing.
- Hierarchically divided into:
  - **Primary Storage (Main Memory)**: RAM, ROM — fast, volatile (RAM), directly accessible by CPU.
  - **Secondary Storage**: Hard Disk, SSD, Optical Disk — slow, non-volatile, high capacity.
- Memory is organized as a sequence of **bytes** or **words**, each with a unique **address**.

### (c) Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)
- Performs all **arithmetic operations** (add, subtract, multiply, divide) and **logic operations** (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, compare, shift).
- Operands are fetched from memory or registers; results are stored back in **Accumulator (AC)** or a general-purpose register.

### (d) Control Unit (CU)
- The **nerve center** of the computer.
- Interprets (decodes) instructions and orchestrates the operation of all other units by generating **control signals**.
- Does **not** process or store data itself; it only directs traffic.
- Implemented as **hardwired control** (using finite state machines) or **microprogrammed control** (using microinstructions stored in a control memory).

### (e) Output Unit
- Converts binary results produced by the computer into a human-understandable form.
- **Examples**: Monitor, Printer, Speakers, LED indicators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined CPU Block**
> For examination purposes, the **ALU + Control Unit + Registers** is collectively referred to as the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**.

---

## 1.3 Basic Operational Concepts

The computer performs its tasks by executing a sequence of **fetch–decode–execute** cycles, often called the **instruction cycle** or **machine cycle**.

### The Three-Step Operational Cycle
1. **Fetch Phase**: The CPU fetches the next instruction from memory (address held in **Program Counter, PC**), using the address bus and reading the data bus.
2. **Decode Phase**: The Control Unit decodes the fetched instruction to determine which operation is to be performed and identifies the operands.
3. **Execute Phase**: The ALU performs the operation. The result is stored in a register or memory, and the PC is incremented to point to the next instruction.

### The Role of Registers
Registers are high-speed storage elements inside the CPU. The most essential ones are:
- **Program Counter (PC)**: Holds the address of the **next instruction** to be fetched.
- **Instruction Register (IR)**: Holds the **currently fetched instruction** while it is being decoded and executed.
- **Accumulator (AC) / General Purpose Registers**: Hold **operands and results** of ALU operations.
- **Memory Address Register (MAR)**: Holds the **memory address** to be accessed.
- **Memory Data Register (MDR)** / **Memory Buffer Register (MBR)**: Temporarily holds the **data being transferred to/from memory**.

### Interrupt-Driven Operation
Modern computers also support **interrupts** — external signals that temporarily halt the CPU's normal sequential flow so that an urgent I/O or error condition can be serviced. After servicing, the CPU resumes its previous task.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite**
> The phrase *"The program counter supplies the address of the next instruction; the instruction is fetched, decoded, and executed"* appears repeatedly in KTU question papers. Memorize this phrasing.

---

## 1.4 Bus Structures

A **bus** is a **shared communication pathway** consisting of a set of common lines (wires) through which data, addresses, and control signals are transmitted between the components of a computer.

### Types of Buses
1. **Data Bus**: Carries the **actual data** (bidirectional). The width of the data bus (e.g., 8, 16, 32, 64 bits) determines the **word size** of the computer.
2. **Address Bus**: Carries the **memory address** of the location to be accessed (unidirectional, from CPU to memory/I/O). The width of the address bus determines the **maximum addressable memory**.
3. **Control Bus**: Carries **timing and control signals** such as Read/Write, Interrupt Request, Clock, and Bus Request.

### Common Bus Architectures

| Architecture | Description | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Bus Structure** | All units (CPU, memory, I/O) share **one common bus** | Simple, low cost, easy expansion | Bottleneck — only one transfer at a time |
| **Multiple Bus Structure** | Separate dedicated buses for different traffic (e.g., Local bus for CPU-Memory, System bus for I/O) | Higher throughput, parallelism | Higher cost, more complex control |
| **System Bus** | Composite of Data + Address + Control bus connecting CPU, memory, and I/O | Standardized, widely used | Still a shared resource |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Formula — Maximum Addressable Memory**
> $$\text{Maximum Addressable Memory} = 2^{n} \text{ bytes}$$
> where *n* is the number of **bits in the address bus**.
> Example: A **32-bit** address bus can address $2^{32} = 4 \text{ GB}$ of memory.

---

## 1.5 Memory Locations and Addresses

### Memory as an Array of Locations
Main memory is viewed by the CPU as a linear **array of storage cells**, each holding **1 byte (8 bits)** by default. Each cell has a unique **binary address**, starting from **0** to $2^{n} - 1$.

### Word and Byte Addressing
- **Byte-addressable memory**: Each address refers to 1 byte (most modern computers).
- **Word-addressable memory**: Each address refers to one full word (e.g., 4 bytes). Less common.
- **Big-endian vs. Little-endian**: Defines the byte-ordering within a word.
  - **Big-endian**: Most Significant Byte (MSB) stored at the **lowest** address.
  - **Little-endian**: Least Significant Byte (LSB) stored at the **lowest** address.

### Address Space vs. Memory Space
- **Address space**: The range of all possible addresses the CPU can generate (determined by address bus width).
- **Memory space**: The actual physical memory installed in the system.
- Address space $\geq$ Memory space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Pitfall**
> Students often confuse **addressability** (the size of each location) with **address bus width** (the number of addressable locations). They are independent parameters.

---

## 1.6 Memory Operations

The CPU communicates with memory using two fundamental operations:

### (a) Memory Read (Load)
The CPU **fetches** data from a memory location.
1. CPU places the desired address on the **address bus**.
2. CPU asserts the **Memory Read** control signal.
3. Memory places the contents of that address on the **data bus**.
4. CPU reads the data from the data bus into a register (typically MDR).

### (b) Memory Write (Store)
The CPU **stores** data into a memory location.
1. CPU places the desired address on the **address bus**.
2. CPU places the data to be written on the **data bus**.
3. CPU asserts the **Memory Write** control signal.
4. Memory stores the data from the data bus into the addressed location.

### Key Timing Parameters
- **Memory Access Time ($t_{access}$)**: Time from when an address is presented to memory until the data is available.
- **Memory Cycle Time ($t_{cycle}$)**: Minimum time between two successive memory operations (read or write). Usually $t_{cycle} \geq t_{access}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Favourite**
> **Bus Bandwidth** (Data Transfer Rate) is calculated as:
> $$\text{Bandwidth} = \frac{\text{Bus Width (bytes)} \times \text{Clock Frequency}}{N \text{ cycles per transfer}}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Operational Cycle Analysis

The CPU operation is governed by a precise, repetitive sequence known as the **Instruction Cycle**. Each instruction passes through several sub-cycles, each consuming one or more **clock cycles (T-states)**.

### 2.1.1 Fetch Sub-Cycle (Universal for all instructions)
$$\text{Steps:} \quad \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Memory Read} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MDR} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$$

| Internal Step | Action | Bus Used | Control Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Place PC contents on the address bus | Address Bus | — |
| 2 | Issue Memory Read command | Control Bus | $\overline{\text{MEMR}}$ |
| 3 | Memory places instruction on data bus | Data Bus | — |
| 4 | Load instruction into IR (via MDR) | Internal path | — |
| 5 | Increment PC | Internal ALU/CU | — |

### 2.1.2 Decode Sub-Cycle
The opcode bits of the IR are sent to the **Instruction Decoder**, which activates the corresponding logic circuits in the ALU and Control Unit.

### 2.1.3 Execute Sub-Cycle
The exact steps depend on the instruction. For example:
- **ADD instruction**: Fetch operand from memory $\rightarrow$ Place in ALU $\rightarrow$ Add to Accumulator $\rightarrow$ Store result back in Accumulator.
- **JUMP instruction**: Replace PC contents with the target address from the instruction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Insight**
> The total time to execute a program is:
> $$\text{CPU Time} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{Cycles per Instruction (CPI)} \times \text{Clock Cycle Time}}{N_{\text{cores}}}$$
> Reducing **CPI** through better instruction design is one of the central goals of computer architecture.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive into Bus Structures

### 2.2.1 Three-State (Tri-State) Bus Buffers
For multiple devices to share a single bus safely, each device's output must be electrically isolated when not in use. This is achieved using **tri-state buffers**, which have three output states:
- **Logic 0** (driven low)
- **Logic 1** (driven high)
- **High Impedance (Hi-Z)** — effectively disconnected

Only one device drives the bus at any time, controlled by **bus arbitration** logic.

### 2.2.2 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Buses
- **Synchronous Bus**: All operations are tied to a **shared clock** line. Simple, fast, but limited to short distances (used inside the CPU and motherboard).
- **Asynchronous Bus**: Operations use **handshaking signals** (request/acknowledge). Slower but more flexible over longer distances (used in some peripheral buses).

### 2.2.3 Bus Arbitration Methods
When multiple devices want to use the bus, an **arbiter** decides who goes first. Common schemes:
- **Daisy Chain Arbitration**: Fixed priority based on physical chain position.
- **Centralized Parallel Arbitration**: Each device has a dedicated request line to a central arbiter.
- **Distributed Arbitration**: Each device contends for the bus using its own priority logic.

---

## 2.3 Memory Organization Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Memory Cell Array
A memory chip of $2^n$ locations, each $k$ bits wide, is internally organized as a **2D array**:
- **Row decoder** (n/2 input lines) selects the row.
- **Column decoder** (n/2 input lines) selects the column.
- This reduces the number of address pins from *n* to *n/2*.

### 2.3.2 Memory Interleaving
To overcome the bottleneck of sequential memory accesses, banks of memory can be **interleaved**:
- **Low-order interleaving**: Consecutive addresses in different banks (e.g., address 0 in bank 0, address 1 in bank 1).
- **High-order interleaving**: Consecutive addresses in the same bank (used for large data structures).
- **N-way interleaving** allows $N$ parallel fetches, increasing effective bandwidth by a factor of $N$.

### 2.3.3 Memory Hierarchy
A fundamental design principle — **faster, smaller, more expensive memory closer to the CPU**:
$$\text{Registers} \rightarrow \text{L1 Cache} \rightarrow \text{L2 Cache} \rightarrow \text{L3 Cache} \rightarrow \text{Main Memory (RAM)} \rightarrow \text{SSD} \rightarrow \text{HDD} \rightarrow \text{Tape}$$

Each level acts as a **cache** for the next, governed by the principle of **locality**:
- **Temporal Locality**: Recently accessed items are likely to be accessed again.
- **Spatial Locality**: Items near a recently accessed address are likely to be accessed soon.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are high-frequency in KTU 2024 Scheme exams.

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Max Memory} = 2^{n}$ | Maximum addressable memory for *n*-bit address bus | bytes / words |
| 2 | $\text{Word Size} = \text{Data Bus Width}$ | Number of bits transferred per memory access | bits |
| 3 | $\text{Bus Bandwidth} = \dfrac{W \times f}{N}$ | $W$ = width, $f$ = frequency, $N$ = cycles/transfer | bytes/sec |
| 4 | $\text{Memory Capacity} = 2^{n} \times k$ | $n$ = address bits, $k$ = bits per location | bits |
| 5 | $\text{CPU Time} = \dfrac{I \times \text{CPI}}{f}$ | $I$ = instruction count, $f$ = clock frequency | seconds |
| 6 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| 7 | $\text{Amdahl's Law: } S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | $f$ = fraction enhanced, $k$ = speedup of that part | dimensionless |
| 8 | $t_{\text{cycle}} \geq t_{\text{access}}$ | Cycle time must accommodate access time | seconds |
| 9 | $N_{\text{pins}} = n + k + c$ | Address pins + Data pins + Control pins (minimum) | pins |
| 10 | $\text{Interleaving Speedup} = N$ | $N$ = number of memory banks (ideal case) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**
> In the table above, the **vertical bar symbol** that would normally be used for absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) has been deliberately replaced with regular parentheses to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. Inside the table cells, this is a markdown safety rule, not a mathematical one.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Von Neumann Bottleneck**: The single bus between CPU and memory is the inspiration for **cache memory** design in modern processors.
- **Bus Bandwidth** calculations directly influence the choice of **DDR4 vs. DDR5**, **PCIe Gen 3 vs. Gen 5**, and **SATA vs. NVMe** in real system design.
- **Memory interleaving** is the reason modern GPUs use **HBM (High Bandwidth Memory)** with hundreds of banks.
- **Address bus width** decisions defined the transition from 32-bit to 64-bit computing — the 4 GB limit of 32-bit systems became obsolete.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Addressable Memory

**Problem Setup:** A processor has an address bus of width *n* bits. Derive the maximum number of memory locations it can address, and express the capacity in bytes, kilobytes, and gigabytes.

**Step 1 — Identify the Count of Unique Binary Patterns**
An *n*-bit binary number can take on $2^{n}$ distinct values. Since the address bus presents a unique binary pattern for each memory access:

$$N_{\text{locations}} = 2^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Bytes**
By default, each addressable location is **1 byte (8 bits)** in byte-addressable systems (the KTU standard assumption):

$$C_{\text{bytes}} = 2^{n} \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Larger Units**
Using the standard hierarchy: $1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10} \text{ bytes}$, $1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes}$, $1 \text{ GB} = 2^{30} \text{ bytes}$:

$$C_{\text{KB}} = \frac{2^{n}}{2^{10}} = 2^{n-10} \text{ KB}$$

$$C_{\text{MB}} = \frac{2^{n}}{2^{20}} = 2^{n-20} \text{ MB}$$

$$C_{\text{GB}} = \frac{2^{n}}{2^{30}} = 2^{n-30} \text{ GB}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:** *n* = 32
$$N = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ locations}$$
$$C = 2^{32} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}$$
This is precisely the well-known 4 GB limit of 32-bit systems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bus Bandwidth Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A system has a 64-bit data bus running at 100 MHz. Each bus transfer takes 4 clock cycles. Compute the peak bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters**
$$\text{Bus Width} = 64 \text{ bits} = 8 \text{ bytes}$$
$$\text{Clock Frequency} = f = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$$
$$\text{Cycles per Transfer} = N = 4$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Number of Transfers per Second**
$$\text{Transfers/sec} = \frac{f}{N} = \frac{100 \times 10^{6}}{4} = 25 \times 10^{6} \text{ transfers/sec}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Bandwidth**
$$\text{Bandwidth} = \text{Transfers/sec} \times \text{Bytes/transfer}$$
$$\text{Bandwidth} = 25 \times 10^{6} \times 8 = 200 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes/sec}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MB/s**
$$\text{Bandwidth} = \frac{200 \times 10^{6}}{2^{20}} = \frac{200 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 190.73 \text{ MB/s}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Memory Capacity of a Chip

**Problem Setup:** A memory chip has 16 address pins and 8 data pins. Find the total capacity.

**Step 1 — Number of Addressable Locations**
$$N_{\text{locations}} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ locations}$$

**Step 2 — Bits per Location (Data Bus Width)**
$$k = 8 \text{ bits/location}$$

**Step 3 — Total Capacity in Bits**
$$C_{\text{bits}} = N \times k = 2^{16} \times 8 = 2^{19} \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Bytes**
$$C_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{2^{19}}{8} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Amdahl's Law (Speedup Limitation)

**Problem Setup:** A program spends 40% of its time in a part that can be sped up by a factor of 5. Compute the overall speedup.

**Step 1 — Identify the Fractions**
$$\text{Enhanced fraction: } f = 0.40$$
$$\text{Remaining fraction: } 1 - f = 0.60$$
$$\text{Local speedup: } k = 5$$

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law**
$$S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$$

$$S = \frac{1}{0.60 + \dfrac{0.40}{5}}$$

$$S = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.08} = \frac{1}{0.68} \approx 1.47$$

**Interpretation:** Despite making 40% of the program 5× faster, the overall program is only **1.47× faster**. This is why diminishing returns force architects to optimize the *critical path*.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Effective CPI and CPU Time

**Problem Setup:** A processor has three instruction classes with the following parameters:

| Class | CPI | Instruction Mix (%) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 1 | 30 |
| B | 2 | 50 |
| C | 4 | 20 |

Clock rate = 500 MHz. Program has $10^{6}$ instructions. Find total CPU time.

**Step 1 — Compute Effective CPI**
$$\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = \sum_{i} (\text{CPI}_i \times \text{Fraction}_i)$$
$$\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = (1 \times 0.30) + (2 \times 0.50) + (4 \times 0.20)$$
$$\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 0.30 + 1.00 + 0.80 = 2.10$$

**Step 2 — Compute Clock Cycle Time**
$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Compute CPU Time**
$$T_{\text{CPU}} = I \times \text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} \times T_{\text{cycle}}$$
$$T_{\text{CPU}} = 10^{6} \times 2.10 \times 2 \times 10^{-9}$$
$$T_{\text{CPU}} = 4.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4.2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compute MIPS Rating**
$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{500 \times 10^{6}}{2.10 \times 10^{6}} \approx 238.1 \text{ MIPS}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Question Types

| Question Type | Key Formula | What Examiners Test | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max addressable memory | $2^{n}$ bytes | Power-of-2 conversion to GB | Confusing bits with bytes |
| Bus bandwidth | $W \times f / N$ | Unit conversion to MB/s | Forgetting cycles per transfer |
| Chip capacity | $2^{n} \times k$ bits | Pin count vs. capacity | Mixing address and data pins |
| Amdahl's speedup | $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/k]$ | Effect of optimizing small fractions | Forgetting the un-enhanced portion |
| CPU time / CPI | $T = I \times \text{CPI} / f$ | Multi-class instruction mix | Using single CPI for mixed program |
| Interleaving speedup | Up to $N$ for *N* banks | When interleaving is effective | Applying to high-order addresses |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Basic Computer

The figure below shows the high-level organization of a single-bus computer, identifying all major functional units and their interconnections. The CPU internally contains the ALU, Control Unit, and the essential registers (PC, AR, IR, DR, AC).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef bus fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef cpu fill:#A7C7E7,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000

    subgraph CPU_block[CPU]
        direction TB
        ALU_node[ALU]
        CU_node[Control Unit]
        PC_node[Program Counter PC]
        AR_node[Address Register AR]
        IR_node[Instruction Register IR]
        DR_node[Data Register DR]
        AC_node[Accumulator AC]
    end

    MEM_node[Main Memory]
    INPUT_node[Input Devices]
    OUTPUT_node[Output Devices]

    CPU_block --- BUS_node
    MEM_node --- BUS_node
    INPUT_node --- BUS_node
    OUTPUT_node --- BUS_node

    BUS_node{System Bus Data Address Control}

    class CPU_block,ALU_node,CU_node,PC_node,AR_node,IR_node,DR_node,AC_node cpu
    class MEM_node mem
    class INPUT_node,OUTPUT_node io
    class BUS_node bus
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **single bus** in the diagram above represents the shared system bus. In a multiple-bus design, the CPU would connect to memory via a dedicated high-speed local bus, and a separate bus would handle I/O traffic.

---

## 4.2 Internal CPU Registers and Data Path

This diagram zooms into the CPU to show how the registers interconnect with the ALU, Control Unit, and the external system bus during a typical fetch–decode–execute cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef reg fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef alu fill:#A7C7E7,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef cu fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000

    PC_n[PC] --> AR_n[AR]
    AR_n --> ADDR[Address Bus]
    ADDR --> MEMEXT[Memory Outside CPU]

    MEMEXT --> DATA[Data Bus]
    DATA --> DR_n[DR]
    DR_n --> IR_n[IR]
    IR_n --> CU_n[Control Unit]
    IR_n --> ALU_n[ALU]
    DR_n --> ALU_n
    AC_n[AC] --> ALU_n
    ALU_n --> AC_n
    CU_n -.control signals.-> ALU_n
    CU_n -.control signals.-> MEMEXT

    class PC_n,AR_n,DR_n,IR_n,AC_n reg
    class ALU_n alu
    class CU_n cu
    class ADDR,DATA,MEMEXT ext
```

---

## 4.3 Memory Read and Memory Write Timing

The diagram below illustrates the two fundamental memory operations as a sequence of bus-level events. The control signals MEMREAD and MEMWRITE distinguish the operations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef step fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#827717,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#A7C7E7,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000

    S1[Step 1: Place address on Address Bus] --> S2
    S2[Step 2: Place data on Data Bus for WRITE only] --> S3
    S3[Step 3: Assert MEMREAD or MEMWRITE control signal] --> S4
    S4[Step 4: Wait for memory access time t access] --> S5
    S5[Step 5: Data is available on Data Bus for READ only] --> S6
    S6[Step 6: Latch data into DR register and complete]

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 step
```

---

## 4.4 Bus Architecture Comparison (Single vs. Multiple)

The architectural flow below contrasts a **single shared bus** (simple but bottlenecked) with a **two-bus organization** (dedicated local bus for CPU–Memory, separate bus for I/O).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef sbus fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef mbus fill:#A7C7E7,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef unit fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000

    subgraph SBus[Single Bus System]
        CPU_S[CPU] --- SBUS_S[Shared System Bus]
        MEM_S[Main Memory] --- SBUS_S
        IO_S[I/O Devices] --- SBUS_S
    end

    subgraph MBus[Multiple Bus System]
        CPU_M[CPU] --- LBUS_M[Local CPU-Memory Bus]
        MEM_M[Main Memory] --- LBUS_M
        CPU_M --- SBUS_M[System I/O Bus]
        IO_M[I/O Devices] --- SBUS_M
    end

    class CPU_S,MEM_S,IO_S unit
    class SBUS_S sbus
    class CPU_M,MEM_M,IO_M unit
    class LBUS_M,SBUS_M mbus
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip for Diagrams**
> KTU examiners award full marks for diagrams that clearly label the **three bus types** (Data, Address, Control) inside a single bus block, and the **five functional units** (Input, Output, Memory, ALU, CU).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define the term **bus**. List the three types of buses used in a computer system and state one function of each.

**Model Answer:**

A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of a set of common signal lines used to transfer data, addresses, and control signals between the components of a computer.

The three types of buses are:
- **Data Bus**: Carries the actual data (operands, instructions) between CPU, memory, and I/O. It is **bidirectional** and its width defines the word size of the processor.
- **Address Bus**: Carries the memory address of the location to be read or written. It is **unidirectional** (CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory/I/O) and its width defines the maximum addressable memory.
- **Control Bus**: Carries timing and control signals such as Memory Read, Memory Write, Interrupt Request, and Clock.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional II]** What is an **interrupt**? How does interrupt-driven I/O improve upon simple program-controlled I/O?

**Model Answer:**

An **interrupt** is an external asynchronous signal sent to the CPU by a hardware device (or generated internally) to request immediate attention. It causes the CPU to **suspend** its current execution, **save the state** (PC, status registers) on the stack, and transfer control to a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. Once the ISR finishes, the saved state is restored and the original program resumes.

**Advantage over program-controlled I/O:**
In program-controlled I/O, the CPU wastes countless cycles **polling** the I/O device to check if it is ready, leading to severe under-utilization. Interrupt-driven I/O eliminates this polling — the CPU continues useful work and is notified **only** when the I/O device actually needs attention. This dramatically improves **CPU utilization** and overall **system throughput**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both options below are full 14-mark questions with sub-parts. KTU allows the student to answer **either** OR.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** Explain the **basic structure of a computer** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each functional unit in detail.

#### (a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a basic computer system. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

The basic structure of a computer consists of four major subsystems connected through a system bus:

1. **Input Unit**: Accepts raw data and instructions from the user. Examples: keyboard, mouse, scanner. It converts human-readable input into binary form.

2. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**: The brain of the computer, comprising:
   - **ALU (Arithmetic and Logic Unit)**: Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, XOR) operations.
   - **Control Unit (CU)**: Directs and coordinates all other units; fetches, decodes, and executes instructions by generating control signals.
   - **Registers**: High-speed internal storage (PC, IR, AC, MAR, MDR) used for temporary data holding and instruction processing.

3. **Memory Unit**: Stores data, instructions, and results. Divided into primary (RAM, ROM — fast, volatile) and secondary (HDD, SSD — slow, non-volatile, high capacity) storage.

4. **Output Unit**: Converts binary results into human-understandable form. Examples: monitor, printer, speakers.

5. **System Bus**: The communication backbone comprising **Address Bus** (carries addresses), **Data Bus** (carries data), and **Control Bus** (carries control signals like Read/Write, Interrupt).

**Diagram** — Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete Mermaid block diagram. In an answer script, draw the standard rectangular blocks connected by a triple-line bus structure, with arrows showing data flow.

---

#### (b) Explain the **instruction fetch and execute cycle** in detail, highlighting the role of PC, IR, MAR, and MDR. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

The CPU executes every instruction by passing it through a repetitive cycle of phases:

**Phase 1 — Fetch Phase** (Common to all instructions)
1. The contents of the **Program Counter (PC)** — the address of the next instruction — are loaded into the **Memory Address Register (MAR)**.
2. The address in MAR is placed on the **Address Bus**, and the **Memory Read** control signal is asserted.
3. The instruction at that memory address is placed on the **Data Bus** and loaded into the **Memory Data Register (MDR)**.
4. The instruction is transferred from MDR to the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
5. The PC is incremented by 1 (to point to the next instruction).

**Phase 2 — Decode Phase**
The opcode portion of the IR is decoded by the Control Unit to determine which operation is required and which operands are needed.

**Phase 3 — Execute Phase** (Instruction-specific)
For example, for an **ADD** instruction:
1. The operand address in the IR is loaded into MAR.
2. A Memory Read is performed; the operand is loaded into MDR.
3. The operand from MDR is added to the contents of the **Accumulator (AC)** by the ALU.
4. The result is stored back in the AC.

**Role of Registers Recap:**
- **PC**: Holds address of next instruction (acts as a pointer).
- **IR**: Holds the currently executing instruction.
- **MAR**: Holds the address for the next memory access.
- **MDR**: Temporarily holds data being transferred to/from memory.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the PC increment step** at the end of the fetch phase — this costs **1 full mark**.
> - Many confuse the **Address Register (AR)** with the **Accumulator (AC)**. AR holds a memory address; AC holds an operand/result. The examiner checks this distinction strictly.
> - In the execute phase, students often forget to specify **which control signals** are asserted (Memory Read, Memory Write). Always mention them.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]** Explain the various **bus structures** used in computer systems. Compare **single bus** and **multiple bus** organizations with suitable diagrams.

#### (a) Explain the **three types of system buses** (Data, Address, Control) with their functions and characteristics. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**1. Data Bus**
- **Function**: Carries the actual data being transferred between CPU, memory, and I/O devices.
- **Direction**: **Bidirectional** — data flows both to and from the CPU.
- **Width**: Typically 8, 16, 32, or 64 bits. The width equals the **word size** of the processor.
- **Characteristic**: Wider data bus $\rightarrow$ more bits transferred per cycle $\rightarrow$ higher performance.

**2. Address Bus**
- **Function**: Carries the memory address of the location to be accessed.
- **Direction**: **Unidirectional** — only the CPU drives it.
- **Width**: Typically 16, 20, 24, 32, or 64 bits. Determines the **maximum addressable memory** ($2^{n}$).
- **Characteristic**: Wider address bus $\rightarrow$ larger memory space.

**3. Control Bus**
- **Function**: Carries **timing and command signals** that synchronize and coordinate the activities of all units.
- **Direction**: **Bidirectional** (some signals in, some out).
- **Typical Signals**:
  - **Memory Read (MEMR)** and **Memory Write (MEMW)**
  - **I/O Read (IOR)** and **I/O Write (IOW)**
  - **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** and **Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA)**
  - **Bus Request (BR)** and **Bus Grant (BG)**
  - **Clock** and **Reset**

---

#### (b) Compare **single bus** and **multiple bus** organizations. Discuss **bus arbitration** and the role of **tri-state buffers**. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Single Bus Organization**
- All units (CPU, memory, I/O) share **one common bus**.
- **Advantage**: Simple, inexpensive, easy to add new devices.
- **Disadvantage**: Only one data transfer can occur at a time — creates a severe **performance bottleneck** known as the **Von Neumann Bottleneck**.
- **Use Case**: Simple embedded systems, early microprocessors (8088).

**Multiple Bus Organization**
- Uses **two or more separate buses** with different functions. Common variant:
  - **Local Bus**: High-speed connection between CPU and main memory.
  - **System Bus**: Connects CPU, main memory, and I/O.
  - **Expansion Bus**: Connects slower peripheral devices.
- **Advantage**: Multiple simultaneous transfers possible, higher throughput.
- **Disadvantage**: More complex control, higher cost.

**Bus Arbitration**
When multiple devices request the bus simultaneously, an **arbiter** must decide who gets control. Common methods:
- **Daisy Chain**: Fixed priority based on physical position (simple but not flexible).
- **Centralized Parallel**: Each device has a dedicated request line; a central arbiter grants the bus.
- **Distributed Arbitration**: Each device determines priority using a self-contained logic circuit.

**Tri-State Buffers**
To allow multiple devices to share a bus **safely**, each device's output is connected through a **tri-state buffer** with three states: **Logic 0**, **Logic 1**, and **High Impedance (Hi-Z)**.
- When a device is **not selected**, its buffer output is in Hi-Z, effectively disconnecting it from the bus.
- When **selected**, the buffer drives the bus with the device's data.
- This prevents **bus contention** (two devices driving conflicting voltages).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - In comparison questions, students often produce a **one-sided answer** (only listing advantages of one type). The examiner expects a **balanced table** with at least 3–4 comparison points.
> - Many students draw a single thick line for the bus without labelling **Data, Address, Control** lines. Always label them.
> - In arbitration, students often forget to mention that the bus can be granted to only **one device at a time** — this is the central reason for arbitration.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1**

- ⭐ A computer has **4 main blocks**: CPU, Memory, I/O, and System Bus.
- ⭐ **CPU = ALU + Control Unit + Registers** (PC, IR, AC, MAR, MDR).
- ⭐ **Von Neumann Architecture** stores both data and instructions in the **same** memory.
- ⭐ The instruction cycle has **3 phases**: **Fetch, Decode, Execute**.
- ⭐ The **PC always points to the next instruction** and is auto-incremented after each fetch.
- ⭐ The **Fetch phase is identical for every instruction**; only the Execute phase varies.
- ⭐ **Three bus types**: **Data** (bidirectional, width = word size), **Address** (unidirectional, width = $n$ bits), **Control** (timing/command signals).
- ⭐ **Maximum addressable memory** = $2^{n}$ bytes (where *n* = address bus width).
- ⭐ 32-bit address bus $\rightarrow$ 4 GB limit (a classic KTU numerical).
- ⭐ **Memory Read vs. Write**: Read uses $\overline{\text{MEMR}}$; Write uses $\overline{\text{MEMW}}$.
- ⭐ **Bus Bandwidth** = $\frac{\text{Bus Width} \times \text{Frequency}}{\text{Cycles per transfer}}$ — always convert to MB/s carefully.
- ⭐ **Tri-state buffers** are essential to prevent bus contention; Hi-Z state means "disconnected".
- ⭐ **Bus arbitration** is needed when multiple devices compete for the bus; common methods are daisy chain, centralized, and distributed.
- ⭐ **Amdahl's Law** reminds us: improving a small fraction of a program yields limited overall speedup.
- ⭐ **Byte-addressable memory** is the KTU default assumption unless stated otherwise.
- ⭐ **Interrupts** save CPU cycles by eliminating polling and dramatically improve system efficiency.
- ⭐ **Endiness** (Big-endian vs Little-endian) is rarely asked directly, but be aware of the term.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip**
> Module 1 is the foundation. KTU frequently tests the **fetch–execute cycle** as a 7-mark sub-question and the **bus structures** as a full 14-mark question. Memorize the register names (PC, IR, MAR, MDR, AC) and the three bus types — they appear in almost every question paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instructions and Instruction Sequencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT304 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Instructions and Instruction Sequencing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Machine Instruction? > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition** > A **machine instruction** is a binary-coded command, stored in memory, that directs the processor (CPU) to perform a specific operation on specified data items (operands). The complete set of such instructions valid for a given processor constitutes its **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**. Every machine instruction must explicitly or implicitly encode: 1. **Operation Code (Opcode)** — *what* to do (e.g., ADD, MOV, JMP). 2. **Operands / Operand Specifiers** — *where* the data resides. 3. **Addressing Mode Information** — *how* to interpret the operand fields. ## 1.2 What is Instruction Sequencing? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition** > **Instruction sequencing** is the systematic, ordered process by which the CPU fetches, decodes, and executes a stream of machine instructions, while correctly handling the **Program Counter (PC)** updates, branch decisions, and subroutine linkage so that the program runs in the intended logical order. ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — "The Recipe & The Cook" | CPU Component | Kitchen Analogy | |---|---| | Instruction | A single step written in a recipe card | | Program (list of instructions) | The full recipe book placed on a shelf | | Memory | The kitchen shelf where recipe cards are stored | | Program Counter (PC) | The chef's pointing finger indicating the *current* card being read | | Instruction Sequencing | The disciplined act of reading card 1, then card 2, then card 3… | | Branch Instruction | A note in the recipe saying *"if sauce is too salty, jump to step 12"* | | Subroutine Call | A reference to a common sub-recipe (e.g., "make roux") reused in many dishes | The chef never randomly grabs a card — he follows a strict, well-defined reading order. Likewise, the CPU never randomly executes an instruction — it follows the **PC**, updating it after every fetch (normally `PC ← PC + 1`, where "1" actually means *length of the current instruction*). ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Word size** in KTU syllabus: typically **16-bit** (for the pedagogical processor) or **32-bit** (modern RISC like ARM/MIPS). - **Memory addressability**: usually **1 byte** per addressable unit. - **Instruction length**: variable in CISC (e.g., x86: 1–15 bytes), fixed in RISC (e.g., ARM: 4 bytes, MIPS: 4 bytes). - **Opcode size**: typically **6 to 8 bits** for fixed-length RISC ISAs. - **Standard memory access time**: denoted as **t (or t_mem)** in timing diagrams. - **Clock period**: **T** (seconds per clock cycle). - **CPU clock frequency**: **f = 1 / T** in **Hz**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1** > Module 1 establishes the foundational vocabulary of the course. Mastery of *instruction format* and *addressing modes* is a prerequisite for understanding pipelining (Module 3), memory hierarchies (Module 4), and I/O (Module 5). KTU board questions frequently combine addressing-mode calculations with PC-update logic — keep both ready. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Instruction Format — Generic Structure A generic machine instruction is partitioned into functional fields: ``` | Opcode | Mode Bits (per operand) | Operand 1 | Operand 2 | ... | ``` The **length** of each field and the **number of explicit operand fields** define the instruction's *address format*. ### 2.1.1 Classification by Number of Explicit Addresses | Address Format | Syntax Example (semantics) | Pros | Cons | Used In | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Three-address** | `ADD R1, R2, R3` → R1 ← R2 + R3 | Powerful, compact programs | Long instruction word | MIPS, ARM (RISC) | | **Two-address** | `ADD R1, R2` → R1 ← R1 + R2 | Moderate length | One source destroyed | x86 (typical) | | **One-address** | `ADD X` → ACC ← ACC + MEM[X] | Short instructions | Implicit Accumulator bottleneck | PDP-8, simple MCUs | | **Zero-address (Stack)** | `ADD` → TOS ← TOS + NOS | Very compact, no operand field | Stack overhead | Java VM, x87 FPU | ### 2.1.2 Classification by Instruction Length - **Fixed-length instructions** — every instruction occupies the same number of bits (e.g., **32 bits** in MIPS). Simplifies fetch & decode; may waste bits. - **Variable-length instructions** — instructions occupy 1 to *n* bytes (e.g., **1–15 bytes** in x86). Compact encoding; complex fetch logic. ## 2.2 Instruction Types (KTU Categorization) | Category | Typical Operations | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Data Transfer** | `LOAD`, `STORE`, `MOVE`, `PUSH`, `POP` | Move data between memory, registers, and stack | | **Data Processing / Arithmetic** | `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `INC` | Perform arithmetic on data | | **Data Processing / Logical** | `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`, `SHL`, `SHR` | Bitwise manipulation and shifting | | **Control Transfer** | `JMP`, `BRANCH`, `CALL`, `RET`, conditional jumps | Alter the sequential flow (modify PC) | | **Input/Output** | `IN`, `OUT` | Communicate with peripheral devices | | **System / Privileged** | `HALT`, `NOP`, `INT`, `TRAP` | System control and OS interaction | ## 2.3 Addressing Modes — The Heart of Instruction Sequencing The **addressing mode** tells the CPU *how to compute the Effective Address (EA)* from the raw bits found in the instruction's operand field. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Effective Address (EA)** is the *final, actual memory address* (or register identifier) that the CPU uses to access the operand. **EA ≠ address field value** in most modes. ### 2.3.1 Why Addressing Modes Matter - They provide **programming flexibility** (program relocation, array indexing, pointer indirection). - They affect **instruction length** (more powerful modes = more bits per operand). - They directly impact the **number of memory accesses** per instruction — a critical KTU exam question. ### 2.3.2 Complete List of Standard Addressing Modes | # | Mode | EA Computation Formula | Memory Accesses to Get Operand | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Immediate** | Operand = value embedded in instruction | **0** (value is in IR) | Loading constants | | 2 | **Direct (Absolute)** | EA = Address field in instruction | **1** | Accessing global/static variables | | 3 | **Register** | EA = Register name (no memory access) | **0** | Fast ALU operations | | 4 | **Register Indirect** | EA = contents of Ri | **1** | Pointer dereferencing, array traversal | | 5 | **Displacement (Based)** | EA = (Ri) + Address field | **1** (plus 0 for register fetch) | Local variables, struct access | | 6 | **Indexed** | EA = (Ri) + Address field (Addr = base, Ri = index) | **1** | Array element access `A[i]` | | 7 | **Relative** | EA = PC + Address field (signed offset) | **1** (if memory operand) | Branch / loop control | | 8 | **Auto-increment** | EA = (Ri); then Ri ← Ri + d | **1** | Sequential list traversal | | 9 | **Auto-decrement** | Ri ← Ri − d; then EA = (Ri) | **1** | Stack push operations | > [!NOTE] > **Note for KTU students** — Modes 5, 6, and 7 are *syntactically identical* in EA formula; the *interpretation* of which field is the base and which is the offset is what differentiates them. The Board expects you to clearly state the convention used in your textbook. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU board question on Module 1.** | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | PC update (sequential) | `PC_new ← PC_old + L` | L = instruction length in addressable units | | PC update (unconditional branch) | `PC_new ← EA_of_branch_target` | — | | PC update (conditional branch) | If condition true → `PC_new ← EA`, else → `PC_new ← PC_old + L` | — | | Branch displacement range | `−2^(n−1) ≤ offset ≤ (2^(n−1)) − 1` | n = bits in offset field | | Effective Address — Displacement | `EA = (BaseReg) + signed_offset` | bytes / words depending on addressing | | Effective Address — Indexed | `EA = (IndexReg) + base_address` | — | | Effective Address — Relative | `EA = PC + signed_offset` | offset is **relative to next PC** (PC + L) | | Stack growth (descending) | `SP_new ← SP_old − word_size` (on PUSH) | Full descending = SP points to last item | | Total memory accesses for operand | `N_mem = 1` (direct/indirect) or `0` (register/immediate) | — | | Instruction size (MIPS style) | `Size = 32 bits` | Fixed | | Instruction size (x86) | `Size ∈ [8, 15, 24, …, 120] bits` | Variable, multiples of 8 | > [!NOTE] > In the Relative mode used in KTU problems, the displacement is **almost always measured from the *next* instruction's PC** (i.e., PC + L), not from the *current* PC. Forgetting this `+L` adjustment is the single most common error in branch-target calculations. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compiler Design** — compilers map high-level constructs to addressing modes: `*p` → register-indirect, `a[i]` → indexed, `if (x>0) goto L` → PC-relative conditional branch. - **Operating Systems** — position-independent code (PIC/shared libraries) relies on **PC-relative addressing** so that code can be loaded at any memory address. - **Embedded Systems** — auto-increment mode is heavily used in DSPs for FIR filter coefficient loops. - **Cybersecurity** — understanding addressing modes is essential to reverse engineering and exploit development (e.g., shellcode uses clever addressing tricks to avoid null bytes). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Effective Address for the Five Most-Examined Modes We will derive the EA step-by-step using a generic 32-bit instruction, where the operand field contains a 16-bit value denoted as `A` (address or signed offset) and `Ri` is a general-purpose register. ### 3.1.1 Immediate Mode $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Operand value } V &:= A \quad \text{(the literal is embedded in the instruction)} \\ \text{Effective Address EA} &:= \text{not applicable (no memory lookup)} \\ \text{Memory accesses for operand } N_{\text{mem}} &:= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The bits of the instruction itself hold the data, so the CPU extracts `A` directly from the Instruction Register (IR) without touching memory. --- ### 3.1.2 Direct (Absolute) Mode $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Instruction field} &:= A \quad (\text{16-bit unsigned address}) \\ \text{Step 1: Read operand field from IR} & \Rightarrow A \\ \text{Step 2: Compute EA} & \Rightarrow EA = A \\ \text{Step 3: Fetch operand} & \Rightarrow \text{Operand} = M[A] \\ \text{Memory accesses } N_{\text{mem}} &:= 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The operand field *is* the memory address. No arithmetic, no indirection. --- ### 3.1.3 Register Indirect Mode $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Instruction field} &:= R_i \quad (\text{register number}) \\ \text{Step 1: Read register number from IR} & \Rightarrow i \\ \text{Step 2: Retrieve pointer from register file} & \Rightarrow P = (R_i) \\ \text{Step 3: Compute EA} & \Rightarrow EA = P \\ \text{Step 4: Fetch operand} & \Rightarrow \text{Operand} = M[P] \\ \text{Memory accesses } N_{\text{mem}} &:= 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The register holds a *pointer*; one memory read dereferences the pointer. --- ### 3.1.4 Displacement (Based) Mode $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Instruction fields} &:= (R_i,\; A) \quad \text{where } R_i \text{ = base, } A \text{ = signed offset} \\ \text{Step 1: Read register number and offset from IR} & \Rightarrow i,\; A \\ \text{Step 2: Retrieve base value from register file} & \Rightarrow B = (R_i) \\ \text{Step 3: Sign-extend the 16-bit } A \text{ to 32 bits} & \Rightarrow A_{32} \\ \text{Step 4: Add base + signed offset} & \Rightarrow EA = B + A_{32} \\ \text{Step 5: Fetch operand} & \Rightarrow \text{Operand} = M[EA] \\ \text{Memory accesses } N_{\text{mem}} &:= 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** A base register holds the starting address of a data structure; the offset reaches into the structure. --- ### 3.1.5 Relative (PC-Relative) Mode — *Most-KTU-Tested* This is used in branch instructions. The displacement is measured from the **address of the *next* instruction**, not the current one. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Instruction fields} &:= (\text{opcode},\; A) \quad \text{where } A \text{ is a signed branch offset} \\ \text{Current PC value} &:= PC_{\text{cur}} \\ \text{Instruction length (addressable units)} &:= L \\ \text{Step 1: Read signed offset } A \text{ from IR} & \Rightarrow A \\ \text{Step 2: Sign-extend } A \text{ to full word width} & \Rightarrow A_{32} \\ \text{Step 3: Compute address of next instruction} & \Rightarrow PC_{\text{next}} = PC_{\text{cur}} + L \\ \text{Step 4: Add signed offset to next PC} & \Rightarrow EA = (PC_{\text{cur}} + L) + A_{32} \\ \text{Step 5: If condition true} & \Rightarrow PC_{\text{new}} = EA \\ \text{Step 6: If condition false} & \Rightarrow PC_{\text{new}} = PC_{\text{cur}} + L \\ \text{Memory accesses for branch target } N_{\text{mem}} &:= 0 \text{ (EA is computed, not fetched)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** Some textbooks define the offset relative to the *current* PC. The KTU-pedagogical-processor convention (used in Hamacher/Vranesic) is **relative to the next PC** (i.e., `PC + L`). Always verify which convention your Module 1 notes adopt and stick to it consistently. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — A Complete Address Calculation **Problem (typical KTU style):** A processor has a 32-bit instruction format. Branch instructions use **PC-relative addressing** with a **16-bit signed offset** field. The current PC value is `2000`. The current instruction length is `4` bytes. The branch offset field contains the two's-complement bit pattern `1111 1111 1111 1100`. **Find:** The branch target address (EA). **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Identify current PC} & \Rightarrow PC_{\text{cur}} = 2000 \\ \text{Step 2: Instruction length} & \Rightarrow L = 4 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Step 3: Compute next PC} & \Rightarrow PC_{\text{next}} = 2000 + 4 = 2004 \\ \text{Step 4: Decode signed offset (16 bits)} & \Rightarrow \text{bits} = 1111\;1111\;1111\;1100 \\ \text{Step 5: Find MSB} & \Rightarrow \text{MSB} = 1 \Rightarrow \text{number is negative} \\ \text{Step 6: Take two's complement to get magnitude} & \Rightarrow \\ \sim\, 1111\;1111\;1111\;1100 & = 0000\;0000\;0000\;0011 \\ +\,1 & = 0000\;0000\;0000\;0100 = 4 \\ \therefore A_{16} & = -4 \\ \text{Step 7: Sign-extend to 32 bits} & \Rightarrow A_{32} = 0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0100 \text{ (as } -4\text{)} \\ \text{Step 8: Compute EA} & \Rightarrow EA = PC_{\text{next}} + A_{32} = 2004 + (-4) = 2000 \\ \text{Step 9: Verify} & \Rightarrow \text{branch loops back to the instruction at address 2000.} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Number of Memory Accesses per Addressing Mode This matrix is a **board-exam favourite** for 3-mark and 7-mark sub-part questions. | Addressing Mode | Registers Read | Memory Reads (for operand) | Memory Writes (for store) | Total Memory Cycles (for LOAD) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Immediate | 0 | 0 | 0 | **0** | | Direct | 0 | 1 | 1 | **1** | | Register | 1 | 0 | 0 | **0** | | Register Indirect | 1 | 1 | 1 | **1** | | Displacement | 1 | 1 | 1 | **1** | | Indexed | 1 | 1 | 1 | **1** | | Relative (for branch) | 0 | 0 | 0 | **0** | | Auto-increment | 1 | 1 + 0 (no write-back to mem) | 1 | **1** | | Auto-decrement | 1 | 1 | 1 | **1** | > [!NOTE] > **Why Register and Immediate modes are "free" in cycles** — They keep the operand inside the CPU (in a register or in the instruction fetch buffer), avoiding the slow main-memory bottleneck. This is precisely why RISC architectures (MIPS, ARM) heavily favour register-based instructions. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Instruction Format — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Instruction Word]:::instWord --> B[Opcode Field] A --> C[Mode Bit 1] A --> D[Mode Bit 2] A --> E[Operand Field 1] A --> F[Operand Field 2] B:::opcode --> G[Decoder / Control Unit] C:::mode --> G D:::mode --> G E:::operand --> H[Effective Address Computation Unit] F:::operand --> H G --> H H --> I[Operand Fetched from Memory or Register File]:::output classDef instWord fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef opcode fill:#7AC0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef mode fill:#A6E3A1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef operand fill:#FFB3BA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#D7BDE2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **opcode** and **mode bits** are decoded by the Control Unit. - The **operand fields** are fed to the Effective Address Computation Unit. - The control unit tells the EA unit *which mode to use*. - The final EA is then sent to memory (or register file) to fetch the operand. --- ## 4.2 Instruction Sequencing — The Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Power ON / Reset]):::startEnd --> Init[PC ← Starting Address]:::step Init --> FetchStep{Start of
New Cycle}:::decision FetchStep --> Fetch[FETCH Phase
1. MAR ← PC
2. Read Memory M MAR to IR
3. PC ← PC + L]:::fetch Fetch --> Decode[DECODE Phase
1. Opcode → Control Unit
2. Decode Addressing Mode
3. Read Operands from Registers]:::decode Decode --> OperandFetch[OPERAND FETCH PHASE
1. Compute EA using addressing mode
2. Read M EA if needed]:::operand OperandFetch --> Execute[EXECUTE PHASE
1. ALU performs operation
2. Set Condition Codes / Flags]:::execute Execute --> WriteBack[WRITE-BACK PHASE
1. Store result in Register or Memory]:::writeBack WriteBack --> BranchCheck{Is this a
Branch or Jump?}:::decision BranchCheck -- Yes, taken --> UpdatePC[PC ← EA of branch target]:::branch BranchCheck -- No --> Loop[Proceed to next sequential instruction]:::loop UpdatePC --> FetchStep Loop --> FetchStep HALT([HALT instruction encountered]):::startEnd Execute --> HALT classDef startEnd fill:#333,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef step fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fetch fill:#A6E3A1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef decode fill:#7AC0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef operand fill:#FFB3BA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef execute fill:#D7BDE2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef writeBack fill:#F5CBA7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef branch fill:#F1948A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef loop fill:#82E0AA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Addressing Mode Hierarchy — Tree View ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[Operand Specifier in Instruction]:::root --> ClassA[Class A: Operand in Instruction Itself]:::classA Root --> ClassB[Class B: Operand in Register]:::classB Root --> ClassC[Class C: Operand in Memory]:::classC ClassA --> A1[Immediate Mode
Operand = literal bits]:::leaf ClassA --> A2[Direct Mode
EA = address field]:::leaf ClassB --> B1[Register Mode
Operand = contents of Ri]:::leaf ClassB --> B2[Register Indirect Mode
EA = contents of Ri]:::leaf ClassC --> C1[Displacement Based
EA = Ri + offset]:::leaf ClassC --> C2[Indexed
EA = Ri + base addr]:::leaf ClassC --> C3[Relative
EA = PC + offset]:::leaf ClassC --> C4[Auto-increment
EA = Ri, then Ri + 1]:::leaf ClassC --> C5[Auto-decrement
Ri - 1, then EA = Ri]:::leaf classDef root fill:#333,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef classA fill:#7AC0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef classB fill:#A6E3A1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef classC fill:#FFB3BA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Every operand specifier falls into one of three fundamental classes — embedded in the instruction, in a CPU register, or in main memory. From each class, the specific addressing mode is selected by the mode-bits field. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part A questions test **Remembering** and **Understanding** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be crisp, definition-style, 3–4 sentences each. ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **List and briefly explain any three addressing modes used in computer organization.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Immediate Addressing** — the operand value is embedded directly in the instruction field. Example: `MOV R1, #5` loads the constant 5 into R1. No memory access is required to fetch the operand. 2. **Direct (Absolute) Addressing** — the instruction contains the full memory address of the operand. Example: `LOAD R1, 2000` fetches the value stored at memory location 2000 into R1. Requires one memory read. 3. **Register Indirect Addressing** — the instruction specifies a register that *contains* the memory address of the operand. Example: `LOAD R1, (R2)`, where R2 holds a pointer. One memory read is needed to dereference the pointer. *(Any three correct modes with definitions and examples fetch full 3 marks.)* --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the function of the Program Counter (PC) during instruction sequencing? Explain how it is updated for (i) sequential execution and (ii) an unconditional branch instruction.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Program Counter (PC)** is a special CPU register that holds the **address of the next instruction to be fetched**. It is the central element that drives instruction sequencing. - **(i) Sequential execution:** After each instruction is fetched, the PC is automatically incremented by the length `L` of the just-fetched instruction: `PC_new = PC_old + L`. This moves the CPU to the next instruction in memory. - **(ii) Unconditional branch:** The PC is *overwritten* with the effective address (EA) computed from the branch target: `PC_new = EA_branch`. This redirects the fetch to a non-sequential instruction, breaking the default linear flow. *(1 mark for defining PC + 1 mark each for the two update rules.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions test **Applying**, **Analyzing**, and **Evaluating** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Each carries **14 marks** and must be answered in **about 7–8 pages of a standard answer booklet**. Internal choice is *within* a single Part-B sub-question — students answer either (a) OR (b) from each group. --- ### Question Set 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]` #### ✦ Question 1A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **three-address**, **two-address**, **one-address**, and **zero-address** instruction formats. For each format, write a sample instruction to compute the expression `X = (A + B) × (C + D)`, and state one advantage and one disadvantage. **Model Solution:** | Format | Sample Instruction Sequence | Advantage | Disadvantage | |---|---|---|---| | **Three-address** | `ADD R1, A, B` → R1 ← A+B
`ADD R2, C, D` → R2 ← C+D
`MUL X, R1, R2` → X ← R1×R2 | Programs are very short and readable | Each instruction is long → larger program memory footprint | | **Two-address** | `MOV R1, A` → R1 ← A
`ADD R1, B` → R1 ← A+B
`MOV R2, C` → R2 ← C
`ADD R2, D` → R2 ← C+D
`MUL R1, R2` → R1 ← R1×R2
`MOV X, R1` | Reasonable balance of length and program size | One source operand is destroyed (limits parallelism) | | **One-address** (Accumulator) | `LOAD A` → ACC ← A
`ADD B` → ACC ← A+B
`STORE T1` → T1 ← ACC
`LOAD C` → ACC ← C
`ADD D` → ACC ← C+D
`MUL T1` → ACC ← T1×(C+D)
`STORE X` → X ← ACC | Very compact instruction encoding (short words) | Accumulator becomes a severe bottleneck; many memory accesses | | **Zero-address** (Stack) | `PUSH A`
`PUSH B`
`ADD` → TOS ← A+B
`PUSH C`
`PUSH D`
`ADD` → TOS ← C+D
`MUL` → TOS ← (A+B)×(C+D)
`POP X` | Shortest possible instructions; no operand field needed | Stack management overhead; not friendly to register allocation | *(3 marks formats, 2 marks examples, 2 marks pros/cons — total 7.)* **(b) [7 marks]** A 32-bit processor uses a **16-bit signed offset** in branch instructions. The current PC = `4000`, instruction length = `4` bytes, and the offset field contains the bits `1111 1111 1110 1100`. Compute the **branch target address** in **bytes**. Also compute the **range of branch reach** in bytes (forward and backward). **Model Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(i) Decode signed offset from bits: } & 1111\;1111\;1110\;1100 \\ \text{MSB} = 1 & \Rightarrow \text{negative number} \\ \text{Magnitude via two's complement:} & \Rightarrow \\ \sim\, 1111\;1111\;1110\;1100 & = 0000\;0000\;0001\;0011 \\ + 1 & = 0000\;0000\;0001\;0100 = 20 \\ \therefore A_{16} & = -20 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Sign-extend to 32 bits:} & \Rightarrow A_{32} = 0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0001\;0100 \text{ as } -20 \\ \text{Compute next PC:} & \Rightarrow PC_{\text{next}} = 4000 + 4 = 4004 \\ \text{Branch target EA:} & \Rightarrow EA = 4004 + (-20) = 3984 \\ \text{Verification:} & \Rightarrow \text{Branch goes back from 4000 to 3984 (looping back 20 bytes)}. \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(ii) Range of branch offset with 16-bit signed field:} & \\ \text{Minimum offset} & = -2^{15} = -32768 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Maximum offset} & = +2^{15} - 1 = +32767 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Therefore, branch reach (backward)} & = \mathbf{32768} \text{ bytes} \\ \text{And branch reach (forward)} & = \mathbf{32767} \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ *(3 marks EA computation, 2 marks range formula, 2 marks final values — total 7.)* --- #### ✦ Question 1B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **fetch–decode–execute cycle** of a single instruction. Clearly show the role of **MAR**, **MBR**, **IR**, **PC**, and the **Control Unit**. **Model Solution:** The CPU executes every instruction by repeating the **fetch–decode–execute** cycle. The key registers and their roles are: | Register | Full Name | Function | |---|---|---| | **PC** | Program Counter | Holds address of the *next* instruction to fetch | | **MAR** | Memory Address Register | Holds the address to be sent to memory for read/write | | **MBR** (or MDR) | Memory Buffer/Data Register | Holds the data being transferred to/from memory | | **IR** | Instruction Register | Holds the *currently executing* instruction after fetch | | **Control Unit** | CU | Decodes opcode and generates control signals | **Cycle Phases (textual block diagram description):** 1. **Fetch Phase** - `MAR ← PC` — send next instruction's address to memory. - `MBR ← M[MAR]` — read memory; the instruction word comes back into MBR. - `IR ← MBR` — copy the instruction into the Instruction Register. - `PC ← PC + L` — increment PC by the instruction length. 2. **Decode Phase** - Control Unit examines `IR.opcode`. - Mode bits and operand specifiers are extracted and routed to the EA unit. 3. **Operand Fetch Phase** - Compute EA using the decoded addressing mode. - If a memory operand is needed, `MAR ← EA`, then `MBR ← M[MAR]`. 4. **Execute Phase** - ALU performs the operation (ADD, MUL, branch, etc.). - Condition codes (Zero, Negative, Carry, Overflow) are updated. 5. **Write-Back / Branch Resolution Phase** - For register target → write to register file. - For branch taken → `PC ← EA_target`; for branch not taken → PC is left as `PC + L`. 6. **Loop** — return to Fetch Phase. *(3 marks register roles, 2 marks phases, 2 marks block diagram description — total 7. Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the graphical Mermaid cycle.)* **(b) [7 marks]** A processor uses the following addressing modes. For each mode, give the **EA formula** and state the **number of memory accesses** required to fetch the operand: (i) Register indirect, (ii) Indexed, (iii) Displacement, (iv) Auto-increment, (v) Relative, (vi) Immediate, (vii) Direct. **Model Solution:** | Mode | EA Formula | Memory Accesses | |---|---|---| | (i) Register indirect | `EA = (R_i)` | **1** | | (ii) Indexed | `EA = (R_i) + base_address` | **1** | | (iii) Displacement | `EA = (R_b) + signed_offset` | **1** | | (iv) Auto-increment | `EA = (R_i);` then `R_i ← R_i + d` | **1** | | (v) Relative | `EA = PC + signed_offset` | **0** (computed, not fetched) | | (vi) Immediate | Operand is in instruction itself | **0** | | (vii) Direct | `EA = address_field` | **1** | *(1 mark per row × 7 = 7 marks. Deduct ½ mark for missing units; deduct 1 mark for confusing relative-mode EA with the branch target address.)* --- ### Question Set 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` #### ✦ Question 2A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 marks]** Compare **RISC** and **CISC** instruction set architectures with respect to **(i) instruction length**, **(ii) addressing modes**, **(iii) number of clock cycles per instruction**, **(iv) code size**, and **(v) examples**. State which architecture is dominant in modern mobile and embedded processors and justify. **Model Solution:** | Parameter | RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) | CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) | |---|---|---| | (i) Instruction length | **Fixed** (e.g., 32 bits in MIPS) | **Variable** (1–15 bytes in x86) | | (ii) Addressing modes | **Few** (5–6 main modes) | **Many** (12+ modes in x86) | | (iii) Clocks per instruction | **CPI ≈ 1** (most complete in one cycle) | **CPI > 1** (multi-cycle complex instructions) | | (iv) Code size | **Larger** (need more instructions for same task) | **Smaller** (one complex instruction replaces several) | | (v) Examples | MIPS, ARM, RISC-V, SPARC | Intel x86, x86-64, Motorola 68000 | | (vi) Pipelining | Highly pipelined | Harder to pipeline | | (vii) Memory access | Only LOAD/STORE access memory | Many operations can access memory directly | **Dominant architecture in modern mobile/embedded:** **ARM (RISC)**. Justification: - Low power consumption (single-cycle instructions simplify clock gating). - Predictable CPI allows aggressive pipelining and clock-frequency scaling. - Compiler-friendly fixed encoding simplifies the toolchain. - Massive mobile ecosystem (smartphones, IoT, tablets) is built on ARM cores (Cortex-A, Cortex-M). *(2 marks table, 3 marks examples, 2 marks justification — total 7.)* **(b) [7 marks]** A 32-bit instruction set has the format: **Opcode (6 bits) | Mode (2 bits) | Operand 1 (12 bits) | Operand 2 (12 bits)**. Mode `00` = direct, `01` = immediate, `10` = register indirect, `11` = displacement. Decode the following 32-bit machine instruction in **hexadecimal** `0x4A0C3005` and identify the operation. **Model Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Convert hex to binary} & \Rightarrow 0x4A0C3005 = \\ \text{4} = 0100,\; A = 1010,\; 0 = 0000,\; C = 1100,\; 3 = 0011,\; 0 = 0000,\; 0 = 0000,\; 5 = 0101 \\ \therefore \text{binary} & = 0100\;1010\;0000\;1100\;0011\;0000\;0000\;0101 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2: Slice the bit-fields (total 32 bits):} & \\ \text{Opcode (bits 31–26)} & = 010010 = \mathbf{18} \\ \text{Mode (bits 25–24)} & = 10 = \mathbf{2} \Rightarrow \text{Register Indirect} \\ \text{Operand 1 (bits 23–12)} & = 0000\;1100\;0011 = 0x0C3 = \mathbf{195} \\ \text{Operand 2 (bits 11–0)} & = 0000\;0000\;0101 = 0x005 = \mathbf{5} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 3: Interpret:} & \\ \text{Opcode 18} & \Rightarrow \text{(assume ADD per system reference)} \\ \text{Mode 2 (Register Indirect)} & \Rightarrow \text{Operand 1 = (R5), Operand 2 = (R195)} \\ \text{Decoded instruction} & = \text{ADD R?} \leftarrow \text{M[R5] + M[R195]} \\ & \Rightarrow \text{Result register depends on the opcode-table convention (often } R_{\text{default}} = R0 \text{ or the first operand register).} \end{aligned} $$ *(2 marks binary conversion, 3 marks slicing, 2 marks final interpretation — total 7.)* --- #### ✦ Question 2B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **PC-relative addressing mode** in detail. A branch instruction at address `1500` (length 4 bytes) has an offset field of value `+24` (decimal). Find the **branch target address**. If the offset field were `−32`, what would the target be? **Model Solution:** In **PC-relative addressing**, the effective address (branch target) is computed by adding a signed displacement to the value of the Program Counter. By the KTU/standard convention, the PC value used is that of the **next sequential instruction** (i.e., `PC + L` after the current fetch). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Case 1: Offset} = +24: & \\ PC_{\text{cur}} & = 1500 \\ L & = 4 \\ EA & = (1500 + 4) + 24 = 1504 + 24 = \mathbf{1528} \\ \text{Interpretation:} & \Rightarrow \text{Branch skips forward 24 bytes from the next instruction.} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Case 2: Offset} = -32: & \\ PC_{\text{cur}} & = 1500 \\ L & = 4 \\ EA & = (1500 + 4) + (-32) = 1504 - 32 = \mathbf{1472} \\ \text{Interpretation:} & \Rightarrow \text{Branch jumps backward 32 bytes from the next instruction, landing at 1472.} \end{aligned} $$ *(3 marks mode explanation, 2 marks Case 1, 2 marks Case 2 — total 7.)* **(b) [7 marks]** Explain **auto-increment** and **auto-decrement** addressing modes. Show, using a step-by-step trace, how each mode operates when used to traverse a contiguous array of `N` elements stored in memory starting at address `BASE`. **Model Solution:** **Auto-increment mode:** The effective address is the *current* contents of register `R_i`, and *after* the operand is fetched, the register is automatically incremented by `d` (the data size: 1 for byte, 2 for word, 4 for double-word). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{For iteration } k \text{ (0-indexed):} & \\ EA_k & = (R_i)_k \\ \text{Operand}_k & = M[EA_k] \\ (R_i)_{k+1} & = (R_i)_k + d \end{aligned} $$ **Trace for array traversal using auto-increment (R1 initially = BASE, d = 4):** | Step k | R1 (before) | EA | Operand (M[EA]) | R1 (after) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | BASE + 0 | BASE + 0 | `arr[0]` | BASE + 4 | | 1 | BASE + 4 | BASE + 4 | `arr[1]` | BASE + 8 | | 2 | BASE + 8 | BASE + 8 | `arr[2]` | BASE + 12 | | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | | N−1 | BASE + 4(N−2) | BASE + 4(N−1) | `arr[N−1]` | BASE + 4N | **Auto-decrement mode:** The register is decremented *first*, and then the new contents are used as the EA. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{For iteration } k \text{ (0-indexed):} & \\ (R_i)_{k+1} & = (R_i)_k - d \\ EA_k & = (R_i)_{k+1} \\ \text{Operand}_k & = M[EA_k] \end{aligned} $$ **Trace for auto-decrement (R1 initially = BASE + 4N, d = 4):** | Step k | R1 (after decrement) | EA | Operand (M[EA]) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | BASE + 4N − 4 | BASE + 4N − 4 | `arr[N−1]` | | 1 | BASE + 4N − 8 | BASE + 4N − 8 | `arr[N−2]` | | 2 | BASE + 4N − 12 | BASE + 4N − 12 | `arr[N−3]` | | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | | N−1 | BASE | BASE | `arr[0]` | **Engineering significance:** Auto-increment is used for **forward traversal** (e.g., reading a file buffer, iterating an array left-to-right). Auto-decrement is used for **stack push** operations (descending stack growth) and for reverse-order processing. *(3 marks mode description, 2 marks auto-increment trace, 2 marks auto-decrement trace — total 7.)* --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks on this topic — read carefully:** > 1. **Forgetting the `+L` correction in PC-relative addressing** — Always add the instruction length to PC *before* applying the offset. The displacement is relative to the *next* PC, not the current one. Losing 1–2 marks per question. > 2. **Confusing displacement mode with indexed mode** — In displacement, the register holds the base and the constant is the offset; in indexed, the constant is the base and the register is the index. Mixing them costs full marks. > 3. **Sign-extension errors in two's-complement decoding** — When given a negative offset bit pattern, many students forget to take the two's complement to find the magnitude. Always show the two's-complement step explicitly. > 4. **Reporting wrong memory-access counts** — Immediate and Register modes have *zero* memory accesses for the operand. Relative mode has zero accesses for the *branch target computation* (it's a PC arithmetic operation, not a memory read). Conflating these loses marks. > 5. **Not labelling the Mermaid-style block diagrams in the answer sheet** — When asked to "draw the fetch–decode–execute cycle," students often draw a flow but forget to label the registers (PC, MAR, MBR, IR) on the arrows. The examiner deducts 1–2 marks for missing labels. > 6. **Confusing addressing modes with data types** — A `BYTE` operand with displacement still uses the same EA formula; only `d` (data size) changes for auto-increment/decrement. > 7. **Mixing up "address field" and "effective address"** — The address field is a *constant in the instruction*; the effective address is the *final computed address* after mode resolution. Examiners will check this distinction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam** - **Machine Instruction** = Opcode + Operand field(s) + Mode bit(s). - **Instruction Sequencing** = ordered Fetch → Decode → Operand-fetch → Execute → Write-back → PC-update cycle. - **PC update (sequential)**: `PC ← PC + L`, where `L` is the instruction length in addressable units. - **PC update (branch)**: `PC ← EA`, where `EA = (PC + L) + signed_offset` in PC-relative mode. - **Effective Address (EA)** ≠ address field; it is the *final* address after mode resolution. - **Immediate mode** = operand is in the instruction itself → **0 memory accesses**. - **Register mode** = operand is in a register → **0 memory accesses**. - **Direct mode** = `EA = address field` → **1 memory access**. - **Register indirect** = `EA = (R_i)` → **1 memory access**. - **Displacement / Indexed** = `EA = (R_i) + offset` → **1 memory access**. - **PC-relative** = `EA = (PC + L) + offset` → **0 memory accesses** for the target computation. - **Auto-increment**: `EA = (R_i)` first, *then* `R_i ← R_i + d`. - **Auto-decrement**: `R_i ← R_i − d` first, *then* `EA = (R_i)`. - **Three-address format** keeps programs short but makes each instruction long; **one-address / accumulator** keeps instructions short but requires more instructions. - **RISC** uses fixed-length instructions, fewer addressing modes, and `LOAD/STORE` memory access only. - **CISC** uses variable-length instructions, many addressing modes, and allows memory operands in arithmetic operations. - **Sign-extension** of a 16-bit signed offset to 32 bits is *required* before addition to PC. - **Branch offset range** with `n`-bit signed field: `−2^(n−1)` to `+2^(n−1) − 1`. - **Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle** always uses the same five registers: PC, MAR, MBR/MDR, IR, and the Control Unit. - **Condition codes (flags)** — Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow — are set during the Execute phase and consumed by the next conditional branch.

topics.title: Addressing Modes, Basic Processing Unit - Fundamental Concepts - Instruction Cycle - Execution of a Complete Instruction - Single Bus and Multiple Bus Organization, Register Transfer Logic: Inter Register Transfer - Arithmetic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT304 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Addressing Modes, Basic Processing Unit - Fundamental Concepts - Instruction Cycle - Execution of a Complete Instruction - Single Bus and Multiple Bus Organization, Register Transfer Logic: Inter Register Transfer - Arithmetic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Addressing Modes

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Addressing Modes are the various techniques used in computer architecture to specify the operand(s) of an instruction. They define how the effective address of an operand is calculated from the bits given in the instruction, determining where the data needed for the operation actually resides.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Addressing modes are a **mandatory Part B topic**. You must know the *format*, *effective address calculation*, and *use case* of at least 7 standard addressing modes.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of addressing modes as different ways of giving someone directions to find your house:
- **Immediate** → "Come to my house number 42" (the address is the data itself).
- **Direct** → "Go to 123 Main Street" (the address is given fully).
- **Indirect** → "Go to the post office, they have my address" (a pointer chain).
- **Register** → "Look in my pocket notebook, the address is in page 3" (data is in CPU).

## 1.2 Basic Processing Unit (BPU)

**Formal Definition:**
