## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Control Signals for a Sample Instruction (ADD R1, R2)

> **Goal:** Derive the Boolean expression for the control signal `ALU_ADD` for the instruction `ADD R1, R2` (which computes R1 ← R1 + R2) in a single-bus CPU, given opcode `I_ADD = 0011`.

**Assumed hardware:** Single internal bus, registers PC, AR, DR, AC, IR, R0–R3, ALU with output buffer Y. Signals available: `PC_out`, `PC_in`, `AR_in`, `AR_out`, `DR_out`, `DR_in`, `AC_out`, `AC_in`, `R1_out`, `R1_in`, `R2_out`, `R2_in`, `ALU_ADD`, `ALU_out`, `Y_in`.

**Step 1 — Write the RTL sequence for the execution phase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{T2:} \quad & DR \leftarrow R1 \\
\text{T3:} \quad & AC \leftarrow DR \quad \text{(transfer R1 to AC first, since same bus)} \\
\text{T4:} \quad & DR \leftarrow R2 \\
\text{T5:} \quad & AC \leftarrow AC + DR \quad \text{(ALU performs ADD)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic explanation:* The single bus can carry only one register's data at a time. To use the ALU (which needs both operands on its input lines), one operand must be moved to the ALU's Y buffer first, the second operand is then moved via the bus, the ALU adds, and the result is written back.

**Step 2 — Identify the cycle where `ALU_ADD` must be active:**
The signal `ALU_ADD` is required only in **T5**, when the addition actually occurs.

**Step 3 — Condition for T5 to occur:**
T5 is reached only if the current instruction is `ADD R1, R2`, i.e., opcode `I_ADD = 0011`.

**Step 4 — Final Boolean expression:**

$$
\text{ALU\_ADD} = I_{ADD} \cdot T_5
$$

Where $I_{ADD}$ is the decoded output of opcode `0011` (assumed 1 when the instruction is `ADD R1, R2`).

**Step 5 — Generalize for all control signals of this instruction:**

| Signal | Active Cycle | Boolean Expression |
|---|---|---|
| `R1_out` | T2 | $I_{ADD} \cdot T_2$ |
| `DR_in` | T2 | $I_{ADD} \cdot T_2$ |
| `DR_out` | T3 | $I_{ADD} \cdot T_3$ |
| `AC_in` | T3 | $I_{ADD} \cdot T_3$ |
| `R2_out` | T4 | $I_{ADD} \cdot T_4$ |
| `DR_in` | T4 | $I_{ADD} \cdot T_4$ |
| `ALU_ADD` | T5 | $I_{ADD} \cdot T_5$ |
| `AC_in` | T5 | $I_{ADD} \cdot T_5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** Each control signal is active for exactly one (instruction × timing) pair. In a real hardwired control unit, the OR of all such pairs for that signal is implemented as a wired-OR/AND-OR logic block.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Bus Organizations for `R3 ← R1 + R2`

We derive the number of micro-cycles required to execute the same operation under three bus architectures.

**Case A — Single Bus:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{T1: } & Y \leftarrow R1 \quad (\text{via bus}) \\
\text{T2: } & Z \leftarrow Y + R2 \quad (\text{R2 via bus, ALU computes}) \\
\text{T3: } & R3 \leftarrow Z \quad (\text{result via bus})
\end{aligned}
$$
**Total cycles = 3**

**Case B — Two Buses (Bus A, Bus B feeding ALU):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{T1: } & \text{ALU}_{in} \leftarrow R1, R2 \quad (\text{both buses used}) \\
\text{T1: } & R3 \leftarrow \text{ALU}_{out}
\end{aligned}
$$
**Total cycles = 1** (assuming ALU result writes directly to R3 via a dedicated path; else 2 cycles)

**Case C — Three Buses (A, B in, C out):**
$$
\text{T1: } R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2
$$
**Total cycles = 1**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Many students write "2 cycles" for a two-bus CPU, forgetting that the ALU result still needs a path back to the destination. Only **three-bus** guarantees single-cycle ALU-to-register transfers.

### 3.3 Derivation: Microprogrammed Control — Address Sequencing

For a microprogrammed control unit, the Next Address logic for a given microinstruction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Next\_Addr} & = \text{CAR} + 1 \quad \text{(default sequential)} \\
\text{IF (Branch\_cond)} & \rightarrow \text{Next\_Addr} = \text{Branch\_target field of } \mu\text{IR} \\
\text{IF (Routine call)} & \rightarrow \text{Stack push, Next\_Addr} = \text{Subroutine entry}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** If the current microinstruction is at address 0x1A and the next sequential address is 0x1B, but a conditional branch to subroutine at 0x05 is requested, the CDR's *Branch Address Field (BAF)* = `0000 0101` is loaded into the CAR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Control Address Register (CAR)** holds the address of the next microinstruction to fetch. The **Control Data Register (CDR)** holds the microinstruction currently being executed. Together, they are the microengine's "PC + IR".

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single-Bus CPU Data Path (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regBank["Register Bank (PC, AR, DR, AC, IR, R0..R3)"] -->|"common single bus"| muxA["Bus Driver / Tristate MUX"]
    muxA --> yBuf["Y Buffer (ALU input A)"]
    r2Bus["R2 (direct 2nd operand path)"] --> aluUnit["ALU Unit (ADD/SUB/AND/OR/CMP)"]
    yBuf --> aluUnit
    aluUnit --> zReg["Z Register (ALU output)"]
    zReg -->|"back to bus"| regBank
    ctrlUnit["Hardwired Control Unit"] -->|"control signals (Reg_in, Reg_out, ALU_op)"| regBank
    ctrlUnit --> aluUnit
    ctrlUnit --> ctrlUnit
    flagReg["Flag Register (Z,N,C,V)"] --> ctrlUnit
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The single horizontal arrow from the register bank to the ALU represents the single internal bus. Only one register can output on this bus at a time — that is why operations take multiple cycles.

### 4.2 Two-Bus CPU Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rbA["Register Bank A side"] -->|"Bus A"| aluA["ALU Input A"]
    rbB["Register Bank B side"] -->|"Bus B"| aluB["ALU Input B"]
    aluA --> aluCore["ALU Core"]
    aluB --> aluCore
    aluCore --> aluOut["ALU Output"]
    aluOut -->|"write-back via internal path"| rbA
    aluOut --> rbB
    ctrl["Control Unit (Hardwired/Microprogrammed)"] --> rbA
    ctrl --> rbB
    ctrl --> aluCore
```

### 4.3 Hardwired Control Unit — Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    instrReg["Instruction Register (Opcode)"] --> dec["Instruction Decoder (n-to-2^n)"]
    dec -->|"I1, I2, ... Im (one-hot)"| andGates["AND-OR Logic Matrix (per control signal)"]
    seqGen["Sequence Counter (T0, T1, ... Tk)"] --> andGates
    condFlags["Condition Flags (Z, N, C, V)"] --> andGates
    andGates -->|"c1, c2, ... cp (output control signals)"| datapath["Data Path Registers & ALU"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why one-hot?** The instruction decoder has $m$ outputs, exactly one of which is HIGH for the currently decoded instruction. This makes the AND-OR logic matrix straightforward: each output control signal is the OR of a few decoded-opcode × timing product terms.

### 4.4 Microprogrammed Control Unit — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    car["Control Address Register (CAR)"] --> cmAddr["Address lines of Control Memory (ROM)"]
    cmAddr --> cMem["Control Memory (CM) — stores microprogram"]
    cMem --> cdr["Control Data Register (CDR) — holds current microinstruction"]
    cdr -->|"Control Field"| datapath["Data Path Control Lines"]
    cdr -->|"Next Address Field + Branch Cond"| nxtAddrLogic["Next Address Generator (NAG)"]
    nxtAddrLogic --> car
    cdr -->|"CDR.cond (Z,N,C)"| nxtAddrLogic
    extAddr["External / Map ROM"] --> nxtAddrLogic
```

### 4.5 Instruction Execution State Machine (Fetch–Decode–Execute)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Fetch
    Fetch --> Decode : T1 complete
    Decode --> Execute : T2 complete
    Execute --> Fetch : instruction done
    Execute --> MemoryWait : memory access required
    MemoryWait --> Execute : MFC (Memory Function Complete)
    Execute --> InterruptCheck : end of instruction
    InterruptCheck --> Fetch : no interrupt / ISR done
    InterruptCheck --> InterruptService : interrupt pending
    InterruptService --> Fetch : ISR complete
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The state diagram above generalizes to the microprogrammed control flow. In hardwired control, these states correspond to the timing signals T0, T1, T2, ... Tn of the sequence counter.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Register Transfer Language (RTL). Write the RTL statements for the fetch phase of an instruction, assuming a single-bus CPU with memory.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** RTL is a symbolic notation used to describe the micro-operations, data transfers between registers, and the control conditions under which these transfers occur in a digital system, especially a processor.

**Fetch phase RTL (2 marks):**
- `T0: AR ← PC`
- `T0: DR ← M[AR]` (or `IR ← M[AR]` if combined)
- `T1: PC ← PC + 1`
- `T1: IR ← DR`
- `T2: Decoder ← Opcode(IR)` *(decode — may be considered part of execute phase)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Do not write `M[PC] ← IR`. The direction of data flow is FROM memory TO the register. Also, do not forget `PC ← PC + 1`; many students lose 1 mark here.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Compare hardwired control and microprogrammed control units. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Combinational gates (AND-OR) | ROM/RAM + sequencer |
| Speed | **Fast** (direct logic) | **Slower** (CM access adds delay) |
| Flexibility | Hard to modify | **Easy** — just change microcode |
| Cost for complex ISA | Very expensive (gates explode) | Economical |
| Used in | RISC (ARM, RISC-V) | CISC (legacy x86), microcode updates |

**Advantage–Disadvantage (1 mark each):**
- Hardwired — *Adv:* highest speed (no microinstruction fetch overhead). *Disadv:* rigid; design errors require hardware respin.
- Microprogrammed — *Adv:* flexible, easy to add new instructions or patch bugs (as Intel did for F00F and Pentium FDIV). *Disadv:* slower due to two-level fetch (instruction → microinstruction).

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a single-bus CPU data path to execute the instruction `SUB R1, R2` (meaning `R1 ← R1 - R2`). Show the complete sequence of micro-operations (RTL) and the control signals required in each cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks)** For the same instruction, derive the Boolean expression for the control signal `ALU_SUB` and draw the corresponding AND-OR logic circuit using a 2-to-4 instruction decoder and a sequence counter (T0–T5). Assume the opcode for `SUB R1, R2` is `0101`.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A:**

**(a) Single-Bus Data Path — RTL Sequence (7 marks):**

**Hardware assumed:** Registers: PC, AR, DR, AC, IR, R0–R3. ALU with input Y (buffer) and output Z. Single internal bus.

**Fetch phase (T0–T1):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{T0: } & AR \leftarrow PC \\
\text{T0: } & DR \leftarrow M[AR] \\
\text{T1: } & PC \leftarrow PC + 1 \\
\text{T1: } & IR \leftarrow DR
\end{aligned}
$$

**Execute phase (T2–T5):** The instruction `SUB R1, R2` means R1 ← R1 - R2. Since the single bus can carry only one operand at a time, and the ALU needs both operands, we stage them via DR (a temporary register):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{T2: } & DR \leftarrow R1 \quad (R1_{\text{out}}, DR_{\text{in}}) \\
\text{T3: } & AC \leftarrow DR \quad (DR_{\text{out}}, AC_{\text{in}}) \quad \text{— stage R1 into AC} \\
\text{T4: } & DR \leftarrow R2 \quad (R2_{\text{out}}, DR_{\text{in}}) \quad \text{— load R2 into DR} \\
\text{T5: } & AC \leftarrow AC - DR \quad (DR_{\text{out}}, ALU_{\text{SUB}}, AC_{\text{in}}) \quad \text{— perform subtraction}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary of control signals (1 mark):**
| Cycle | Active Signals |
|---|---|
| T2 | `R1_out`, `DR_in` |
| T3 | `DR_out`, `AC_in` |
| T4 | `R2_out`, `DR_in` |
| T5 | `DR_out`, `ALU_SUB`, `AC_in` |

**(b) Boolean Derivation and AND-OR Logic (7 marks):**

**Boolean expression for `ALU_SUB` (2 marks):**
The ALU performs SUB only in cycle T5, and only for the SUB instruction (opcode `0101`, which we call $I_{SUB}$). Therefore:
$$
\text{ALU\_SUB} = I_{SUB} \cdot T_5
$$

**Logic circuit description (5 marks):**
- **Step 1:** The opcode `0101` feeds a 3-to-8 instruction decoder. The output line corresponding to `0101` is the one-hot signal $I_{SUB}$.
- **Step 2:** The sequence counter outputs timing signals $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_5$. We need the $T_5$ line.
- **Step 3:** An **AND gate** combines $I_{SUB}$ and $T_5$ to produce `ALU_SUB`.
- **Step 4:** In a real design with multiple control signals, each control signal is the OR of multiple such AND terms. For `ALU_SUB`, only one AND term is needed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    irBits["IR bits [2:0] = 0101"] --> dec3to8["3-to-8 Decoder"]
    dec3to8 -->|"I_SUB line (output 5)"| andG["AND gate (2-input)"]
    seqCnt["Sequence Counter"] -->|"T5 output"| andG
    andG --> aluSub["Control signal: ALU_SUB"]
    andG2["AND gate for AC_in"] --> acIn["Control signal: AC_in"]
    dec3to8 --> andG2
    seqCnt -->|"T5"| andG2
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is using `I_SUB` (the raw 3-bit opcode) directly in the AND gate instead of the **decoded one-hot** output. The opcode is in binary; the decoder converts it to one-hot form. Marking will specifically check for this distinction (1–2 marks lost otherwise).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **microprogrammed control unit** with a neat block diagram. Compare horizontal and vertical microinstruction formats. The control memory has 8 words; how many address bits are needed? If the control memory is 32 words × 24 bits, what is its capacity in bytes?

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a microprogrammed control unit, the current microinstruction at address `0x12` has a **branch-on-condition** bit set, and the branch target address field of the CDR is `0x05`. If the condition flag Z = 1, what will be the next address fetched? Show the complete step-by-step working.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B:**

**(a) Microprogrammed Control Unit — Block Diagram & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks)** — see Section 4.4 above. The key components are:
- **Control Address Register (CAR):** holds the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched.
- **Control Memory (CM):** a ROM storing the microprogram (one word per microinstruction).
- **Control Data Register (CDR):** holds the currently fetched microinstruction, partitioned into *Control Field* (drives data-path control signals) and *Branch/Next-Address Field*.
- **Next Address Generator (NAG):** combinational logic that decides the next address based on CDR's branch field, condition flags, and external CAR inputs.

**Working (2 marks):** When a new machine instruction is fetched into IR, its opcode is mapped (via a Map ROM) to the starting address of its microprogram in CM. The microsequencer then steps through the microinstructions, branching to subroutines for shared micro-operations (e.g., a common fetch microsequence).

**Comparison (1 mark):**
| Feature | Horizontal | Vertical |
|---|---|---|
| Width | Wide (1 bit per control signal) | Narrow (coded fields) |
| Speed | **Fast** (no decoding) | Slower (field decoders) |
| Concurrency | **High** (many signals at once) | Limited by field size |
| ROM size | Large | Compact |

**Numerical answers (1 mark):**
- 8 words → $\log_2 8 = 3$ address bits.
- Capacity = $32 \times 24$ bits = $32 \times 3$ bytes = **96 bytes**.

**(b) Branching Logic Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Current microinstruction address: `0x12` (decimal 18, binary `0001 0010`).
- CDR's branch-target field: `0x05` (decimal 5, binary `0000 0101`).
- Branch condition bit in CDR: **SET** (i.e., branch is requested).
- Condition flag: `Z = 1` (branch condition met).

**Step 1 — Identify default next address:**
Sequential execution would point to `0x12 + 1 = 0x13`.

**Step 2 — Check branch condition:**
- Branch bit in CDR = 1 → branch IS requested.
- Condition flag Z = 1 → condition IS satisfied.

**Step 3 — Decision by Next Address Generator (NAG):**
Since both branch-requested AND condition-met are TRUE, the NAG ignores the default `+1` and loads the branch-target field into the CAR.

**Step 4 — Compute new CAR value:**

$$
\text{CAR}_{\text{new}} = \text{CDR}_{\text{branch field}} = 0\text{x}05
$$

**Step 5 — Microsequencer output:**
The next microinstruction fetched from Control Memory is the one at address `0x05`.

**Final Answer (1 mark):** The next address fetched is **`0x05`**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students confuse "branch bit set" with "branch always taken". The branch happens only if the **condition flag** is also TRUE. Both conditions must hold. Lose 2 marks if you skip the condition flag check. Also, do not write `0x13` (the default sequential address) as the answer when a branch is clearly intended.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **RTL = Register Transfer Language.** Universal notation. Master conditional and timed forms: `T_i : P(i) : R2 ← R1`.
- ✅ **Fetch phase micro-operations are universal:** `AR ← PC`, `DR ← M[AR]`, `PC ← PC + 1`, `IR ← DR`, then decode.
- ✅ **Single-bus CPU** is the *most-asked* KTU topic. The bus can carry only ONE source at a time → multi-cycle execution.
- ✅ **Hardwired control expression:** `Control_Signal = Σ (Decoded_Opcode × T_j × Condition)` — write it as an OR of AND terms.
- ✅ **Microprogrammed control** uses a Control Memory (ROM) and a microsequencer. The CAR is the "micro-PC".
- ✅ **Horizontal microcode = 1 bit per signal** (fast, wide). **Vertical microcode = coded fields** (compact, slower).
- ✅ **Bus count vs. speed:** 1-bus (slow, cheap) → 2-bus → 3-bus (fast, expensive). Trade-off matters in design.
- ✅ **Branch logic in microprogrammed CU:** Branch happens when (branch bit in CDR) AND (condition flag) are BOTH true.
- ✅ **Control Memory sizing:** words = $2^{(\text{address bits})}$. Capacity (bits) = words × word_width.
- ✅ **Hardwired CU = RISC style; Microprogrammed CU = CISC style.** Modern CPUs use a **hybrid** approach.
- ✅ **Examiner loves:** "Write RTL for instruction X", "Draw the AND-OR logic for control signal Y", "How many cycles for Z on a 3-bus CPU?" — practice these three question types.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processor Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT304 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Processor Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Processor Organization** refers to the internal architectural arrangement and operational structure of the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** of a digital computer. It encompasses the design, interconnection, and functional behavior of the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, the **Register File**, the **Control Unit (CU)**, the **Internal CPU Buses**, and the **Data Paths** that collectively enable the **Fetch–Decode–Execute (FDE)** cycle required to process machine-level instructions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Processor Organization is studied as a sub-component of the broader *Computer Organization* hierarchy, with specific emphasis on:
- **Register Set Architecture (GPRs vs. SPRs)**
- **Instruction Formats and Encoding**
- **Addressing Modes**
- **Stack and Stack Frames**
- **Hardwired vs. Microprogrammed Control**
- **RISC vs. CISC Design Philosophies**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCIT304 Module 2), students are expected to demonstrate competence in mapping high-level procedural operations onto the underlying register-transfer operations of the processor. This includes tracing the full lifecycle of an instruction — from its fetch in primary memory to its execution in the ALU.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Processor as a Factory Floor"

Imagine a **highly automated factory** that receives raw materials and a list of instructions (a "job order"). The factory is the CPU. The components of the factory map beautifully to processor elements:

| Factory Element | Processor Component | Function |
|-----------------|---------------------|----------|
| Factory Manager | **Control Unit (CU)** | Reads instructions, issues control signals |
| Workbenches | **Registers** | Hold intermediate data being processed |
| Main Assembly Robot | **ALU** | Performs arithmetic & logical operations |
| Storage Yard (raw materials) | **Primary Memory (RAM)** | Where instructions and data are stored |
| Conveyor Belt | **System Buses (Data, Address, Control)** | Transports data & instructions between units |
| Job Order Sheet | **Instruction Register (IR)** | Holds the currently executing instruction |
| Order Number on Wall | **Program Counter (PC)** | Tracks which job is next |
| Time Clock / Conveyor Switch | **Clock Signal & Control Lines** | Synchronizes all operations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The CPU is **fundamentally sequential** in its internal operation. Even in modern multi-core, pipelined processors, the **basic machine cycle** of *Fetch → Decode → Execute* remains the conceptual heartbeat. All advanced optimizations (pipelining, superscalar, out-of-order execution) are clever ways to *overlap* these basic steps, not to replace them.

## 1.3 The CPU as a Black-Box (Block Diagram Overview)

At the highest level of abstraction, the CPU is bounded by three primary interface buses:

$$
\text{CPU} \leftrightarrow \text{System Bus} \leftrightarrow \text{Memory \& I/O}
$$

The System Bus consists of three sub-buses:
- **Data Bus (DB):** Bidirectional, carries the actual data being transferred. Width is **8, 16, 32, or 64 bits** depending on processor.
- **Address Bus (AB):** Unidirectional (CPU → Memory/I/O), carries the memory address. Width determines the **maximum addressable memory** as $2^n$ locations for an $n$-bit bus.
- **Control Bus (CB):** Bidirectional, carries timing and command signals (Read/Write, Interrupt Acknowledge, Bus Request, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Metric:** For a processor with a 16-bit address bus, the directly addressable memory is $2^{16} = \textbf{65,536 locations} = \textbf{64 KB}$. This is a frequently tested relationship in KTU examinations.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Components of the CPU

The CPU is architecturally divided into two major sub-units:

### 2.1.1 The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
The computational core of the processor. It accepts two operands, performs a specified operation, and outputs a result along with status flags. Modern ALUs support:

- **Arithmetic Operations:** ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, INC, DEC, NEG, CMP
- **Logical Operations:** AND, OR, XOR, NOT, NAND, NOR
- **Shift/Rotate Operations:** SHL, SHR, SAL, SAR, ROL, ROR
- **Data Transfer (internal):** MOV (between registers)

### 2.1.2 The Control Unit (CU)
The "brain" that orchestrates all CPU activity. It:
- Fetches instructions from memory (using PC and MAR)
- Decodes the operation code (opcode)
- Generates the **Control Word** — a binary pattern that activates the correct ALU function and routes data through the correct paths
- Manages branch conditions, interrupts, and bus arbitration

### 2.1.3 The Register File
A high-speed, small-capacity storage array inside the CPU. Access time is measured in **nanoseconds** (typically < 1 ns) compared to RAM's **50–100 ns**. The register file is partitioned into:

#### A. User-Visible (General Purpose) Registers
| Register | Typical Width | Primary Use |
|----------|---------------|-------------|
| **AX / EAX / RAX** (Accumulator) | 16/32/64-bit | Default operand & result of ALU ops |
| **BX / EBX / RBX** (Base) | 16/32/64-bit | Holds base address for memory access |
| **CX / ECX / RCX** (Counter) | 16/32/64-bit | Loop counter, shift/rotate count |
| **DX / EDX / RDX** (Data) | 16/32/64-bit | I/O port addressing, MUL/DIV partner |
| **SI / DI** (Source/Dest Index) | 16-bit | String operations, indexed addressing |
| **SP** (Stack Pointer) | 16/32/64-bit | Top-of-Stack address |
| **BP** (Base Pointer) | 16/32/64-bit | Stack frame base address |

#### B. Special Purpose Registers (SPRs) — User-Invisible
| Register | Full Form | Function |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| **PC** | Program Counter | Holds address of the *next* instruction to fetch |
| **IR** | Instruction Register | Holds the *currently executing* instruction |
| **MAR** | Memory Address Register | Holds the memory address for read/write |
| **MBR / MDR** | Memory Data / Buffer Register | Temporarily holds data being read/written to memory |
| **PSW / SR** | Program Status Word / Status Register | Contains condition flags (Z, C, N, V, etc.) |
| **AC** | Accumulator (in some ISAs) | Implicit operand of many ALU instructions |

## 2.2 The Flags / Condition Code Register

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** stores the result of the most recent ALU operation. The standard flag bits (in a typical 16-bit x86-style PSW) are:

| Flag Bit | Symbol | Condition Set When... |
|----------|--------|------------------------|
| **CF** | Carry Flag | An unsigned arithmetic operation produces a carry-out or borrow |
| **ZF** | Zero Flag | The result of the operation is zero |
| **SF** | Sign Flag | The MSB of the result is 1 (negative in 2's complement) |
| **OF** | Overflow Flag | A signed arithmetic operation overflows |
| **PF** | Parity Flag | The result has an even number of 1-bits in its LSB byte |
| **AF** | Auxiliary Flag | BCD-carry between bit 3 and bit 4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the ALU performs subtraction (A − B), the flags are set as if the operation were A + (~B) + 1 (i.e., 2's complement addition). This means the **Carry Flag becomes the Borrow Flag** (CF = 1 if a borrow is required). Examiners frequently test this relationship.

## 2.3 The Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle (The Heartbeat)

Every CPU executes a continuous loop of three sub-cycles:

### Step 1: Fetch Phase
1. $\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC}$ (Copy the address of next instruction to MAR)
2. $\text{Read} \rightarrow \text{MBR}$ (Memory places the instruction onto data bus, MBR captures it)
3. $\text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MBR}$ (Transfer instruction into Instruction Register)
4. $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + n$ (Increment PC by instruction length $n$ in bytes)

### Step 2: Decode Phase
1. **Opcode Decoder** examines the bits of `IR[opcode-field]`
2. The CU's decoding logic produces a unique **Control Word**
3. Operands (if memory-based) are fetched: $\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{Effective Address}$, $\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{Memory[MAR]}$
4. Operands are loaded into internal CPU registers (e.g., **A and B registers** feeding the ALU)

### Step 3: Execute Phase
1. ALU performs the operation
2. Result is written to the destination register or memory location
3. **Flags/PSW** are updated based on the result
4. Cycle repeats from Step 1

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Interfacing Note:** The single-bus internal architecture (using a single internal bus between registers and ALU) is the most commonly tested configuration in KTU, and is covered in detail with diagram in Section 4.

## 2.4 Bus Organization — The Three-State Snag

When multiple registers must share a single common bus, **tri-state buffers** (or multiplexers) are used to prevent electrical contention. Only **one** register is allowed to "drive" the bus at any given instant; all others must be in a high-impedance (Hi-Z) state.

The selection logic is governed by the control unit's **select signals** $S_0, S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n$ (where $n$ is the number of registers). For a 1-out-of-$n$ decoder, $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ select lines are needed.

## 2.5 The Stack Organization

A **stack** is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure implemented in main memory and managed by the CPU via a dedicated **Stack Pointer (SP)** register.

### 2.5.1 Stack Operations
- **PUSH:** $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 1$, then $\text{Memory[SP]} \leftarrow \text{data}$ (Stack grows *downward* in most ISAs)
- **POP:** $\text{data} \leftarrow \text{Memory[SP]}$, then $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1$
- **CALL (Subroutine invocation):** PUSH the return address (current PC) onto the stack, then load PC with the subroutine's address
- **RET (Return from subroutine):** POP the return address from the stack back into PC

### 2.5.2 Stack Frames
Modern languages use **stack frames** (activation records) to manage nested subroutine calls. Each frame contains:
- **Return address** of the calling function
- **Saved frame pointer** (old BP)
- **Local variables**
- **Function arguments** (in some calling conventions)

The **Frame Pointer (BP/EBP)** provides a stable reference to the base of the current frame, allowing local variables to be accessed via fixed offsets regardless of how the stack pointer moves.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Addressable Memory | $M_{max} = 2^n$ | $n$ = address bus width in bits; result in *bytes* (or words, if word-addressed) |
| Memory Size (KB) | $\text{Size} = 2^n \div 1024$ KB | $n$ = address bus width |
| Memory Size (MB) | $\text{Size} = 2^{n-20}$ MB | Applies for $n \geq 20$ |
| Effective Address (Direct) | $\text{EA} = \text{Address field of instruction}$ | Simplest mode |
| Effective Address (Indirect) | $\text{EA} = \text{Contents of address-field register}$ | One extra memory access |
| Effective Address (Indexed) | $\text{EA} = \text{Base} + \text{Index} \times d$ | $d$ = scale factor (1, 2, 4, 8) |
| Effective Address (Base Reg) | $\text{EA} = \text{Base Register} + \text{Displacement}$ | Used for arrays/structures |
| PC Update | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + k$ | $k$ = instruction length in bytes (not bits) |
| Stack Pointer (PUSH) | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - w$ | $w$ = word size in bytes (descending stack) |
| Stack Pointer (POP) | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + w$ | After data is read |
| Branch Target | $\text{New PC} = \text{PC} + \text{offset}$ | For PC-relative branches |
| Throughput (Pipeline) | $\text{TP} = f \times N$ | $f$ = clock, $N$ = stages (idealized) |
| Control Memory Size | $\text{ROM size} = 2^k \times m$ | $k$ = opcode bits decoded, $m$ = control word width |
| Average Memory Access Time | $T_{avg} = h \cdot T_c + (1-h)(T_c + T_m)$ | $h$ = hit ratio, $T_c$ = cache, $T_m$ = main memory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Trap:** For word-addressed machines, the addressable *word* count is $2^n$ but the byte-addressable memory might be $2^{n+2}$ (for 32-bit words). Always read the problem statement carefully.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Processor organization principles directly inform:
- **Compiler Design:** Compilers must map high-level constructs onto the limited register set of the target ISA. **Register allocation** algorithms (graph coloring, linear scan) are direct applications of register organization theory.
- **Operating Systems:** Context switches save/restore register state. The PSW is critical for preemptive multitasking.
- **Embedded Systems:** Microcontroller selection (8051 vs ARM Cortex-M) depends on register organization, addressing mode richness, and stack behavior.
- **Performance Engineering:** Profiling tools report *cache misses* and *branch mispredictions* — both directly traceable to memory organization and PC-update logic.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Addressable Memory vs. Address Bus Width

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Type):** A processor has an $n$-bit address bus and an 8-bit data bus. Derive the maximum addressable memory, both in *bytes* and in *KB*.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The address bus carries a binary pattern of width $n$ bits. Each unique pattern corresponds to **one** unique memory location. The number of unique binary patterns of length $n$ bits is:

$$
N_{locations} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^{i} = 2^{n}
$$

If the data bus is $w$ bits wide, each location stores $w$ bits = $w/8$ bytes. For an 8-bit data bus ($w = 8$), each location stores exactly **1 byte**. Therefore:

$$
M_{max} = 2^{n} \text{ bytes}
$$

To convert to Kilobytes (where 1 KB = $2^{10}$ bytes = 1024 bytes):

$$
M_{max} = \frac{2^{n}}{2^{10}} \text{ KB} = 2^{n-10} \text{ KB}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU Past Year Pattern)
Given: $n = 20$ address lines, 8-bit data bus.

$$
M_{max} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
M_{max} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{1024} = 1024 \text{ KB} = 1 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check:** $2^{n-10} = 2^{20-10} = 2^{10} = 1024$ KB. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Address for Indexed Addressing Mode

**Problem:** Derive the Effective Address (EA) calculation for the *indexed* addressing mode, given a base address $B$, an index register contents $X$, and a scale factor $s$.

### Derivation

In indexed addressing, the instruction contains a constant **offset/address** and refers to an **index register**. The hardware computes:

$$
\text{EA} = B + (X \times s)
$$

Where:
- $B$ = base address (from instruction or base register)
- $X$ = signed integer contents of the index register
- $s$ = scale factor (typically 1, 2, 4, or 8 — chosen to match element size in bytes)

The CPU's address generation unit (AGU) performs the multiplication $X \times s$ in hardware (often using a barrel shifter for $s \in \{1, 2, 4, 8\}$, since these are simple shift operations). Then it adds the scaled index to the base:

$$
\text{AGU Output} = B + \overbrace{(X \ll \log_2 s)}^{X \times s}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example
- Base register (in 8086-style): $B = 2000\text{H}$
- Index register (SI): $X = 0005\text{H} = 5_{10}$
- Scale factor: $s = 2$ (we are accessing 16-bit words)

$$
\text{EA} = 2000\text{H} + (0005\text{H} \times 2) = 2000\text{H} + 000\text{A}\text{H} = 200\text{A}\text{H}
$$

The physical memory address is $\text{200AH}$. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Single-Bus CPU Register-Transfer for ADD Operation

**Problem:** A single-bus CPU has registers PC, AR (Address Register), DR (Data Register), AC (Accumulator), IR, and a single shared internal bus. Derive the **micro-operations** required to execute the instruction `ADD addr`.

### Step-by-Step

**Instruction Format (assumed):** `ADD addr` is a 3-byte instruction:
- **Byte 1:** Opcode `ADD`
- **Bytes 2–3:** 16-bit memory address of the operand

**Step 1: Fetch Opcode**
- $T_0$: $\text{AR} \leftarrow \text{PC}$ (Place PC's address on address bus; read signal asserted; data flows into DR)
- $T_1$: $\text{IR} \leftarrow \text{DR}$, $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$ (Instruction loaded; PC incremented)

**Step 2: Fetch Address Bytes**
- $T_2$: $\text{AR} \leftarrow \text{PC}$ (Get low byte of address)
- $T_3$: $\text{DR} \leftarrow \text{Memory[AR]}$, $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$
- $T_4$: $\text{AR} \leftarrow \text{DR}$ (Move low byte to Address Register)
- $T_5$: $\text{AR} \leftarrow \text{PC}$ (Get high byte of address)
- $T_6$: $\text{DR} \leftarrow \text{Memory[AR]}$, $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$
- $T_7$: $\text{AR} \leftarrow \text{AR} + \text{DR} \times 2^8$ (Combine into 16-bit address; or shift & add)

**Step 3: Fetch Operand**
- $T_8$: $\text{DR} \leftarrow \text{Memory[AR]}$ (Read operand from effective address)

**Step 4: Execute (ALU)**
- $T_9$: $\text{AC} \leftarrow \text{AC} + \text{DR}$ (Perform addition; flags updated by ALU)

**Total:** 10 clock cycles for a single-bus, single-memory-port design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimization Note:** A **three-bus internal architecture** can reduce this to ~4–5 cycles because two source operands can be routed to the ALU simultaneously. KTU frequently asks students to **compare** single-bus, two-bus, and three-bus designs in terms of cycle count and hardware complexity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Addressing Modes

| Addressing Mode | Effective Address Formula | Memory Accesses for Operand | Use Case | KTU Frequency |
|----------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|----------|---------------|
| **Immediate** | Operand is in instruction | 0 | Loading constants | ★★★★★ |
| **Direct (Absolute)** | EA = Address field | 1 | Static global variables | ★★★★ |
| **Indirect (Register)** | EA = [Register] | 1 (extra, for EA) | Pointers, dereferencing | ★★★★★ |
| **Register** | Operand is in register | 0 | Fast ALU operations | ★★★★★ |
| **Register Indirect** | EA = [Register] | 1 | Pointer-based loops | ★★★★ |
| **Displacement (Base+Offset)** | EA = [Base Reg] + Offset | 1 | Local variables, struct fields | ★★★★★ |
| **Indexed** | EA = [Index Reg] + Base | 1 | Array traversal | ★★★★ |
| **Scaled Indexed** | EA = [Index] × s + Base | 1 | Typed array access | ★★★ |
| **Relative (PC-relative)** | EA = PC + Offset | 1 | Position-independent code, branches | ★★★★★ |
| **Auto-increment** | EA = [R]; R = R + d | 1 | Sequential scan (forward) | ★★★ |
| **Auto-decrement** | R = R - d; EA = [R] | 1 | Sequential scan (reverse), stack PUSH | ★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The number of **memory accesses required to fetch the operand** (excluding the instruction fetch itself) directly determines the cycle count. **Register and Immediate modes are the fastest (0 extra accesses)** and form the foundation of the **RISC load-store architecture**.

## 3.5 Derivation: Stack-Based Arithmetic Evaluation (Reverse Polish Notation)

**Problem:** Evaluate the infix expression `A + B × C - D / E` using a stack-based processor, showing the stack state at each step.

### Step-by-Step Stack Trace

The compiler first converts infix to **Postfix (RPN):** `A B C × + D E / -`

Stack grows downward; assume an empty stack initially. Each token triggers an operation:

| Step | Token | Action | Stack (top → bottom, with rightmost = top) |
|------|-------|--------|--------------------------------------------|
| 1 | `A` | PUSH A | [A] |
| 2 | `B` | PUSH B | [A, B] |
| 3 | `C` | PUSH C | [A, B, C] |
| 4 | `×` | POP B, POP C; PUSH (B×C) | [A, (B×C)] |
| 5 | `+` | POP A, POP (B×C); PUSH (A + B×C) | [(A + B×C)] |
| 6 | `D` | PUSH D | [(A + B×C), D] |
| 7 | `E` | PUSH E | [(A + B×C), D, E] |
| 8 | `/` | POP D, POP E; PUSH (D/E) | [(A + B×C), (D/E)] |
| 9 | `-` | POP (A+B×C), POP (D/E); PUSH ((A+B×C) − (D/E)) | [Result] |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why stacks matter for processors:** Stack-based ISAs (e.g., Java Virtual Machine) eliminate the need for explicit registers, simplifying the hardware. **Register-based ISAs** (x86, ARM) are faster but require complex register allocation in software.

## 3.6 Quantitative Comparison: RISC vs. CISC

| Parameter | RISC (Reduced Instruction Set) | CISC (Complex Instruction Set) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Instruction count | Small, fixed (typically < 128) | Large, variable (often 200+) |
| Instruction length | Fixed (e.g., 32 bits) | Variable (e.g., 1–15 bytes for x86) |
| Addressing modes | Few (3–5) | Many (often 12+) |
| Memory access | Load/Store only (ALU ops on registers) | Any instruction can access memory |
| Clock cycles per instruction | 1 (ideally) | 1–20+ |
| Control unit | Hardwired | Often microprogrammed |
| Pipelining | Highly amenable | Difficult, complex |
| Examples | ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, SPARC | x86, x86-64, IBM Z, VAX |
| Compiler complexity | Higher (must manage registers) | Lower (instructions do more) |

$$
\text{Simplified CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)}: \text{CPI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{CPI}_i \times I_i)}{I_{total}}
$$

Where $I_i$ is the count of instructions of class $i$ and $\text{CPI}_i$ is its cycle count.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level CPU Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    CPU[Central Processing Unit]
    CU[Control Unit]
    ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    RF[Register File]
    IBUS[Internal CPU Bus]
    PCREG[Program Counter PC]
    IRREG[Instruction Register IR]
    MARREG[Memory Address Register MAR]
    MBRREG[Memory Buffer Register MBR]
    PSWREG[Program Status Word PSW]

    CPU --> CU
    CPU --> ALU
    CPU --> RF
    CU --> IBUS
    ALU --> IBUS
    RF --> IBUS
    RF --> PCREG
    RF --> IRREG
    RF --> MARREG
    RF --> MBRREG
    RF --> PSWREG
```

## 4.2 Single-Bus CPU Organization (Detailed)

```mermaid
graph LR
    PC[PC - Program Counter]
    AR[AR - Address Register]
    DR[DR - Data Register]
    AC[AC - Accumulator]
    IR[IR - Instruction Register]
    TR[TR - Temporary Register]
    ALU[ALU - Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    SHARED[Single Internal Bus]
    EXT[External System Bus]
    MUX1[Bus MUX / Select Logic]
    FLG[Flags Register]

    PC --> SHARED
    AR --> SHARED
    DR --> SHARED
    AC --> SHARED
    IR --> SHARED
    TR --> SHARED
    SHARED --> ALU
    ALU --> AC
    ALU --> FLG
    SHARED --> EXT
    AR --> EXT
    SHARED --> MUX1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this diagram:** The arrows indicate *one possible* data path at a given clock cycle. Because the bus is shared, only one register can drive it at a time. The **tri-state buffer select logic** (MUX1) ensures this discipline.

## 4.3 Three-Bus CPU Organization (Comparison)

```mermaid
graph TB
    RFA[Register File A]
    RFB[Register File B]
    RFC[Register File C - Destination]
    ALU2[ALU]
    BUS_A[Bus A - Source 1]
    BUS_B[Bus B - Source 2]
    BUS_C[Bus C - Result]
    SHIFT[Barrel Shifter]
    FLG2[Flags]

    RFA --> BUS_A
    RFB --> BUS_B
    BUS_A --> ALU2
    BUS_B --> ALU2
    BUS_A --> SHIFT
    BUS_B --> SHIFT
    SHIFT --> ALU2
    ALU2 --> BUS_C
    BUS_C --> RFC
    ALU2 --> FLG2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three buses are faster:** Two source operands can be fetched **simultaneously** from the register file and presented to the ALU inputs in a single cycle. The result is written back via the third bus, also in the same cycle. This enables the classic **R-type instruction execution in 1 clock cycle**:
>
> $$\text{Reg[C]} \leftarrow \text{Reg[A]} \text{ op } \text{Reg[B]}$$

## 4.4 Fetch–Decode–Execute State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset
    Reset --> Fetch1 : Power On
    Fetch1 --> Fetch2 : AR gets PC
    Fetch2 --> Decode1 : IR gets MBR
    Decode1 --> Decode2 : Opcode extracted
    Decode2 --> OperandFetch : Memory operand required
    Decode2 --> Execute : Register operands ready
    OperandFetch --> Execute : Operand loaded
    Execute --> StoreResult : Result available
    StoreResult --> Writeback : Result to destination
    Writeback --> Fetch1 : PC incremented
    StoreResult --> Fetch1 : No writeback needed
    Writeback --> Interrupt : Interrupt pending
    Interrupt --> InterruptService
    InterruptService --> Fetch1 : ISR complete
```

## 4.5 Stack Frame Layout (x86-style)

```mermaid
graph TB
    HI[Higher Memory Addresses]
    ARG_N[Argument N of current function]
    ARG_1[Argument 1 of current function]
    RET_ADDR[Return Address - pushed by CALL]
    SAVED_BP[Saved Frame Pointer - old BP]
    LOCALS[Local Variables of current function]
    SAVED_REGS[Saved Callee-Save Registers]
    LO[Lower Memory Addresses - SP points here after PUSH]
    HI --> ARG_N
    ARG_N --> ARG_1
    ARG_1 --> RET_ADDR
    RET_ADDR --> SAVED_BP
    SAVED_BP --> LOCALS
    LOCALS --> SAVED_REGS
    SAVED_REGS --> LO
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the stack diagram:** Memory grows *upward* in this visualization, but the stack grows *downward* (SP decreases on PUSH). The frame pointer BP points to **SAVED_BP**, and local variables are accessed via negative offsets from BP (e.g., `[BP − 4]` for a 4-byte local).

## 4.6 Hardwired vs. Microprogrammed Control Unit

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HARDWIRED[Hardwired Control Unit]
        H_IR[Instruction Register]
        H_CLK[Clock]
        H_DEC[Decoders]
        H_LOGIC[Combinational Logic]
        H_OUT[Control Signals]
        H_IR --> H_DEC
        H_CLK --> H_DEC
        H_DEC --> H_LOGIC
        H_LOGIC --> H_OUT
    end

    subgraph MICRO[MICROPROGRAMMED Control Unit]
        M_IR[Instruction Register]
        M_CAR[Control Address Register CAR]
        M_ROM[Control Memory ROM - microcode]
        M_CDR[Control Data Register CDR]
        M_COND[Condition Flags]
        M_OUT[Control Signals]
        M_IR --> M_CAR
        M_CAR --> M_ROM
        M_ROM --> M_CDR
        M_CDR --> M_OUT
        M_COND --> M_CAR
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Summary:** Hardwired control is **faster** (no ROM access delay) but **hard to modify** — changing the instruction set requires redesigning combinational logic. Microprogrammed control is **slower** (1–2 extra cycles for microcode fetch) but **flexible** — modifying the instruction set only requires writing new microcode. Modern x86 CPUs use a **hybrid** approach: hardwired for simple instructions, microcode for complex ones.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Program Counter (PC) and explain its role during the fetch phase. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Program Counter (PC)** is a special-purpose CPU register that holds the **memory address of the next instruction to be fetched and executed**. It is automatically incremented after each instruction fetch so that the processor naturally sequences through instructions stored in consecutive memory locations (assuming no branch).

**Role during the Fetch Phase:**

During the fetch phase of the instruction cycle, the following sequence occurs:

1. The contents of the PC are transferred to the **Memory Address Register (MAR)**.
2. A **memory read** operation is initiated at that address.
3. The fetched instruction is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)** via the **Memory Buffer Register (MBR)**.
4. The PC is then **incremented** by the length of the fetched instruction (e.g., by 2 or 3 bytes), so that it now points to the *next* instruction.

In the case of a **branch or jump** instruction, the PC is *overwritten* with the target address during the execute phase, rather than being incremented.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse PC with IR. Remember: **PC holds the address of the *next* instruction; IR holds the instruction that is *currently being decoded/executed***. This distinction is a favorite KTU trick question.

### Question 2: List any three addressing modes with an example of each. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Immediate Addressing:** The operand value is embedded directly within the instruction itself.
   - Example: `MOV AX, 25H` — Loads the value `25H` directly into register AX. **No memory access is needed to fetch the operand** beyond the instruction fetch.

2. **Direct (Absolute) Addressing:** The instruction contains the full memory address of the operand.
   - Example: `MOV AX, [2000H]` — Loads the contents of memory location `2000H` into AX. **One memory access** is required to fetch the operand.

3. **Register Indirect Addressing:** The instruction specifies a register whose *contents* are the memory address of the operand.
   - Example: `MOV AX, [BX]` — If BX = `3000H`, then this loads the contents of memory location `3000H` into AX. **One memory access** is required. This is the basis of *pointer* dereferencing in high-level languages.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** In Register Indirect mode, do **not** say "the register is the operand." The **register holds the address** of the operand, and the operand resides in memory. This subtle distinction is a common source of lost marks.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14-Mark Question **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a single-bus CPU organization and explain the fetch–decode–execute cycle with reference to the registers. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the various addressing modes used in computer processors with suitable examples. Compare immediate, direct, and indirect addressing modes in terms of execution speed and memory accesses. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution to Question A (a)

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 for the Mermaid block diagram. For written exam, students should redraw it neatly on paper.)*

The single-bus CPU consists of the following major components connected to a common internal data bus:
- **General Purpose Registers:** PC, AC (Accumulator), DR (Data Register), AR (Address Register), IR (Instruction Register), TR (Temporary Register)
- **ALU** with two inputs and one output feeding the bus
- **Flags Register** capturing condition codes
- **Control Unit** providing timing and select signals

**Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle (with register transfer notation):**

| Phase | Micro-Operations | Explanation |
|-------|------------------|-------------|
| **Fetch Opcode** | `AR ← PC` | Address of next instruction is loaded into AR |
| | `DR ← Memory[AR]`, `PC ← PC + 1` | Instruction is read from memory; PC is incremented |
| | `IR ← DR` | Instruction is moved to IR for decoding |
| **Decode** | `D ← IR[opcode]` | Decoder examines the opcode field and identifies the operation |
| **Operand Fetch** | `AR ← IR[address]` | Address field of instruction is loaded into AR (for memory operands) |
| | `DR ← Memory[AR]` | Operand is fetched from memory into DR |
| **Execute** | `AC ← AC + DR` (for ADD) | ALU performs the operation; result stored in AC; flags updated |
| **Store Result** | *(for memory destination: `Memory[AR] ← AC`)* | If the instruction requires writing back to memory |

**Cycle counting:** A single-bus design with single memory port requires **7–10 clock cycles** per instruction, which is its main drawback compared to multi-bus designs.

### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Immediate | Direct | Indirect |
|-----------|-----------|--------|----------|
| **Operand Location** | Embedded in instruction | Memory address in instruction | Register holds memory address |
| **Memory Accesses for Operand** | **0** | **1** | **2** (one to fetch register's pointed value, if register itself is fetched; or 1 if addressing is by register contents) |
| **Execution Speed** | Fastest | Moderate | Slowest (of the three) |
| **Address Space** | Limited by instruction size | Limited by address field size | Full memory space (2ⁿ) |
| **Flexibility** | Low (constants only) | Medium (fixed locations) | High (dynamic pointers) |
| **Example** | `MOV AX, 25H` | `MOV AX, [2000H]` | `MOV AX, [BX]` where `BX = 2000H` |

**Speed Rank:** Immediate > Direct > Indirect (in terms of pure operand-fetch cycles).

**Trade-off:** Immediate and direct modes are *static* — the operand location is fixed at compile time. Indirect mode is *dynamic* — the address can be computed at runtime, enabling **pointers**, **dynamic data structures**, and **polymorphism**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Examiners often deduct marks if students do **not** explicitly state the **number of memory accesses** for each mode. Always include this in your answer — it's a 2-mark line item.

---

### Question B: 14-Mark Question (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** What is a stack? Explain the PUSH and POP operations with diagrams. How is the stack used for subroutine calls and returns? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare hardwired and microprogrammed control units. Explain the working of a microprogrammed control unit with a neat block diagram. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Definition of Stack:**

A **stack** is a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** data structure implemented in the main memory of a computer, with its top-of-stack location tracked by a dedicated register called the **Stack Pointer (SP)**. The stack is a critical CPU resource used for subroutine management, expression evaluation, and local variable storage.

**PUSH Operation:**

The PUSH operation stores a word at the top of the stack. For a **descending stack** (which grows toward lower memory addresses), the sequence is:

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - w
$$

$$
\text{Memory[SP]} \leftarrow \text{Source Register}
$$

Where $w$ is the word size in bytes (e.g., $w = 4$ for a 32-bit processor).

**POP Operation:**

The POP operation retrieves a word from the top of the stack:

$$
\text{Destination Register} \leftarrow \text{Memory[SP]}
$$

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + w
$$

> **Important:** Note the **ordering** — for PUSH, decrement SP *first*, then write. For POP, read *first*, then increment SP. This prevents the read-from-empty-stack bug.

**Stack Diagram (PUSH and POP):**

```
        PUSH Operation (Descending Stack)         POP Operation
        ───────────────────────────────           ───────────────────────────────
Initial:  SP →  ┌─────┐                          Initial:  SP →  ┌─────┐
                │     │                                    │  X  │ ← value to pop
                ├─────┤                                    ├─────┤
                │     │                                    │     │
                ├─────┤                                    ├─────┤
                │     │                                    │     │
                └─────┘                                    └─────┘

Step 1:   SP ─→ ┌─────┐                          Step 1:   Read Memory[SP] = X
        -2    │  X  │  ← new SP                    SP →  ┌─────┐
                ├─────┤                                    │  X  │ ← value to pop
                │  Y  │  ← old SP                        ├─────┤
                ├─────┤                                    │     │
                │  Z  │                                    ├─────┤
                └─────┘                                    │     │
                                                           └─────┘

Step 2:  Data written to top                        Step 2:  SP incremented
       SP → ┌─────┐                                        SP → ┌─────┐
                │  W  │  ← new data pushed                    │  X  │  ← old top
                ├─────┤                                          ├─────┤
                │  X  │  ← previously top                       │     │
                ├─────┤                                          ├─────┤
                │  Y  │                                          │     │
                ├─────┤                                          └─────┘
                │  Z  │
                └─────┘
```

**Use of Stack in Subroutine Calls (CALL and RET):**

1. **CALL Subroutine:**
   - The CPU pushes the **return address** (the address of the instruction immediately after CALL) onto the stack.
   - The PC is loaded with the **starting address of the subroutine**.
   - Execution jumps to the subroutine.

2. **Subroutine Execution:**
   - The subroutine may push additional data: **saved registers**, **parameters**, **local variables**.

3. **RET (Return):**
   - The CPU pops the **return address** from the top of the stack into the PC.
   - The calling program's execution resumes from the instruction right after the CALL.

This mechanism enables **nested subroutine calls** and **recursion**, because each CALL pushes a new return address that RET will later pop, in LIFO order.

**Stack Frame (Activation Record):**

A complete stack frame typically includes:
- **Function parameters** (pushed by caller or placed in registers)
- **Return address** (pushed by CALL instruction)
- **Saved frame pointer (old BP)** (pushed by subroutine prologue)
- **Local variables** (allocated in subroutine prologue)
- **Saved callee-save registers** (per calling convention)

The **Frame Pointer (BP/EBP)** provides a stable reference point — local variables are accessed via **fixed negative offsets from BP**, regardless of how SP moves during expression evaluation.

### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**Hardwired vs. Microprogrammed Control Unit — Comparison:**

| Parameter | Hardwired CU | Microprogrammed CU |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Implementation** | Combinational logic gates, decoders, flip-flops | Control memory (ROM/RAM) storing microinstructions |
| **Speed** | Very fast (no ROM access) | Slower (1–2 extra cycles for microcode fetch) |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid — changes require hardware redesign | Highly flexible — changes require only rewriting microcode |
| **Complexity for Large ISAs** | Combinatorial explosion; unmanageable for CISC | Scales linearly with instruction count |
| **Cost** | High initial design cost, low per-unit | Lower design cost, moderate per-unit (ROM cost) |
| **Best Suited For** | RISC processors (simple, fixed ISA) | CISC processors (complex, variable ISA) |
| **Examples** | ARM, MIPS, RISC-V cores | x86 (modern CPUs use hybrid: μcode for complex ops, hardwired for simple) |

**Working of Microprogrammed Control Unit:**

*(Refer to Section 4.6 for the block diagram.)*

The microprogrammed control unit uses a **Control Memory (ROM)** that stores the **microprogram** — a sequence of microinstructions, one per clock cycle of the instruction execution.

**Components:**

1. **Control Address Register (CAR):** Holds the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched. Width = $\log_2(\text{ROM size})$ bits.
2. **Control Memory (ROM):** Stores microinstructions. Size = $2^k \times m$ bits, where $k$ = CAR width and $m$ = control word width.
3. **Control Data Register (CDR):** Latches the microinstruction fetched from ROM in the current cycle. The bits of the CDR directly drive the CPU's control signals.
4. **Sequencing Logic:** Determines the **next** CAR value based on:
   - The current microinstruction's **next-address field**
   - The **opcode** of the instruction being executed (from IR)
   - **External conditions** (flags, interrupts)

**Execution Steps:**

1. At the start of each machine instruction, the opcode in IR is loaded into the CAR, selecting the **starting microaddress** of that instruction's micro-routine.
2. The ROM at that address outputs a microinstruction (the **control word**).
3. The CDR latches this microinstruction; its bits activate specific CPU signals (e.g., `PC_out`, `ALU_add`, `Memory_read`).
4. The sequencing logic computes the next CAR value:
   - **Sequential:** $\text{CAR} \leftarrow \text{CAR} + 1$
   - **Conditional branch:** If a flag is set, branch to a target address encoded in the microinstruction.
5. The cycle repeats until the micro-routine emits a `FETCH` signal, which loads the CAR with the address of the *next* machine instruction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Each *machine instruction* in the ISA corresponds to a *micro-routine* (a sequence of microinstructions) in the control memory. The relationship is:
> $$\text{1 Machine Instruction} = n \text{ Microinstructions}, \quad n \geq 1$$
> RISC processors typically have $n = 1$ (one microinstruction per machine instruction, also called **direct decoding**). CISC processors often have $n \gg 1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **Control Memory** (ROM containing microcode) with **Main Memory** (RAM containing programs and data). Make this distinction crystal clear in your answer — it is a guaranteed follow-up question in KTU.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PBCIT304 Module 2 — Processor Organization**

**🔑 Core Components:**
- **CPU = ALU + Control Unit + Register File + Internal Buses**
- **ALU** performs arithmetic, logical, and shift operations on operands held in registers
- **Control Unit** orchestrates instruction fetch, decode, execution, and signal generation
- **Register File** provides ultra-fast temporary storage (sub-nanosecond access)

**🔑 Essential Registers (Memorize Their Roles):**
- **PC (Program Counter):** Address of NEXT instruction to fetch
- **IR (Instruction Register):** Currently executing instruction
- **MAR (Memory Address Register):** Address used for memory read/write
- **MBR/MDR (Memory Buffer/Data Register):** Data being transferred to/from memory
- **AC (Accumulator):** Default operand and result register
- **SP (Stack Pointer):** Top-of-stack address
- **PSW/SR (Program Status Word):** Holds condition flags (Z, C, N, V, etc.)

**🔑 The Three Phases of Instruction Execution:**
- **Fetch:** $AR \leftarrow PC \rightarrow$ Read $IR \rightarrow PC \leftarrow PC + k$
- **Decode:** Opcode identified; control word generated
- **Execute:** ALU operates; flags updated; result stored

**🔑 Bus Architectures:**
- **Single-Bus:** Simple, slow (many cycles per instruction)
- **Two-Bus:** Balanced; allows simultaneous source fetch
- **Three-Bus:** Fastest; enables single-cycle R-type execution

**🔑 Critical Formulas:**
- Maximum addressable memory: $M_{max} = 2^n$ bytes (for $n$-bit address bus)
- Memory in KB: $M_{max} = 2^{n-10}$ KB
- PC update: $PC \leftarrow PC + k$ (where $k$ = instruction length in *bytes*)
- PUSH: $SP \leftarrow SP - w$; then write
- POP: Read first; then $SP \leftarrow SP + w$

**🔑 Addressing Modes (Memorize All 11):**
- Operand memory accesses: **Immediate = 0, Register = 0, All others ≥ 1**
- Fastest modes: Register, Immediate
- Most flexible: Indirect, Indexed, PC-Relative
- RISC restricts to: Register, Immediate, PC-Relative, and one or two displacement modes (Load/Store architecture)

**🔑 Stack Operations:**
- **Descending stack** (most common): grows toward lower addresses
- **PUSH order:** Decrement SP FIRST, then write
- **POP order:** Read FIRST, then increment SP
- **CALL:** PUSH return address, then jump to subroutine
- **RET:** POP return address into PC

**🔑 Control Unit Design:**
- **Hardwired:** Fast, inflexible, suited for RISC
- **Microprogrammed:** Slower, flexible, suited for CISC
- **Modern x86:** Hybrid approach (hardwired for simple ops, μcode for complex)
- **Control Memory Size:** $2^k \times m$ bits ($k$ = address bits, $m$ = control word bits)

**🔑 RISC vs. CISC Quick Facts:**
- RISC: Fixed-length instructions, load-store, 1 CPI (ideal), hardwired control
- CISC: Variable-length, memory-operand instructions, multi-cycle, microprogrammed control
- **Memory access in CISC:** Any instruction can access memory
- **Memory access in RISC:** Only LOAD and STORE can access memory

**🔑 Frequently Tested Numerical Patterns:**
- "Given $n$ address lines, find memory size" → Use $2^n$ formula
- "Show micro-operations for ADD/SUB/MOV instructions" → Use 1-bus register transfer notation
- "Convert infix to postfix and trace stack" → Standard RPN evaluation problem
- "Differentiate addressing modes" → Tabular comparison with examples

**🔑 Common KTU Conceptual Traps to Avoid:**
- ❌ Confusing PC (next addr) with IR (current instruction)
- ❌ Saying PC points to "current" instruction (it points to the *next* one)
- ❌ Confusing address bus width with addressable *bytes* vs. *words*
- ❌ Forgetting to decrement SP before PUSH (or incrementing after POP)
- ❌ Believing all addressing modes are equally fast (memory accesses matter!)
- ❌ Confusing Control Memory (μcode ROM) with Main Memory (RAM)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arithmetic Logic Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT304 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic Logic Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** is the fundamental combinational digital subsystem of the Central Processing Unit (CPU) responsible for executing all arithmetic and bit-wise logical operations on integer binary operands. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) syllabus, the ALU is defined as a combinational circuit that takes two `n`-bit operands (typically `A` and `B`), a set of control/function-select inputs, and produces an `n`-bit result `Y` along with a small set of **status flags** (Condition Codes) such as Zero, Carry, Overflow, and Negative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The ALU is a combinational circuit (no internal memory/sequential elements in its core data path) that forms the computational heart of the datapath. It implements operations dictated by the Control Unit through a binary opcode.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a highly skilled **calculator on a factory assembly line**:
- The **operands (A, B)** are the raw materials (numbers) entering from two conveyor belts.
- The **control signal (opcode)** is the instruction slip from the supervisor (Control Unit) telling the worker *which* operation to perform — "add them", "subtract", "compare", "AND the bits", etc.
- The **worker (ALU)** does the operation instantly and pushes the result onto the output belt.
- The **status flags** are the small indicator lights on the worker's desk (e.g., a red "OVERFLOW" lamp, a green "ZERO" lamp) that the supervisor peeks at to make branching decisions.

So, in plain English: *the ALU is the "calculator brain" inside the processor — it doesn't decide what to do, but it executes the math/logic operation instantly the moment it is told to.*

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Operand Widths (KTU standard):** **32-bit** (single word for most KTU architecture questions) and **64-bit** (double word in modern processors).
- **Standard Flag Set:** **4 flags** — Zero (Z), Carry (C), Overflow (V), Negative (N) / Sign (S).
- **Opcode Width for `n` operations:** $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ bits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test the boundary between *combinational* (the ALU core) and *sequential* (the flag register) parts. Remember: the ALU itself is combinational, but the flags are often latched into a Status Register for later use by branch instructions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Organization of an ALU

The ALU is built by combining three major sub-circuits and using a final output multiplexer:

1. **Arithmetic Unit (AU)** — built around a parallel adder (ripple-carry or carry-lookahead). It handles:
   - Addition: $A + B$
   - Subtraction (via 2's complement): $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$
   - Increment / Decrement
   - Multiplication / Division (in higher-end ALUs, otherwise delegated to a separate multiplier unit).

2. **Logic Unit (LU)** — performs bitwise operations on $A$ and $B$:
   - AND: $A \land B$
   - OR: $A \lor B$
   - XOR: $A \oplus B$
   - NOR, NAND (occasionally).

3. **Shifter** — performs logical left shift, logical right shift, and arithmetic right shift.

4. **Output Multiplexer (MUX)** — selects one of the parallel outputs (Arithmetic / Logic / Shift) based on a 2- or 3-bit operation-select line.

5. **Flag Generation Logic** — purely combinational circuits that derive $Z, C, V, N$ directly from the operands and the raw result.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Design

- **Why use 2's complement for subtraction?** Because the same parallel adder can be reused — the control unit simply inverts $B$ and forces a Carry-In of 1, *reusing hardware* and saving silicon area. This is the *single most important design trick* in any ALU.
- **Why are flags separate from the result?** Flags encode *meta-information* about the result (was it zero? did it overflow? what sign is it?) needed by subsequent branch/jump instructions. Storing them separately lets the programmer (or compiler) make conditional decisions *after* the operation completes.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| 2's complement negation | $-B = \overline{B} + 1$ | `n`-bit word |
| Subtraction via adder | $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$ | $C_{in} = 1$, B is inverted |
| Zero flag (Z) | $Z = 1$ iff $Y = 0$ i.e. $\text{NOR}(Y_{n-1}, \ldots, Y_0)$ | Boolean |
| Negative / Sign flag (N) | $N = Y_{n-1}$ | MSB of result |
| Carry flag (C) for addition | $C = C_{out}$ of MSB position | Boolean |
| Carry flag (C) for subtraction | $C = \overline{C_{out}}$ (borrow = NOT carry) | Boolean |
| Overflow (V) for addition | $V = C_{n-1} \oplus C_{n-2}$ (carry into MSB $\oplus$ carry out of MSB) | Boolean |
| Overflow (V) for subtraction | $V = C_{n-1} \oplus C_{n-2}$ (same formula; semantics differ) | Boolean |
| Overflow (sign rule) | $V = (A_{n-1} \odot B_{n-1}) \cdot (Y_{n-1} \oplus A_{n-1})$ | Equiv. to carry rule |
| Set on Less Than (SLT) | $\text{SLT} = (A < B) ? 1 : 0$ implemented as $\text{SLT}(A,B) = \text{SUB}(A,B)[31]$ | Sign of $A-B$ |
| Opcode width for $k$ operations | $\lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ | bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The overflow flag is *only* meaningful for *signed* arithmetic. For unsigned arithmetic, the Carry flag is what matters. Examiners love asking: *"Why is `V` meaningless for unsigned addition?"* — Answer: Unsigned numbers can never "overflow" their bit-width in a signed sense; only a Carry-out indicates magnitude overflow.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ALUs are everywhere — not just in CPUs. Specialized ALUs (or ALU-like SIMD units) drive:
- **GPUs** (parallel SIMD ALUs for graphics shading).
- **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs)** — multiply-accumulate (MAC) ALUs in audio codecs, radar.
- **Cryptographic accelerators** — XOR/Shift-heavy ALUs for AES rounds.
- **Embedded MCUs** in IoT devices (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0+ has a minimalistic 32-bit ALU).

Mastering ALU design = mastering the foundation of all modern digital computing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: 1-Bit ALU Design (Building Block)

The **1-bit ALU** is the smallest building block. The KTU-standard "1-bit ALU" supports `AND`, `OR`, and `ADD` on inputs $A$ and $B$, with $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ ripple signals.

**Step 1 — Generate AND and OR (logic outputs):**

$$
\text{AND}_{out} = A \cdot B
$$

$$
\text{OR}_{out} = A + B
$$

**Step 2 — Generate the full-adder sum and carry:**

$$
\text{Sum} = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}
$$

$$
C_{out} = (A \cdot B) + (C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B))
$$

**Step 3 — Combine via a 4-to-1 MUX controlled by 2-bit `Operation` line:**

| Operation (binary) | Function | Output |
|---|---|---|
| `00` | AND | $A \cdot B$ |
| `01` | OR | $A + B$ |
| `10` | ADD | $A + B + C_{in}$ |
| `11` | (reserved / SLT) | see below |

**Step 4 — Extend to n-bit ALU:** Cascade `n` 1-bit ALUs. $C_{out}$ of bit-$i$ feeds $C_{in}$ of bit-$(i+1)$ — this is the **ripple-carry ALU**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Subtraction Using the Same Adder

**Goal:** Compute $A - B$ using the existing adder.

**Step 1:** Take the bitwise NOT of $B$:

$$
\overline{B_i} = 1 - B_i \quad \forall i \in [0, n-1]
$$

**Step 2:** Feed $\overline{B}$ into the adder's $B$ input and force $C_{in} = 1$:

$$
A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1
$$

**Why this works (algebraic proof):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A + \overline{B} + 1
&= A + (2^n - 1 - B) + 1 \quad &\text{(since } \overline{B} = 2^n - 1 - B \text{ in n-bit unsigned)} \\
&= A + 2^n - B \\
&= A - B + 2^n \\
&\equiv A - B \pmod{2^n} &\text{(drop the carry-out from MSB)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *textbook identity* the KTU examiner expects in any subtraction question.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Overflow Detection (Addition of Two Signed Numbers)

**Setup:** Two `n`-bit signed (2's complement) numbers $A$ and $B$ are added. The result is $Y = A + B$.

**Step 1 — Define the MSB carry signals:** Let $C_{n-1}$ = carry *into* the MSB (i.e., $C_{in}$ of bit $n-1$), and $C_{n}$ = carry *out* of the MSB.

**Step 2 — Apply the overflow identity:**

$$
V = C_{n-1} \oplus C_{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Prove by cases:**

| Case | $A_{n-1}$ | $B_{n-1}$ | Sign of operands | Expected behavior | Overflow when |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | pos + pos | Result should be non-negative | result MSB = 1 ⇒ $V=1$ |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | neg + neg | Result should be non-positive | result MSB = 0 ⇒ $V=1$ |
| 3 | 0 | 1 or 1 | mixed | No overflow possible | $V = 0$ always |

**Step 4 — Equivalent sign-rule formula (often easier for students):**

$$
V = (A_{n-1} \odot B_{n-1}) \cdot (A_{n-1} \oplus Y_{n-1})
$$

In words: overflow occurs *only* when $A$ and $B$ have the **same sign** AND the result has a **different sign** from $A$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Set on Less Than (SLT) Using the ALU

**Goal:** Output a 32-bit word that is `0x00000001` if $A < B$ (signed), else `0x00000000`.

**Step 1:** Compute $A - B$ using the ALU in subtraction mode. Get the raw result and the carry-out.

**Step 2 — For signed comparison, examine the sign of the result AND whether subtraction overflowed:**

$$
\text{SLT}(A,B) = \bigl( Y_{n-1} \oplus V \bigr) \;\; \text{then zero-extend to n bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Why XOR with V?** Because if subtraction overflowed, the sign bit is *flipped* from its true meaning. XORing with $V$ corrects it.

**Step 4 — Place this at bit-position 0 and zero all other bit positions** using a 32-input AND gate (with `Operation = SLT`) driven by a "result-is-from-SLT" control line.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Compute $7 - 5$ in a 4-bit ALU, and determine all flags.

**Step 1:** Represent in 4-bit: $A = 7 = 0111_2$, $B = 5 = 0101_2$.

**Step 2:** Invert $B$: $\overline{B} = 1010_2$.

**Step 3:** Add with $C_{in} = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0111 \\
+ \; 1010 \\
+ \; \; \; 1 \\
\hline
1 \, 0010
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Discard the carry-out (1). Result: $Y = 0010_2 = 2$ ✓

**Step 5:** Flags:
- **Z** = 0 (result is not zero)
- **N** = 0 (MSB of result = 0)
- **C** (borrow inverted) = 1 (no borrow, since $7 \geq 5$)
- **V** = 0 (operands have different signs? No — both positive; but result sign matches — no overflow)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of an `n`-bit ALU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Operand A (n bits)"] --> AU1[Arithmetic Unit]
    A --> LU1[Logic Unit]
    A --> SH1[Shifter]

    B["Operand B (n bits)"] --> BInv["NOT gate per bit"]
    BInv -- "B-bar" --> AU1
    B --> LU1
    B --> SH1

    Cin["C-in from previous stage"] --> AU1

    CTRL["Operation Opcode (k bits) from Control Unit"] --> MUXsel
    CTRL --> AUctrl[Arithmetic Control: add / sub selector]
    CTRL --> LUctrl[Logic Op Selector]
    CTRL --> SHctrl[Shift Amount / Direction]
    CTRL --> FLGctrl[Flag Register Write Enable]

    AUctrl --> AU1
    LUctrl --> LU1
    SHctrl --> SH1

    AU1 --> MUXsel["Output Multiplexer (4-to-1)"]
    LU1 --> MUXsel
    SH1 --> MUXsel

    MUXsel --> Y["Result Y (n bits)"]
    MUXsel --> FG["Flag Generator Block"]

    FG --> FGz["Z flag"]
    FG --> FGc["C flag"]
    FG --> FGv["V flag"]
    FG --> FGn["N flag"]
    FG --> SR[(Status Register - sequential)]
    FLGctrl --> SR
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cascading `n` 1-Bit ALUs into an n-bit Ripple ALU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["A0"] --> ALU0["1-bit ALU slice 0"]
    A1["A1"] --> ALU1["1-bit ALU slice 1"]
    A2["A2"] --> ALU2["1-bit ALU slice 2"]
    A3["A3"] --> ALU3["1-bit ALU slice 3 (MSB)"]

    B0["B0"] --> ALU0
    B1["B1"] --> ALU1
    B2["B2"] --> ALU2
    B3["B3"] --> ALU3

    Cin0["C-in = 0 or 1 (from Control Unit)"] --> ALU0
    ALU0 -- "C-out" --> ALU1
    ALU1 -- "C-out" --> ALU2
    ALU2 -- "C-out" --> ALU3
    ALU3 -- "C-out (final)" --> Cflag["C flag (or Borrow-bar)"]

    Op0["Operation 1"]
    Op1["Operation 0"]
    Op0 --> ALU0
    Op1 --> ALU0
    Op0 --> ALU1
    Op1 --> ALU1
    Op0 --> ALU2
    Op1 --> ALU2
    Op0 --> ALU3
    Op1 --> ALU3

    ALU0 --> Y0["Y0"]
    ALU1 --> Y1["Y1"]
    ALU2 --> Y2["Y2"]
    ALU3 --> Y3["Y3 (MSB)"]
    Y3 --> Nflag["N flag = Y3"]

    ALU3 --> Vflag["V flag = C-in-to-MSB XOR C-out-of-MSB"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CU["Control Unit (decodes opcode)"] -- "Operation Select" --> ALU
    REGFILE["Register File (A, B)"] -- "Operands" --> ALU
    ALU["ALU Core (Combinational)"]
    ALU -- "n-bit result" --> REGBUS["Result / Destination Bus"]
    ALU -- "Flags Z, C, V, N" --> SR2["Status Register (sequential latch)"]
    SR2 --> CU
    REGBUS --> REGFILE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for the exam:** When the question says *"draw the block diagram of the ALU"*, draw the **first Mermaid diagram** (internal architecture). When it says *"explain the datapath interaction"*, draw the **third one** (functional flow) and label the role of the Control Unit explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[Q.A1] [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define an Arithmetic Logic Unit. List any four arithmetic and four logical operations it performs.**

**Model Answer:**
The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) is the combinational part of the CPU that performs arithmetic and logical operations on binary operands. It receives operands and a control signal (operation code) and produces a result along with status flags.

Four arithmetic operations: **Add, Subtract, Increment, Decrement, Multiply, Divide** (any four).
Four logical operations: **AND, OR, XOR, NOT, NOR, NAND** (any four).

---

**[Q.A2] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of status flags in an ALU? Name any four standard flags.**

**Model Answer:**
Status flags are single-bit indicators generated by the ALU after every operation. They capture *meta-information* about the result (such as whether it is zero, whether it overflowed, etc.) and are used by the Control Unit to make branch and conditional decisions.

Four standard flags: **Zero (Z), Carry (C), Overflow (V), Negative / Sign (N)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Part B Module 2 carries 14 marks. Students must answer **one full question** (either OR). Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts: (a) and (b). Sub-parts often test design + analysis.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified Past Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal organization of a basic `n`-bit Arithmetic Logic Unit. Clearly show the role of the arithmetic unit, logic unit, shifter, output multiplexer, and flag generator. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a **1-bit ALU slice** that supports `AND`, `OR`, and `ADD` operations. Show how `n` such slices can be cascaded to form an `n`-bit ALU. Explain how the Carry flag and Overflow flag are derived. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram and explanation (7 marks):**
- Draw the **internal architecture Mermaid diagram** (SECTION 4.1) converted to a hand-drawn-style block diagram.
- Briefly explain:
  1. Arithmetic Unit — performs add/sub using parallel adder and 2's complement trick.
  2. Logic Unit — performs bitwise AND, OR, XOR, etc.
  3. Shifter — performs shift operations.
  4. Output MUX — selects the result based on the `Operation` opcode.
  5. Flag Generator — derives Z, C, V, N from the result and operands.
  6. Control signals originate from the Control Unit; flags are latched in the Status Register (sequential).
- Mention that the ALU core is **combinational**, but flags are latched **sequentially** in a status register.

**(b) Design (7 marks):**

1-Bit ALU Slice:

$$
\text{Result} = \begin{cases}
A \cdot B & \text{if Operation} = 00 \\
A + B & \text{if Operation} = 01 \\
