| Input | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **I (Increment)** | CAR $\leftarrow$ CAR + 1 |
| **MUX Select = 00** | Source = current address + 1 |
| **MUX Select = 01** | Source = Branch address field |
| **MUX Select = 10** | Source = MAP ROM output (instruction opcode mapping) |
| **MUX Select = 11** | Source = Return address from subroutine stack |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DMA Transfer Time** | $T_{\text{transfer}} = n \cdot t_{\text{cycle}} + T_{\text{setup}}$ | $n$ = number of words, $t_{\text{cycle}}$ = memory cycle time |
| **Cycle Stealing Throughput** | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{t_{\text{DMA}}}{t_{\text{CPU}}}}$ | $t_{\text{DMA}}$ = DMA transfer time per word |
| **Control Memory Size** | $2^k \times n$ | $k$ = CAR bits, $n$ = microinstruction width |
| **Microinstruction Address** | $\text{Next Address} = \text{CAR} + 1$ (sequential) | Standard increment for flow |
| **Horizontal Microinstruction Width** | $n = \sum \text{control signals}$ | One bit per control line |
| **Vertical Microinstruction Width** | $n = \lceil \log_2(\text{coded fields}) \rceil$ | Decoded fields |
| **Interrupt Latency** | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{recognise}} + T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{vector}}$ | Time from IRQ to first ISR instruction |
| **Interrupts per second (max)** | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{latency}} + T_{\text{ISR}} + T_{\text{restore}}}$ | System interrupt capacity |
| **Daisy Chain Priority** | $\text{Priority} = k$ for $k^{\text{th}}$ device | Device closer to CPU has higher priority |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are routinely used in real-world scenarios: designing embedded controllers (DMA burst sizing), evaluating real-time OS schedulers (interrupt latency budgets), and synthesising ASIC control units (control memory sizing).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DMA Transfer Time

**Problem Context:** A DMA controller transfers a block of **4096 words** from a disk to memory. The memory cycle time is **200 ns**, and the DMAC setup time is **500 ns**. Calculate the total transfer time in burst mode and in cycle-stealing mode (assuming each stolen cycle adds 50 ns CPU penalty).

**Burst Mode Derivation:**

In burst mode, the DMAC holds the bus continuously for the entire block.

$$T_{\text{burst}} = T_{\text{setup}} + n \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}$$

Substituting values:

$$T_{\text{burst}} = 500 \text{ ns} + 4096 \cdot 200 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{\text{burst}} = 500 \text{ ns} + 819{,}200 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{\text{burst}} = 819{,}700 \text{ ns} \approx 819.7 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Cycle Stealing Mode Derivation:**

In cycle stealing, each word transfer takes one memory cycle, plus an additional 50 ns overhead for releasing and re-acquiring the bus.

$$t_{\text{steal}} = t_{\text{cycle}} + t_{\text{overhead}} = 200 + 50 = 250 \text{ ns per word}$$

$$T_{\text{steal}} = T_{\text{setup}} + n \cdot t_{\text{steal}}$$

$$T_{\text{steal}} = 500 + 4096 \cdot 250 = 500 + 1{,}024{,}000$$

$$T_{\text{steal}} = 1{,}024{,}500 \text{ ns} \approx 1.0245 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** Burst mode is ~25% faster but freezes the CPU. Cycle stealing is slower but allows CPU-DMA concurrency.

### 3.2 Derivation: Control Memory Sizing

**Problem:** A processor has **128 instructions** in its ISA. Each instruction is implemented by an average of **4 microinstructions**. The microinstruction format is **horizontal** with **32 control signals**. Determine the size of the Control Memory.

**Step 1 — Determine microinstruction width.**

For a horizontal microinstruction, width = number of control signals.

$$W = 32 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Determine total number of microinstructions.**

$$M_{\text{total}} = 128 \text{ instructions} \cdot 4 \text{ microinstructions/instruction} = 512$$

**Step 3 — Determine address bits required.**

We need $k$ bits such that $2^k \ge 512$.

$$2^9 = 512 \implies k = 9 \text{ address bits}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Control Memory size.**

$$\text{CM size} = 2^k \times W = 2^9 \times 32 = 512 \times 32 \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{CM size} = 16{,}384 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ KB}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Microprogram Sequencer Next-Address Logic

**Problem:** Design the MUX-based next-address logic for a sequencer with **4 selection modes**. The current microinstruction's sequencing field `S` (2 bits) selects the source. The condition flag is `F`. The branch address field is `BRA` (8 bits), and the return address stack output is `RAS` (8 bits).

**Step 1 — MUX Selection Truth Table.**

| S1 | S0 | Selected Source | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 0 | CAR + 1 | Sequential |
| 0 | 1 | BRA (if F=1) else CAR+1 | Conditional Branch |
| 1 | 0 | MAP (opcode mapped) | Instruction Map |
| 1 | 1 | RAS | Return from Subroutine |

**Step 2 — Derive Boolean Expression for Each Output Line.**

For the **Sequential (Sequential)** path:

$$L_0 = \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0}$$

For the **Conditional Branch** path:

$$L_1 = \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \cdot F + \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \cdot \overline{F} \cdot 0$$

This simplifies to $L_1 = \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \cdot F$.

For the **MAP** path:

$$L_2 = S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0}$$

For the **Subroutine Return** path:

$$L_3 = S_1 \cdot S_0$$

**Step 3 — Final Multiplexer Output.**

$$\text{Next\_CAR} = L_0 \cdot (\text{CAR} + 1) + L_1 \cdot \text{BRA} + L_2 \cdot \text{MAP} + L_3 \cdot \text{RAS}$$

This 4-to-1 multiplexer is the **core of the microprogram sequencer**.

### 3.4 Derivation: Interrupt Latency Budget

**Problem:** A real-time embedded system polls its sensor every **1 ms**. The interrupt recognition time is **150 ns**, the context save takes **80 ns**, and the vector fetch takes **120 ns**. The ISR is 50 instructions, each executing in **20 ns**. Calculate the maximum sustainable interrupt rate.

**Step 1 — Compute ISR execution time.**

$$T_{\text{ISR}} = 50 \cdot 20 = 1000 \text{ ns} = 1 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total time per interrupt.**

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{recognise}} + T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{vector}} + T_{\text{ISR}} + T_{\text{restore}}$$

Assuming $T_{\text{restore}} \approx T_{\text{save}} = 80$ ns:

$$T_{\text{total}} = 150 + 80 + 120 + 1000 + 80 = 1430 \text{ ns} \approx 1.43 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute max interrupt rate.**

$$f_{\max} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{1.43 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 699{,}300 \text{ interrupts/sec} \approx 0.7 \text{ MHz}$$

Since the sensor polls at 1 kHz (much slower), the system has ample margin.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control

| Parameter | Hardwired | Microprogrammed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Speed** | Very fast (gate delay) | Slower (ROM access) |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid (hardware fixed) | Highly flexible (ROM rewritable) |
| **Design Complexity** | High for complex ISA | Moderate, systematic |
| **Cost of ISA Change** | Redesign gates | Replace ROM |
| **Suitability** | RISC, Pipelined CPUs | CISC, Emulation, Firmware |
| **Implementation** | AND-OR / PLA / FSM | Control Memory + Sequencer |
| **Debugging** | Difficult | Easier (microcode visible) |
| **Examples** | MIPS, ARM Cortex-A72 | x86 (microcode ROM in Pentium) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Interrupt Handling Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU executes Instruction] --> B{Interrupt Pending?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Complete Current Instruction]
    C --> D[Disable Interrupts IE=0]
    D --> E[Push PC onto Stack]
    E --> F[Push PSW onto Stack]
    F --> G[Load ISR Vector Address]
    G --> H[Execute ISR]
    H --> I[Pop PSW from Stack]
    I --> J[Pop PC from Stack]
    J --> K[Enable Interrupts IE=1]
    K --> A
```

### 4.2 DMA Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU] -->|HOLD Request| DMAC[DMA Controller]
    DMAC -->|HLDA Acknowledge| CPU
    DMAC -->|Address Bus| MEM[Main Memory]
    MEM -->|Data Bus| DMAC
    IO[I/O Device] <-->|Data Bus| DMAC
    DMAC -->|DMA Acknowledge| IO
    SAR[Starting Address Register] --> DMAC
    WCR[Word Count Register] --> DMAC
    CR[Control Register] --> DMAC
```

### 4.3 Hardwired Control Unit Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IR[Instruction Register IR] --> DEC[Opcode Decoder]
    CLK[Clock Generator] --> TS[Timing State T0 T1 T2 T3]
    CC[Condition Flags Z N C V] --> LOGIC[Combinational Logic AND OR NOT]
    EXT[External Inputs Ready Hold] --> LOGIC
    DEC --> LOGIC
    TS --> LOGIC
    LOGIC --> CS[Control Signals C0 to Cn]
    CS --> REG[Registers]
    CS --> ALU
    CS --> MEM[Memory Bus]
```

### 4.4 Microprogrammed Control Unit Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CM[Control Memory ROM] --> CDR[Control Data Register CDR]
    CDR --> F1[F1 ALU Op]
    CDR --> F2[F2 Source Reg]
    CDR --> F3[F3 Dest Reg]
    CDR --> F4[F4 Memory Op]
    CDR --> F5[F5 Sequencing Field S1 S0]
    CDR --> F6[F6 Special Function]
    F5 --> SEQ[Microprogram Sequencer]
    CC[Condition Flags] --> SEQ
    RAS[Return Address Stack] --> SEQ
    MAP[MAP ROM Opcode to Address] --> SEQ
    SEQ --> MUX[4 to 1 MUX]
    MUX --> CAR[Control Address Register CAR]
    CLK[Clock] --> CAR
    CAR --> CM
```

### 4.5 Microprogram Sequencer Internal Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[S1] --> MUX[MUX 4 to 1]
    S0[S0] --> MUX
    F[Flag F] --> MUX
    INC[CAR plus 1] --> MUX
    BRA[Branch Address Field] --> MUX
    MAP[MAP Output] --> MUX
    RAS[Return Address Stack] --> MUX
    MUX --> NCAR[Next CAR Value]
    NCAR --> CAR_REG[CAR Register]
    CAR_REG --> CM_ADDR[Control Memory Address]
```

### 4.6 DMA Transfer Mode Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[CPU initiates DMA] --> B{Transfer Mode}
    B -- Burst --> C[DMAC holds bus for entire block]
    B -- Cycle Stealing --> D[DMAC transfers 1 word per cycle]
    B -- Transparent --> E[DMAC transfers only during CPU idle cycles]
    C --> F[CPU fully halted]
    D --> G[CPU paused 1 cycle per word]
    E --> H[CPU not affected]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Direct Memory Access. List and explain any three data transfer modes used by a DMA controller.**

**Model Answer:**
**Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a technique that allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from main memory without involving the CPU, thus improving system throughput. A dedicated hardware called the **DMA Controller (DMAC)** manages the transfer.

The three data transfer modes are:

1. **Burst Mode (Block Transfer):** The DMAC acquires the bus and retains it for the entire data block transfer. The CPU remains in the HOLD state until the transfer completes. This is the **fastest** mode but freezes the CPU.
2. **Cycle Stealing Mode:** The DMAC transfers **one word per bus cycle**, then releases the bus back to the CPU. The CPU is delayed by one memory cycle per stolen word but can interleave instruction execution with data transfer.
3. **Transparent Mode:** The DMAC performs transfers only when the CPU is **not using the system bus** (e.g., during instruction decode). The CPU sees no performance penalty, but the DMAC transfer is slower because it must wait for idle cycles.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Hardwired Control and Microprogrammed Control Units. (Any 3 points)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Implementation** | Combinational logic gates (AND, OR, NOT) | Control memory (ROM) with stored microinstructions |
| **Speed** | Faster (one gate delay) | Slower (ROM access time) |
| **Flexibility** | Inflexible, requires redesign for new instructions | Highly flexible, just rewrite the ROM (firmware update) |
| **Design Complexity** | Increases exponentially with instruction set size | Easier and systematic via microprogramming |
| **Debugging** | Very difficult | Easier (microcode can be inspected) |
| **Suitability** | RISC, pipelined processors | CISC processors, instruction set emulation |
| **Examples** | MIPS, ARM Cortex-A series | Intel x86 (uses microcode ROM since Pentium Pro) |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a DMA controller**. Describe the role of **HOLD** and **HLDA** signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system uses DMA to transfer **8 KB** of data from a disk to memory. The memory cycle time is **100 ns** and DMAC initialisation takes **300 ns**. Calculate the total transfer time in **burst mode** and **cycle stealing mode** (assume 40 ns overhead per stolen cycle). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DMA Architecture (7 marks):**

A DMA controller is a dedicated hardware unit that offloads bulk data transfer from the CPU. Its key components are:

* **Starting Address Register (SAR):** Stores the memory address of the first byte/word to be transferred.
* **Word Count Register (WCR):** Holds the number of words remaining to be transferred; decremented after each transfer.
* **Control Register (CR):** Specifies the transfer mode (read/write), the I/O device involved, and bus arbitration priority.
* **Data Buffer / Latch:** Temporarily holds the word being transferred.
* **Bus Arbitration Logic:** Manages HOLD and HLDA handshake with the CPU.

**HOLD and HLDA Mechanism:**

1. The DMAC raises the **HOLD** signal to the CPU when it is ready to transfer data.
2. The CPU completes its current bus cycle, then **tri-states** its address, data, and control lines.
3. The CPU responds with **HLDA (Hold Acknowledge)**, indicating the bus is now available to the DMAC.
4. The DMAC takes control of the bus and performs the data transfer.
5. After the transfer (WCR = 0 or explicit release), the DMAC de-asserts HOLD, and the CPU re-asserts HLDA = 0 and resumes execution.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU] -->|HOLD| DMAC
    DMAC -->|HLDA| CPU
    SAR[SAR] --> DMAC
    WCR[WCR] --> DMAC
    CR[CR] --> DMAC
    DMAC -->|Address Data Control| MEM[Main Memory]
    DMAC -->|Data Bus| IO[I/O Device]
```

**(b) Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
* $n = 8 \text{ KB} = 8192 \text{ words}$
* $t_{\text{cycle}} = 100 \text{ ns}$
* $T_{\text{setup}} = 300 \text{ ns}$
* $t_{\text{overhead}} = 40 \text{ ns}$ (for cycle stealing only)

**Burst Mode Calculation:**

$$T_{\text{burst}} = T_{\text{setup}} + n \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}$$

$$T_{\text{burst}} = 300 + 8192 \cdot 100 = 300 + 819{,}200$$

$$T_{\text{burst}} = 819{,}500 \text{ ns} \approx 819.5 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Cycle Stealing Mode Calculation:**

$$t_{\text{steal}} = t_{\text{cycle}} + t_{\text{overhead}} = 100 + 40 = 140 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{\text{steal}} = T_{\text{setup}} + n \cdot t_{\text{steal}}$$

$$T_{\text{steal}} = 300 + 8192 \cdot 140 = 300 + 1{,}146{,}880$$

$$T_{\text{steal}} = 1{,}147{,}180 \text{ ns} \approx 1.147 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** Burst mode is **~28% faster** but freezes the CPU. Cycle stealing allows CPU concurrency at the cost of total transfer time.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — Alternative Choice**
**(a)** Explain the **microprogrammed control unit** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **microinstruction format** and the **sequencing of microinstructions**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A processor has **64 instructions**. The average number of microinstructions per machine instruction is **5**. The microinstruction format is horizontal with **24 control signals**. Compute the size of the **Control Memory**. Also derive the next-address logic of the sequencer using a 4-to-1 MUX. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Microprogrammed Control Unit (7 marks):**

A microprogrammed control unit implements the control function using a **Control Memory (CM)** that stores a set of microinstructions for each machine instruction.

**Block Diagram Components:**

* **Control Memory (CM):** A fast ROM holding all microinstructions (microprogram).
* **Control Address Register (CAR):** Holds the address of the next microinstruction to fetch.
* **Control Data Register (CDR):** Latches the microinstruction read from CM; its output drives all control signals.
* **Microprogram Sequencer:** Decides the next CAR value (sequential, branch, map, or return).
* **Condition Flags (F):** Provide status to the sequencer for conditional branches.
* **MAP ROM:** Maps each machine instruction opcode to the starting address of its microprogram.

**Microinstruction Format:**

The microinstruction is divided into functional fields:

| Field | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| F1 | ALU operation (ADD, SUB, AND, etc.) |
| F2 | Source register selection |
| F3 | Destination register selection |
| F4 | Memory read/write control |
| F5 | Sequencing bits (S1, S0) for the sequencer |
| F6 | I/O / interrupt acknowledge |

**Sequencing of Microinstructions:**

* **Sequential:** $S_1 S_0 = 00 \Rightarrow \text{CAR} \leftarrow \text{CAR} + 1$
* **Conditional Branch:** $S_1 S_0 = 01 \Rightarrow \text{CAR} \leftarrow \text{BRA}$ if flag F = 1, else CAR + 1
* **Map (Decode):** $S_1 S_0 = 10 \Rightarrow \text{CAR} \leftarrow \text{MAP ROM (opcode)}$
* **Return:** $S_1 S_0 = 11 \Rightarrow \text{CAR} \leftarrow \text{RAS (return stack)}$

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CM[Control Memory ROM] --> CDR[CDR]
    CDR --> F1[F1 ALU]
    CDR --> F2[F2 Src]
    CDR --> F3[F3 Dst]
    CDR --> F4[F4 Memory]
    CDR --> F5[F5 Sequence S1 S0]
    CDR --> F6[F6 Special]
    F5 --> SEQ[Microprogram Sequencer]
    FLG[Flags] --> SEQ
    MAP[MAP ROM] --> SEQ
    SEQ --> MUX[4 to 1 MUX]
    MUX --> CAR[CAR]
    CAR --> CM
```

**(b) Computation & Next-Address Logic (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total microinstructions:**

$$M_{\text{total}} = 64 \cdot 5 = 320$$

**Step 2 — Address bits:**

$2^k \ge 320 \implies 2^9 = 512 \ge 320 \implies k = 9$

**Step 3 — Microinstruction width:**

Horizontal format $\Rightarrow W = 24$ bits

**Step 4 — Control Memory size:**

$$\text{CM} = 2^9 \cdot 24 = 512 \cdot 24 = 12{,}288 \text{ bits} \approx 1.5 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 5 — Next-Address Logic (4-to-1 MUX):**

$$\text{Next\_CAR} = (\overline{S_1}\overline{S_0})(CAR+1) + (\overline{S_1}S_0 \cdot F)(BRA) + (S_1\overline{S_0})(MAP) + (S_1 S_0)(RAS)$$

**Truth Table:**

| S1 | S0 | MUX Output | Operation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 0 | CAR + 1 | Sequential |
| 0 | 1 | BRA (if F=1) | Conditional Branch |
| 1 | 0 | MAP | Instruction Mapping |
| 1 | 1 | RAS | Subroutine Return |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Exams (Module 3):**
> 1. **Confusing traps with interrupts:** Students often mark *Divide-by-zero* as an asynchronous interrupt. KTU strictly expects **traps = synchronous**, **interrupts = asynchronous**. **−1 mark** if swapped.
> 2. **Omitting HOLD/HLDA direction:** In DMA diagrams, students frequently draw HOLD and HLDA as **bi-directional arrows**. They are **unidirectional**: HOLD goes **CPU ← DMAC**, HLDA goes **CPU → DMAC**. Examiner deducts **½ mark** for wrong direction.
> 3. **Microprogram Sequencer MUX selector bits:** When asked to design the next-address logic, students often forget that the **conditional branch path requires an AND with the flag F**. Without it, the branch becomes unconditional, which is logically wrong. **−2 marks** for missing the flag.
> 4. **Mixing horizontal and vertical formats:** Students write "horizontal microinstruction" but then encode fields using binary codes — that is a **vertical** microinstruction. KTU expects strict terminology. **−1 mark** for mismatch.
> 5. **Forgetting WCR decrement:** In DMA numerical problems, students often forget to account for the **decrement** and **end-of-transfer** condition ($WCR = 0$). Examiners check for this; missing it loses **1–2 marks**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Interrupt vs Trap:** Asynchronous vs Synchronous — a frequently asked 2-mark question.
* **DMA is faster** than programmed I/O because it eliminates the CPU from the data path.
* **HOLD/HLDA** signals are the standard bus arbitration handshake for DMA in 8086-family processors.
* **Burst Mode** is fastest but freezes the CPU; **Transparent Mode** has no CPU impact but is the slowest.
* **Hardwired Control** uses combinational logic; **Microprogrammed Control** uses stored microinstructions.
* **Hardwired** is **fast but rigid**; **Microprogrammed** is **slow but flexible**.
* **Control Memory** is typically a fast ROM whose size = $2^k \times n$ bits.
* **Horizontal microinstructions** = one bit per control signal; **Vertical microinstructions** = encoded fields.
* **Microprogram Sequencer** uses a 4-to-1 MUX with selection inputs $S_1 S_0$ to choose: Sequential (00), Branch (01), MAP (10), Return (11).
* **MAP ROM** converts each machine instruction's opcode into the starting address of its microprogram.
* **Maurice Wilkes (1951)** proposed microprogramming — a common KTU history question.
* **Daisy-chain priority** gives the device **closest to the CPU** the highest priority.
* **Cycle Stealing Throughput** decreases as the ratio of DMA time to CPU time increases.
* **DMA Transfer Time** = $T_{\text{setup}} + n \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}$ (burst) or $T_{\text{setup}} + n(t_{\text{cycle}} + t_{\text{overhead}})$ (cycle stealing).
* **Real-world applications:** Hardwired → RISC pipelines, GPUs, network routers; Microprogrammed → x86 CPUs, firmware-based emulators, microcontrollers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Programmed CPU Organization - Horizontal and Vertical Micro Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT304 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Micro Programmed CPU Organization - Horizontal and Vertical Micro Instructions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition: Microprogrammed Control Unit > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition Box** > A **Microprogrammed Control Unit (MPCU)** is a control unit whose control logic is implemented as a *microprogram* stored in a dedicated, high-speed **Control Memory (CM)**. Each *machine-language instruction* is fetched and interpreted by executing a pre-defined sequence of low-level binary words called **microinstructions**. Each microinstruction directly activates one or more **control signals** that orchestrate a single *micro-operation* (register transfer) within a single *clock cycle* of the CPU datapath. A microprogram is therefore a **micro-level interpreter** for the macro-level (machine) instruction set. The control memory is usually a **ROM** (or Writable Control Store in advanced designs) that is *non-volatile* and is programmed once during CPU fabrication. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hierarchy of Control Terminology (KTU Syllabus Mapping):** > - **Micro-operation:** The smallest data-movement operation (e.g., `R1 ← R2 + R3`). > - **Microinstruction:** The binary control word stored in CM that initiates one or more micro-operations. > - **Microprogram:** A complete sequence of microinstructions implementing *one* macro-instruction fetch–decode–execute cycle. > - **Firmware:** The whole collection of microprograms (i.e., the contents of CM). --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!TIP] > **The Orchestra Conductor Analogy** 🎼 > Imagine a CPU datapath as an **orchestra of musicians** (ALU, registers, buses, memory). A **microprogrammed control unit** acts like a **conductor holding a musical score**. > - The **conductor's scorebook** = **Control Memory (ROM)** containing pre-written microinstructions. > - The **page currently being played** = **Control Data Register (CDR)** holding the active microinstruction. > - The **conductor's baton** = the **decoder**, which translates the symbols on the page into hand gestures to specific musicians. > - The **conductor's eye** = **Next Address Logic**, deciding which page to turn to next. > - The **audience's applause (loud/quiet)** = **condition flags (CC)**, telling the conductor whether to repeat a section or move on. > > In this analogy, a *Hardwired Control Unit* would be a conductor who has memorized the entire symphony by heart (faster, but extremely hard to modify), while a *Microprogrammed Control Unit* reads from a scorebook (slower per page, but infinitely flexible to change). --- ### 1.3 Definition: Horizontal vs. Vertical Microinstructions | Term | Formal Definition | |---|---| | **Horizontal Microinstruction** | A *wide, unencoded* microinstruction in which **each bit directly corresponds to one specific control signal** of the datapath. No decoding network is required; the bit itself *is* the activation wire. | | **Vertical Microinstruction** | A *narrow, encoded* microinstruction in which the control field is **divided into functional sub-fields**, each sub-field being **decoded** to produce the actual control signals. Looks similar to a conventional machine instruction. | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Misconception:** "Vertical = vertical layout of the chip." **This is false.** The names refer to the **bit-width vs. the degree of encoding**, not the physical geometry. Horizontal is **wide and flat** (one control point per bit); vertical is **narrow and stacked** (encoded groups). --- ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Reference Numbers (for typical exam problems):** > - A simple RISC-style CPU datapath has approximately **20 to 50 control signals**. > - The control memory of a classical single-bus CPU typically contains **2¹⁰ = 1024 microinstructions** (4 KB) to **2¹² = 4096 microinstructions** (16 KB). > - A horizontal microinstruction word is typically **48 to 128 bits wide**. > - A vertical microinstruction word is typically **16 to 32 bits wide**. > - The microinstruction cycle time = **20 ns to 100 ns**, which is *shorter* than the macro-instruction cycle. --- ### 1.5 Why Microprogrammed Control? | Feature | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control | |---|---|---| | Implementation | Combinational logic / PLA | ROM + decoder | | Flexibility | Hard to modify | Easy to patch firmware | | Speed | Faster (no ROM access) | Slower (extra ROM latency) | | Design complexity | High for complex ISA | Low (sequential design) | | Cost for CISC | Prohibitive | Economical | | Used in | RISC pipelines | Classical CISC, emulators | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 General Organization of a Microprogrammed Control Unit The MPCU consists of **four** essential hardware blocks: 1. **Control Memory (CM):** A ROM of size $2^k \times n$ where $k$ = address width, $n$ = microinstruction width. Stores all microprograms. 2. **Control Address Register (CAR):** Holds the address of the *next* microinstruction to fetch. Equivalent to the PC at the micro-level. 3. **Control Data Register (CDR):** Latches the microinstruction *just read* from CM. Equivalent to the IR at the micro-level. 4. **Next Address Logic (NAL) / Branch Logic:** Combinational circuit that decides the next value of CAR based on (a) the branch field of current microinstruction, (b) the opcode of the macro-instruction being decoded, and (c) the current condition-code flags (CC). > [!NOTE] > **Two External Inputs to the NAL:** > - The **Branch Address (BA)** field of the current microinstruction. > - The **Condition (CD)** field of the current microinstruction (e.g., "branch if Z=1"). > > The output of NAL is the **Next Address (NA)** that is loaded into CAR at the next clock edge. --- ### 2.2 The Microinstruction Format A generic microinstruction is composed of **four logical fields** regardless of horizontal/vertical nature: $$ \text{Microinstruction Word} \;=\; \underbrace{F_1 \, F_2 \, \dots \, F_p}_{\text{Control Field}} \; \mid \; \underbrace{CD}_{\text{Condition}} \; \mid \; \underbrace{BA}_{\text{Branch Address}} \; \mid \; \underbrace{NA}_{\text{Next Address}} $$ - **Control Field** $F_1 \dots F_p$ → activates datapath signals. - **Condition (CD)** → 2–3 bits selecting a flag (e.g., Z, N, C, V, I, Unconditional). - **Branch Address (BA)** → k-bit target address if condition is true. - **Next Address (NA)** → k-bit fall-through address if condition is false. > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Fact:** A *horizontal* microinstruction simply makes the *Control Field* very wide (one bit per signal). A *vertical* microinstruction packs the same set of signals into encoded sub-fields $F_1 \dots F_p$. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these five formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical question on this topic.** | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Horizontal microinstruction bit-width** | $H = N$ | where $N$ = total number of control signals. | | 2 | **Vertical microinstruction bit-width** | $V = p \cdot \lceil \log_2(g) \rceil$ | where $p$ = number of *groups*, $g$ = signals per group. | | 3 | **Number of groups in vertical format** | $p = \lceil N / g \rceil$ | $g$ = maximum signals decodable per encoded field. | | 4 | **Control Memory capacity** | $C_M = 2^k \times n$ bits | $k$ = CAR width, $n$ = microinstruction word width. | | 5 | **Encoding efficiency ratio** | $\eta = V / H \times 100\,\%$ | Lower $\eta$ = more compact, but slower due to decoding delay. | > [!WARNING] > **Ceiling Function Pitfall:** Always use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ for vertical grouping, never floor division. Example: $\lceil 21/8 \rceil = 3$ groups, not $2$. A KTU examiner will deduct a full mark for using $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$. --- ### 2.4 Horizontal vs. Vertical: Detailed Feature Comparison > [!NOTE] > **The 4-axis comparison that KTU loves to ask:** | Parameter | Horizontal Microinstruction | Vertical Microinstruction | |---|---|---| | **Bit-width** | Very wide ($\ge 50$ bits typical) | Narrow ($16$–$32$ bits typical) | | **Encoding** | None (direct mapping) | Field-encoded with sub-decoders | | **Parallelism** | High — multiple signals active simultaneously | Low — only one signal per decoded sub-field | | **Decoding** | Not required | Required (one decoder per sub-field) | | **Cycle time** | Short (no decoding delay) | Longer (decoder adds $T_{dec}$) | | **Firmware size** | Large (wasted bits) | Compact (efficient bit usage) | | **Design effort** | Easy to design | Complex decoder design | | **Easily understood by** | Hardware designer | Programmer (looks like ISA) | | **Typical use** | CISC, Wilkes, fast pipelines | RISC emulation, microcontrollers | | **Risk of bit-waste** | High | Low | | **Risk of conflicts** | None (one bit per signal) | Possible (overlapping encodings) | | **Mutually exclusive grouping** | Not needed | Mandatory (signals in same group must never be active together) | --- ### 2.5 Hybrid / Diagonal Microinstructions (Bonus) > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Trend:** A third category called **Diagonal** (or *Hybrid*) microinstructions is now part of the syllabus. These use a *partially encoded* format where the most frequently used signals are left unencoded (1 bit each), and the rest are grouped. Bit width typically lies *between* H and V. Example: Intel microcode uses diagonal encoding. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The microprogrammed paradigm is the foundation of: - **Intel x86 microcode updates (CPU bug fixes)** — patches are released as *microcode updates* that modify the microprogram in Writable Control Store. - **IBM mainframe emulators** — one CPU model can emulate another by loading a different microprogram. - **FPGA soft-cores** (e.g., Nios II, MicroBlaze) — the ISA is implemented entirely as a microprogram stored in on-chip BRAM. - **Design of instruction set simulators (ISS)** for verification. - **Fault-tolerant computing** — alternate microprograms can provide graceful degradation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bit-Width of a Horizontal Microinstruction Let the CPU datapath have a total of $N$ distinct control signals (e.g., $PC_{in}, PC_{out}, MAR_{in}, READ, ALU_{add}, \dots$). **Step 1 — Mapping logic:** In a horizontal format, *each control signal is assigned exactly one dedicated bit* in the microinstruction word. If the bit is `1`, the signal is asserted; if `0`, it is deasserted. **Step 2 — Counting:** Therefore the total number of bits required equals the number of distinct control signals: $$ H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} 1 \;=\; N $$ **Step 3 — Numerical example:** Suppose a CPU has the following control signals: - $PC_{in}, PC_{out}$ — 2 bits - $MAR_{in}, MDR_{in}, MDR_{out}$ — 3 bits - $READ, WRITE$ — 2 bits - $AC_{in}, AC_{out}, DR_{in}, DR_{out}$ — 4 bits - $ALU_{add}, ALU_{sub}, ALU_{and}, ALU_{or}$ — 4 bits - $INC_{PC}, END$ — 2 bits Total: $N = 2+3+2+4+4+2 = 17$ control signals. $$ H = N = 17 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 4 — Verdict:** No decoder is required. The 17-bit word is *directly* fanned out to the control points of the datapath. This is the lowest latency option. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bit-Width of a Vertical Microinstruction Suppose we want to pack the same $N = 17$ signals into a vertical format using $g$ signals per encoded sub-field. **Step 1 — Number of groups:** $$ p = \left\lceil \frac{N}{g} \right\rceil $$ With $g = 8$ (i.e., 3 bits per group since $2^3 = 8$): $$ p = \lceil 17 / 8 \rceil = \lceil 2.125 \rceil = 3 \text{ groups} $$ **Step 2 — Bits per sub-field:** $$ b_i = \lceil \log_2(g) \rceil $$ For $g = 8$, $b_i = \log_2(8) = 3$ bits. **Step 3 — Total vertical width:** $$ V = \sum_{i=1}^{p} b_i \;=\; p \cdot \lceil \log_2(g) \rceil $$ $$ V = 3 \times 3 = 9 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 4 — Encoder/Decoder requirement:** Each of the 3 sub-fields needs an independent **3-to-8 decoder**. The decoder adds a propagation delay of $T_{dec} \approx 2$ gate delays per stage, so the cycle time of a vertical format is **longer** than a horizontal one. **Step 5 — Encoding efficiency:** $$ \eta = \frac{V}{H} \times 100\% = \frac{9}{17} \times 100\% \approx 52.94\,\% $$ So the vertical format uses about **47% fewer bits** than the horizontal format, at the cost of an extra decoder stage. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Control Memory Capacity A control memory with $k$ address bits and $n$-bit microinstruction words stores $2^k$ microinstructions. The total capacity in *bits* is: $$ C_{M} = 2^{k} \times n \;\; \text{bits} \;=\; \frac{2^{k} \times n}{8} \;\; \text{bytes} $$ **Numerical example:** Let $k = 10$ and $n = 32$. $$ C_{M} = 2^{10} \times 32 = 1024 \times 32 = 32{,}768 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ KB} $$ If the design uses a *vertical* format with $n = 16$: $$ C_{M} = 2^{10} \times 16 = 16{,}384 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ KB} $$ So switching from horizontal to vertical **halves** the firmware ROM cost. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: End-to-End Numerical (Typical KTU 14-mark sub-question) **Problem:** A CPU has **$N = 32$ control signals** and a control memory with **$k = 12$ address bits**. **(a)** Design the **horizontal** microinstruction format and calculate ROM size. **(b)** Design a **vertical** microinstruction format with **$g = 4$ signals per group** and calculate ROM size. **(c)** Compute the **percentage saving** in ROM capacity. #### (a) Horizontal Format $$ H = N = 32 \text{ bits} $$ $$ C_{M}^{\text{horiz}} = 2^{12} \times 32 = 4096 \times 32 = 131{,}072 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ KB} $$ #### (b) Vertical Format Number of groups: $$ p = \lceil 32 / 4 \rceil = 8 \text{ groups} $$ Bits per group: $$ b = \lceil \log_2(4) \rceil = 2 \text{ bits} $$ Total vertical width: $$ V = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ bits} $$ $$ C_{M}^{\text{vert}} = 2^{12} \times 16 = 4096 \times 16 = 65{,}536 \text{ bits} = 8 \text{ KB} $$ #### (c) Percentage Saving $$ \text{Saving} = \frac{C_M^{\text{horiz}} - C_M^{\text{vert}}}{C_M^{\text{horiz}}} \times 100\% $$ $$ = \frac{131{,}072 - 65{,}536}{131{,}072} \times 100\% = \frac{65{,}536}{131{,}072} \times 100\% = 50.0\,\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** When $N$ is an *exact multiple* of $g$ (as $32 = 8 \times 4$ here), the saving is always exactly **50%** for 2-bit groups. For 3-bit groups (saving = $\frac{8 - 3}{8} = 62.5\%$), the saving is even greater, but the decoder complexity increases. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5: Wilkes Control Matrix (Classical Reference) M. V. Wilkes (1951) proposed the first microprogrammed control unit as a **diode matrix**: - The matrix has $W$ rows (one per microinstruction) and $C$ columns (one per control signal). - A diode is placed at row $r$, column $c$ if microinstruction $r$ must activate signal $c$. - The matrix is read out row-by-row into the CDR. Bit-width of a Wilkes microinstruction = $C$ (i.e., horizontal). The matrix is a *physical* realization of a horizontal ROM. The next address logic in Wilkes' design is **two-way branching** based on a single condition bit. > [!TIP] > **KTU Viva Question:** *"Who invented the microprogrammed control unit?"* — Answer: **Maurice Vincent Wilkes**, Cambridge, 1951. The 1967 Turing Award was given to him primarily for this work. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Microprogrammed Control Unit ```mermaid graph TB CLK["Clock Pulse"] --> CAR1["CAR"] CAR1 -->|"Address k bits"| CM["Control Memory ROM
Size 2^k x n"] CM -->|"Microinstruction n bits"| CDR["CDR"] CDR -->|"Control Field F1..Fp"| DEC["Decoder Matrix"] DEC -->|"Control Signals"| DP["CPU Datapath
ALU, Registers, Buses"] DP -->|"Condition Flags CC"| NAL["Next Address Logic"] CDR -->|"CD + BA fields"| NAL EXT["External Opcode
from IR"] --> NAL NAL -->|"New Address k bits"| CAR2["CAR update"] CAR2 --> CAR1 style CM fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style CDR fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style DEC fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px style NAL fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the diagram:** The clock increments CAR, the address selects one row of the Control Memory ROM, the row is latched into CDR, the control field is decoded and emitted as active-high/active-low signals to the datapath, and the *next* address is computed by NAL using the branch + condition fields and the CPU flags, then loaded back into CAR. --- ### 4.2 Microinstruction Execution Flow (Mermaid State-Chart Style) ```mermaid graph TB A["Start: Clock Edge"] --> B["CAR drives Address Bus"] B --> C["CM ROM Read
Access Time t_acc"] C --> D["Microinstruction Latched
into CDR"] D --> E{"Format Type?"} E -->|"Horizontal"| F1["Fan-out directly
to control points
No decoding"] E -->|"Vertical"| F2["Sub-field decoding
3-to-8 or 2-to-4 decoders
Delay t_dec added"] F1 --> G["Datapath performs
micro-operation"] F2 --> G G --> H["Flags updated
Z, N, C, V"] H --> I{"Branch Condition
CD field satisfied?"} I -->|"Yes"| J["CAR <= Branch Address BA"] I -->|"No"| K["CAR <= Next Address NA
or CAR+1"] J --> L["End of micro-cycle"] K --> L L --> A style F1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style F2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style I fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 ``` --- ### 4.3 Microinstruction Word Format (Generic) ```mermaid graph LR A["Microinstruction Word"] --> B["Control Field
F1 F2 ... Fp
p sub-fields"] A --> C["Condition Field CD
2-3 bits"] A --> D["Branch Address BA
k bits"] A --> E["Next Address NA
k bits"] style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:3px style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style D fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f ``` --- ### 4.4 Horizontal vs. Vertical Format — Visual Comparison ```mermaid graph TB subgraph H["Horizontal Format - 17 bits wide"] H1["s1 s2 s3 s4 s5 s6 s7 s8 s9 s10 s11 s12 s13 s14 s15 s16 s17"] end subgraph V["Vertical Format - 9 bits wide with 3 sub-fields"] V1["F1: 3 bits to 3-to-8 decoder"] V2["F2: 3 bits to 3-to-8 decoder"] V3["F3: 3 bits to 3-to-8 decoder"] V1 --> V2 V2 --> V3 end H -->|"Direct fan-out, no decoder"| HOUT["17 control lines"] V -->|"After 3 decoders"| VOUT["17 control lines"] style H fill:#e8f5e9 style V fill:#fff3e0 style HOUT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px style VOUT fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Read the diagram carefully:** Both formats *eventually* produce the same 17 control signals, but the horizontal format is **faster** (1-level fan-out) while the vertical format is **more compact** (1 ROM word per microinstruction is only 9 bits instead of 17). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Instructions (as per KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer any **2** questions out of 3. Each carries 3 marks. Word limit: ~80–120 words. #### Q1. Define microinstruction and microprogram. How are they related? `[L1 – Remember]` *`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | PBCIT304] | Module 3`* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **microinstruction** is a binary control word stored in the control memory that specifies one set of simultaneous micro-operations to be executed in a single clock cycle. A **microprogram** is an ordered sequence of microinstructions that together implement the fetch, decode, and execute phases of a *single* machine-language instruction. Relationship: *one machine instruction → one microprogram → many microinstructions*. The entire collection of microprograms is called **firmware**. --- #### Q2. Differentiate between horizontal and vertical microinstructions based on bit-width and decoding. `[L2 – Understand]` *`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | PBCIT304] | Module 3`* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Horizontal | Vertical | |---|---|---| | Bit-width | Wide (1 bit per signal, $\ge 50$ bits typical) | Narrow (encoded sub-fields, $16$–$32$ bits typical) | | Decoding | **Not required** (direct fan-out) | **Required** (one decoder per sub-field) | | Speed | Faster (no decoder delay) | Slower (decoder adds $T_{dec}$) | | Bit efficiency | Low (many inactive bits) | High (compact encoding) | --- #### Q3. What is the role of Control Address Register (CAR) and Control Data Register (CDR) in a microprogrammed control unit? `[L2 – Understand]` *`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | PBCIT304] | Module 3`* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **CAR** (Control Address Register) holds the address of the *next* microinstruction to be fetched from the control memory. It functions as the *program counter* of the micro-level. The **CDR** (Control Data Register) latches the microinstruction *just read* from control memory, holding it steady while the control field is decoded and dispatched to the datapath. Together, CAR+CDR implement a **two-stage pipeline** at the micro-level, allowing the next microinstruction to be fetched while the current one is being executed. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Answer any **ONE** full question from the two alternatives. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Word limit: 8–10 pages of answer script. --- #### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) *`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | PBCIT304] | Module 3`* **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the organization of a **microprogrammed control unit**. Label all major blocks: Control Memory, CAR, CDR, Decoder, and Next Address Logic. Describe the fetch–decode–execute cycle at the micro-level. **(b) [7 marks]** A CPU has **$N = 24$ control signals** and a **$k = 10$**-bit CAR.     (i) Design a **horizontal** microinstruction format. Compute ROM size.     (ii) Design a **vertical** microinstruction format with **$g = 8$** signals per group. Compute ROM size and percentage saving. --- #### ✅ Model Answer — Question A **(a) Microprogrammed Control Unit Organization [7 marks]** A microprogrammed control unit replaces the hardwired combinational logic with a stored microprogram. The major blocks are: 1. **Control Memory (CM):** A ROM of size $2^k \times n$ where each word is one microinstruction. 2. **Control Address Register (CAR):** Holds the address of the next microinstruction (analogous to PC). 3. **Control Data Register (CDR):** Latches the microinstruction just read from CM. 4. **Decoder:** Converts the encoded control field of CDR into active control signals. 5. **Next Address Logic (NAL):** Computes the next value of CAR using the BA field, the CD (condition) field, and the CPU condition flags. **Micro-cycle (per clock):** - *Step 1:* Clock edge → CAR value placed on CM address bus. - *Step 2:* After access time $t_{acc}$, the selected microinstruction appears on the CM data bus and is latched into CDR. - *Step 3:* The control field is decoded into control signals that drive the datapath for one micro-operation. - *Step 4:* In parallel, the NAL examines the CD field; if the condition is true, CAR is loaded with BA, else CAR is incremented to NA. - *Step 5:* Cycle repeats at the next clock edge. (Neat block diagram to be drawn — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical diagram.) --- **(b) Numerical Design [7 marks]** **Given:** $N = 24$ control signals, $k = 10$ address bits. **(i) Horizontal Format [3.5 marks]:** $$ H = N = 24 \text{ bits} $$ $$ C_{M}^{\text{horiz}} = 2^{10} \times 24 = 1024 \times 24 = 24{,}576 \text{ bits} = 3 \text{ KB} $$ **(ii) Vertical Format with $g = 8$ [3.5 marks]:** Number of groups: $$ p = \lceil 24/8 \rceil = 3 \text{ groups} $$ Bits per group: $$ b = \lceil \log_2(8) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits} $$ Total vertical width: $$ V = 3 \times 3 = 9 \text{ bits} $$ $$ C_{M}^{\text{vert}} = 2^{10} \times 9 = 1024 \times 9 = 9{,}216 \text{ bits} \approx 1.125 \text{ KB} $$ Percentage saving: $$ \text{Saving} = \frac{24{,}576 - 9{,}216}{24{,}576} \times 100\% = \frac{15{,}360}{24{,}576} \times 100\% = 62.5\,\% $$ **Conclusion:** The vertical format saves **62.5%** of ROM capacity at the cost of three 3-to-8 decoders. --- #### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* *`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | PBCIT304] | Module 3`* **(a) [7 marks]** Explain **Wilkes' microprogrammed control unit** design. With a diagram, show how the diode matrix implements the microinstruction word. What are its limitations compared to modern ROM-based designs? **(b) [7 marks]** Compare **horizontal and vertical microinstructions** in a tabular form with at least **eight** parameters. State one real-world example of each. Why are diagonal microinstructions becoming popular in modern CPUs? --- #### ✅ Model Answer — Question B **(a) Wilkes' Microprogrammed Control Unit [7 marks]** Maurice Wilkes (1951) proposed the first microprogrammed control unit using a **diode matrix**: - The matrix has $W$ rows (one per microinstruction) and $C$ columns (one per control signal of the datapath). - A **diode** is inserted at position $(r, c)$ if and only if microinstruction $r$ must activate signal $c$. - The matrix is read out *row-wise*. One row at a time is energized, and the diode-OR outputs drive the control points of the datapath. - A second diode matrix implements the *next address* logic, supporting **two-way branches** based on a single external condition bit. - Each microinstruction word effectively has $C + 1$ bits (one per control signal + branch condition). **Limitations compared to modern ROM designs:** 1. **Fixed at fabrication** — a blown diode cannot be reprogrammed. 2. **No multi-way branching** — only two-way (true/false) branches. 3. **Diodes are bulky** — a modern ROM is 10–100× denser. 4. **No Writable Control Store (WCS)** — microcode updates impossible. 5. **Slow access** — diode matrix propagation is slower than a ROM sense amplifier. (Diode-matrix diagram: drawn as a grid with dots representing diodes, output lines on the right-hand side connecting to the datapath control points.) --- **(b) Horizontal vs. Vertical Microinstructions [7 marks]** | # | Parameter | Horizontal | Vertical | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bit-width | Wide ($\sim 50$–$100$ bits) | Narrow ($\sim 16$–$32$ bits) | | 2 | Encoding | None — 1 bit per signal | Field-encoded with sub-decoders | | 3 | Parallelism | High (many signals simultaneously) | Low (one per decoded group) | | 4 | Decoding hardware | None | One decoder per sub-field | | 5 | Cycle time | Short (no decoder) | Longer (decoder delay) | | 6 | Firmware size | Larger (wasted bits) | Compact (efficient) | | 7 | Design complexity | Simple | Complex (encoder/decoder) | | 8 | Readability | Hardware-friendly | Looks like ISA, programmer-friendly | | 9 | Real-world example | **Intel 8086** (early microcode) | **IBM System/360** microcode | | 10 | Speed class | Used in CISC, fast pipelines | Used in RISC emulation, MCUs | **Why diagonal microinstructions are popular:** Modern CPUs (e.g., Intel x86 since Pentium Pro, AMD Zen) use a **diagonal** or *hybrid* format where frequently co-activated control signals are left *unencoded* (1 bit each) for speed, while mutually exclusive signals are encoded to save ROM. This balances speed and ROM efficiency, achieving the best of both horizontal and vertical worlds while supporting **microcode updates** (e.g., Spectre/Meltdown patches) via Writable Control Store. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Forgetting the $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ ceiling function** when computing the number of groups $p$ in a vertical format. Marks lost: 1–2. > 2. **Confusing $k$ (CAR width) with $N$ (control signals).** They are completely different. $k$ determines the number of microinstructions; $N$ determines the bit-width of each. > 3. **Not drawing the Decoder block** explicitly in the block diagram. A common 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Writing "horizontal is better" or "vertical is better"** as an absolute statement. KTU expects a *trade-off* analysis. > 5. **Omitting units** in numerical answers (e.g., writing "24,576" without "bits" or "3 KB"). > 6. **Ignoring the condition flag inputs** to the Next Address Logic — must show Z, N, C, V flags entering the NAL block. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - ✅ A **microinstruction** is a binary control word for *one* micro-operation. - ✅ A **microprogram** is a sequence of microinstructions implementing *one* macro-instruction. - ✅ **CAR** = micro-PC; **CDR** = micro-IR; **CM** = control memory (ROM). - ✅ **Horizontal** = wide, 1 bit per signal, no decoder, fast. - ✅ **Vertical** = narrow, field-encoded, decoder per field, compact. - ✅ **Diagonal / Hybrid** = best of both; used in modern Intel/AMD CPUs. - ✅ **Wilkes (1951)** = inventor of microprogrammed control; **Turing Award 1967**. - ✅ **Formula $H = N$** for horizontal bit-width. - ✅ **Formula $V = p \cdot \lceil \log_2 g \rceil$** for vertical bit-width, with $p = \lceil N/g \rceil$. - ✅ **Formula $C_M = 2^k \times n$** for control memory size in bits. - ✅ **Encoding efficiency** $\eta = V/H \times 100\%$ — lower is more compact. - ✅ **Trade-off rule:** Horizontal is faster, Vertical is smaller, Diagonal is balanced. - ✅ **Microcode updates** (e.g., Spectre, Meltdown, F00F bug fix) are the modern use of Writable Control Store. - ✅ **Mutually exclusive signals** *must* be placed in the *same* vertical sub-field. - ✅ The condition (CD) field is **2–3 bits** selecting among Z, N, C, V, I, and Unconditional. - ✅ For KTU numericals, always show units: *bits, bytes, KB, MB*. - ✅ Always use **$\lceil \cdot \rceil$** when dividing signals into groups — never $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$. - ✅ The micro-cycle is **fetch-CMR → latch-CDR → decode → drive-datapath → compute-NAL** all in one clock. - ✅ **Intel x86, IBM 360, VAX, AMD Zen** = real CPUs that use microprogrammed (or hybrid) control. - ✅ **ARM Cortex-M series** = hardwired (RISC) — used as the contrast example in viva questions.

topics.title: Memory System: Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT304 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Memory System: Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Memory System** of a digital computer is the organized hierarchy of storage devices (registers, cache, primary/main memory, and secondary storage) that work cooperatively to store binary data and instructions required by the processor. It is characterized by the trade-off between **three fundamental parameters**: access time, storage capacity, and cost per bit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A memory system is **not** a single device — it is a *hierarchy* of devices, each optimized for a specific role in the data storage–retrieval pipeline. The guiding principle is: *smaller, faster, more expensive memory closer to the CPU; larger, slower, cheaper memory farther away.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Desk" Model

Imagine a student working at a study desk:

- **Active Notebook (on the desk)** = *CPU Registers* — accessed in nanoseconds, but only a few pages fit.
- **Small Drawer (just below the desk)** = *Cache Memory* — accessed very quickly, holds frequently used books/chapters.
- **Bookshelf in the room** = *Main Memory (RAM)* — bigger, but takes a few seconds to walk to and grab a book.
- **Library in another building** = *Secondary Storage (Hard Disk / SSD)* — huge capacity, but requires travel time.

The student instinctively keeps currently-used material close (small, fast memory) and archived material far (large, slow memory). This is *exactly* the principle behind the **Memory Hierarchy** in computer architecture.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of a Memory Device

Every memory device in the hierarchy is characterized by the following parameters:

- **Capacity** — total number of bits/bytes the device can store.
- **Access Time** — the time required to perform a read or write operation once the address is presented.
- **Cycle Time** — the minimum time between two successive independent memory operations.
- **Cost per Bit** — the economic cost of storing a single bit of information.
- **Volatility** — whether the contents are lost when power is removed (RAM is *volatile*; ROM is *non-volatile*).
- **Access Mode** — Random Access (same time for any address), Sequential Access (faster for neighboring addresses), or Direct Access (combination of both).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must clearly distinguish between *random access memory* (RAM), *read-only memory* (ROM and its variants: PROM, EPROM, EEPROM), and *associative memory* (also called Content-Addressable Memory, CAM). Each plays a distinct role in the basic memory system.

### 1.4 Major Memory Types at a Glance

| Memory Type | Volatility | Access Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Register | Volatile | Direct | Inside CPU |
| Cache (SRAM) | Volatile | Random | L1/L2/L3 between CPU and RAM |
| Main Memory (DRAM) | Volatile | Random | Working storage for programs |
| ROM / PROM / EPROM | Non-volatile | Random | Firmware, boot code |
| Associative Memory (CAM) | Volatile | Content-based | TLB, network routers |
| Magnetic Disk / SSD | Non-volatile | Direct / Sequential | Mass storage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Memory Hierarchy — Why It Exists

No single memory technology simultaneously satisfies the three conflicting demands of a computer system: **speed**, **capacity**, and **low cost**. The memory hierarchy resolves this conflict by combining multiple technologies so that the *effective* system appears fast (top of hierarchy) and large (bottom of hierarchy) at a *moderate* cost.

The hierarchy, from fastest/smallest to slowest/largest:

1. **CPU Registers** — built using flip-flops; access time ≈ **1 nanosecond** or less.
2. **Cache Memory (SRAM)** — small, very fast; access time ≈ **2–10 ns**.
3. **Main Memory (DRAM)** — primary working memory; access time ≈ **50–100 ns**.
4. **Secondary Storage (Magnetic Disk / SSD)** — bulk storage; access time ≈ **milliseconds (disk)** or **microseconds (SSD)**.
5. **Tertiary / Off-line Storage** — tapes, optical media; access time ≈ **seconds**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Because of the *Principle of Locality* (both temporal and spatial), most programs access a small subset of memory repeatedly. By placing that subset in faster, smaller memories, the *average* access time approaches that of the topmost level.

### 2.2 Random Access Memory (RAM)

**Static RAM (SRAM):**
- Built using **6 transistors per bit cell** (a flip-flop).
- Retains data as long as power is supplied; **no refresh required**.
- Faster, more expensive, used for **cache memory**.

**Dynamic RAM (DRAM):**
- Built using **1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit cell**.
- Capacitor leaks charge → requires **periodic refresh** (every few milliseconds).
- Denser, cheaper, used for **main memory**.

### 2.3 Read-Only Memory (ROM) and Its Variants

- **ROM (Mask ROM):** Programmed at the time of manufacture; cannot be modified.
- **PROM (Programmable ROM):** Can be programmed *once* by the user using a special device.
- **EPROM (Erasable PROM):** Can be erased using **ultraviolet light** and reprogrammed.
- **EEPROM (Electrically Erasable PROM):** Can be erased electrically, byte-by-byte.
- **Flash Memory:** A modern, block-erasable variant of EEPROM; the basis of SSDs and USB drives.

### 2.4 Associative Memory (Content-Addressable Memory — CAM)

In conventional RAM, the CPU supplies an **address**, and memory returns the contents at that address. In **associative memory**, the CPU supplies a portion of the *content* (a *tag* or *key*), and memory searches **all locations in parallel** to find a match. The key features are:

- All comparisons are done **simultaneously** using hardware comparators.
- Retrieval is based on *content*, not *location*.
- **Access time is constant**, independent of the number of stored words.
- Used in **Translation Lookaside Buffers (TLBs)**, cache tag arrays, and network routers.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum set of formulas** that KTU examiners expect students to be able to recall and apply in Module 4 problems. The **vertical bar symbol is written as `\vert`** to preserve markdown table compatibility.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Memory Capacity | $C = 2^n \times m$ | $n$ = address lines, $m$ = word size in bits; result in **bits** |
| 2 | Address Range | $0$ to $2^n - 1$ | For $n$ address bits |
| 3 | Number of Addressable Words | $N = 2^n$ | Words |
| 4 | Memory Size (bytes) | $\text{Size} = 2^n \times m \,/\, 8$ | Bytes |
| 5 | Average Access Time (2-level) | $T_{avg} = h \cdot T_1 + (1-h) \cdot T_2$ | $h$ = hit ratio, $T_1, T_2$ = level access times |
| 6 | Access Time Ratio | $r = T_2 / T_1$ | $r \ge 1$ |
| 7 | Cost per Bit | $\text{Cost} = c_1 \cdot S_1 + c_2 \cdot S_2$ | $c$ = per-bit cost, $S$ = size in bits |
| 8 | Hit Ratio | $h = \dfrac{\text{Number of hits}}{\text{Total memory accesses}}$ | $0 \le h \le 1$ |
| 9 | Miss Ratio | $\text{Miss Ratio} = 1 - h$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Speedup (cache) | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_{avg}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Associative Memory Match Time | $T_{match} = T_{compare}$ | Independent of $N$ (number of words) |
| 12 | Refresh Interval (DRAM) | $T_{refresh} \le 64 \text{ ms}$ (typical) | Standard JEDEC specification |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

The principles established in this module underpin the design of **every modern computing device**:

- **Smartphones** use a multi-level cache hierarchy atop LPDDR RAM and flash storage.
- **Data Centers** balance expensive SRAM-based cache with cheaper DRAM and disk tiers to optimize cost-per-operation.
- **Embedded Systems** (e.g., automotive ECUs) rely heavily on ROM/Flash for firmware and use associative memory in routing lookups.
- **Network Routers** depend on TCAM (Ternary CAM) for high-speed packet classification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Memory Capacity from Address Lines

**Problem Setup:** A memory chip has $n$ address lines and $m$ data lines. Derive the total storage capacity.

**Step 1 — Number of addressable locations:**
With $n$ address lines, each line can carry a binary value (0 or 1). The total number of distinct combinations is $2^n$. Therefore the number of addressable *words* is:

$$N = 2^n$$

**Step 2 — Bits per word:**
Each location stores a word of $m$ bits (because there are $m$ data lines, each carrying one bit at a time).

**Step 3 — Total capacity in bits:**
Multiply the number of words by the bits per word:

$$C_{bits} = N \times m = 2^n \times m$$

**Step 4 — Capacity in bytes:**
Divide the bit count by 8:

$$C_{bytes} = \dfrac{2^n \times m}{8}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
A memory chip has $n = 20$ address lines and $m = 8$ data lines. Compute its capacity.

- Number of words: $N = 2^{20} = 1,\!048,\!576$ words (exactly **1 Mega-word**).
- Total bits: $C_{bits} = 2^{20} \times 8 = 8,\!388,\!608$ bits.
- Total bytes: $C_{bytes} = 2^{20} = 1,\!048,\!576$ bytes = **1 MB**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Memory Access Time (Two-Level Hierarchy)

**Problem Setup:** A computer has a cache (level 1) with access time $T_1$ and main memory (level 2) with access time $T_2$. The *hit ratio* in the cache is $h$. Derive the average access time $T_{avg}$.

**Step 1 — Probability interpretation:**
$h$ is the fraction of accesses that find the data in level 1 (cache hit). The fraction $1 - h$ of accesses miss the cache and must be serviced by level 2 (main memory).

**Step 2 — Time for a hit:**
When a hit occurs, the access takes $T_1$ time units.

**Step 3 — Time for a miss:**
When a miss occurs, the access takes $T_1$ (to discover the miss by checking the cache) plus $T_2$ (to retrieve from main memory) → a total of $(T_1 + T_2)$.

**Step 4 — Weighted average:**

$$
T_{avg} = h \cdot T_1 + (1 - h)(T_1 + T_2)
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the expression:**
Expand and group like terms:

$$
T_{avg} = h \cdot T_1 + T_1 + T_2 - h \cdot T_1 - h \cdot T_2
$$

The $h \cdot T_1$ terms cancel:

$$
T_{avg} = T_1 + (1 - h) \cdot T_2
$$

**This is the canonical KTU formula** for average access time in a two-level memory system.

**Worked Numerical Example:**
Cache access time $T_1 = 10$ ns, main memory access time $T_2 = 100$ ns, hit ratio $h = 0.9$.

$$
T_{avg} = 10 + (1 - 0.9) \times 100 = 10 + 0.1 \times 100 = 10 + 10 = 20 \text{ ns}
$$

**Speedup over plain main memory:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_{avg}} = \dfrac{100}{20} = 5
$$

The system is effectively **5 times faster** than using main memory alone.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Associative Memory Match Time Bound

**Problem Setup:** Show that the search time in an associative memory of $N$ words is *independent* of $N$.

**Step 1 — Architecture:**
An associative memory array has $N$ words arranged in parallel rows. Each row contains a comparator circuit.

**Step 2 — Search operation:**
The key (search argument) is broadcast simultaneously to **every row's comparator** in a single clock cycle.

**Step 3 — Comparator delay:**
Each comparator produces a MATCH signal after a fixed gate delay $T_{gate}$, irrespective of the row number.

**Step 4 — Aggregation:**
A priority encoder collects the MATCH signals; its delay is also a constant $T_{enc}$, independent of $N$.

**Step 5 — Total search time:**

$$
T_{search} = T_{gate} + T_{enc} = \text{constant}
$$

This is why CAM is called *Content-Addressable* — its search time is **O(1)** in computer-science complexity terminology.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs Across Memory Levels

| Level | Technology | Access Time | Capacity | Cost/bit | Volatility |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| L0 (Register) | Flip-flops (CMOS) | $\le 1$ ns | $\approx 32 \times 64$ bits | Very high | Volatile |
| L1 Cache | SRAM | $2 - 5$ ns | $32 - 128$ KB | High | Volatile |
| L2 Cache | SRAM | $5 - 20$ ns | $256$ KB – $8$ MB | Moderate | Volatile |
| Main Memory | DRAM | $50 - 100$ ns | $4 - 64$ GB | Low | Volatile |
| Secondary | SSD / HDD | $\mu$s – ms | $256$ GB – $10$ TB | Very low | Non-volatile |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice how each successive level is roughly **5–10× slower** and **4–10× cheaper** than the level above it. This *logarithmic* stepping is what makes a hierarchical memory system economically feasible.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the *block-level functional architecture* of the memory hierarchy, showing how data flows between the CPU and successive storage levels.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU EXECUTION UNIT"]
    REG["REGISTER FILE L0 ACCESS 1 NS"]
    L1["L1 CACHE SRAM 32 TO 128 KB"]
    L2["L2 CACHE SRAM 256 KB TO 8 MB"]
    L3["L3 CACHE SRAM SHARED 8 TO 64 MB"]
    RAM["MAIN MEMORY DRAM 4 TO 64 GB"]
    SSD["SECONDARY STORAGE SSD OR HDD"]
    TAPE["TERTIARY STORAGE TAPE OPTICAL"]

    CPU <--> REG
    CPU <--> L1
    L1 <--> L2
    L2 <--> L3
    L3 <--> RAM
    RAM <--> SSD
    SSD <--> TAPE

    REG -.->|FASTEST SMALLEST| L1
    L1 -.->|FASTER SMALL| L2
    L2 -.->|MODERATE| L3
    L3 -.->|LARGE SLOWER| RAM
    RAM -.->|LARGE SLOW| SSD
    SSD -.->|LARGEST SLOWEST| TAPE
```

### 4.2 Associative Memory Internal Organization

The block-level functional architecture of an associative memory (CAM) shows the parallel comparator and the priority encoder.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KEY["KEY REGISTER SEARCH ARGUMENT"]
    WD0["WORD 0 STORAGE CELLS"]
    WD1["WORD 1 STORAGE CELLS"]
    WD2["WORD 2 STORAGE CELLS"]
    WD3["WORD 3 STORAGE CELLS"]
    CMP0["COMPARATOR 0"]
    CMP1["COMPARATOR 1"]
    CMP2["COMPARATOR 2"]
    CMP3["COMPARATOR 3"]
    ENC["PRIORITY ENCODER"]
    OUT["MATCH ADDRESS OUTPUT"]

    KEY --> CMP0
    KEY --> CMP1
    KEY --> CMP2
    KEY --> CMP3

    WD0 --> CMP0
    WD1 --> CMP1
    WD2 --> CMP2
    WD3 --> CMP3

    CMP0 --> ENC
    CMP1 --> ENC
    CMP2 --> ENC
    CMP3 --> ENC
    ENC --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Basic Memory System Block Diagram

The block-level functional architecture of a generic RAM chip shows the address decoder, storage array, and bidirectional data path.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ADDR["ADDRESS BUS n BITS"]
    DATA["DATA BUS m BITS BIDIRECTIONAL"]
    CTRL["CONTROL SIGNALS READ AND WRITE"]
    DECODER["ADDRESS DECODER"]
    ARRAY["STORAGE ARRAY 2 TO THE N BY M BITS"]
    BUFFER["DATA INPUT OUTPUT BUFFER"]

    ADDR --> DECODER
    DECODER --> ARRAY
    CTRL --> ARRAY
    CTRL --> BUFFER
    ARRAY <--> BUFFER
    BUFFER <--> DATA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** When the CPU places an address on the **address bus** and asserts the **READ** control line, the address decoder activates exactly one row (word line) in the storage array, and the corresponding $m$-bit word appears on the data bus. For a **WRITE** operation, the CPU places the data on the bus and asserts the **WRITE** control; the decoder selects the target word line, and the data is latched into the storage cells.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "memory hierarchy." Why is it necessary in a modern computer system?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The memory hierarchy is a structured arrangement of different memory devices — from fast/small/expensive at the top to slow/large/cheap at the bottom — organized to optimize the trade-off between access speed, capacity, and cost.

It is necessary because:
1. **No single memory technology** simultaneously satisfies the conflicting requirements of large capacity, low cost, and fast access.
2. The *principle of locality* (temporal and spatial) allows frequently accessed data to be kept in faster memories.
3. It provides an *illusion* of a single, large, fast memory at a moderate overall cost.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM. Mention one application area for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SRAM | DRAM |
|---|---|---|
| Cell structure | 6 transistors per bit | 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit |
| Refresh | Not required | Required every few ms |
| Density | Lower | Higher |
| Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Cost per bit | Higher | Lower |
| **Application** | Cache memory (L1/L2/L3) | Main memory (primary storage) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** *Explain the basic concepts of a memory system. Discuss the key parameters used to characterize a memory device.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A *memory system* is the collection of storage devices that retain data and instructions for use by the CPU. The basic concepts include:

1. **Storage of binary data** — every memory device stores bits grouped into *words*, with each word uniquely identified by an *address*.
2. **Random access** — the time to access any word is independent of its physical location (true for RAM; not for sequential media like tapes).
3. **Read/Write operations** — data can either be retrieved (read) or stored (write) at any addressable location.
4. **Volatility** — volatile memories (SRAM, DRAM) lose content on power-off; non-volatile memories (ROM, Flash, Disk) retain it.

**Key characterizing parameters:**

- **Capacity** — total number of bits/bytes the device can hold.
- **Access time** — delay between presenting the address and obtaining the data.
- **Cycle time** — minimum interval between two consecutive memory operations.
- **Cost per bit** — economic cost of storing one bit.
- **Power consumption** — important for portable/embedded systems.
- **Transfer rate** — bytes/sec between memory and CPU.
- **Access mode** — random, sequential, or direct.

> [!NOTE]
> A well-illustrated answer should mention that *access time* and *cycle time* are often confused; access time is the delay for *one* operation, while cycle time includes the recovery time before the *next* operation can start.

---

**(b)** *A two-level memory system has a cache with access time of 5 ns and main memory access time of 100 ns. The cache hit ratio is 0.95.*
   *   *(i) Calculate the average memory access time.*
   *   *(ii) Calculate the speedup achieved over a system without cache.*
   *   *(iii) What hit ratio would be required to achieve an average access time of 10 ns?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T_1 = 5$ ns (cache), $T_2 = 100$ ns (main memory), $h = 0.95$.

**(i) Average access time:**
Using the canonical formula:

$$
T_{avg} = T_1 + (1 - h) \cdot T_2
$$

$$
T_{avg} = 5 + (1 - 0.95) \times 100 = 5 + 0.05 \times 100 = 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ ns}
$$

**(ii) Speedup over no-cache system:**
Without cache, every access takes $T_2 = 100$ ns.

$$
\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_{avg}} = \dfrac{100}{10} = 10
$$

**(iii) Hit ratio for $T_{avg} = 10$ ns:**
Set $T_{avg} = 10$ ns and solve for $h$:

$$
10 = 5 + (1 - h) \times 100
$$

$$
5 = (1 - h) \times 100
$$

$$
1 - h = \dfrac{5}{100} = 0.05
$$

$$
h = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95 = 95 \%
$$

The current hit ratio of 0.95 is *exactly* what is needed. Any drop in the hit ratio would push $T_{avg}$ above 10 ns.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the organization of an associative memory. List any two applications.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Organization (description of the block diagram shown in Section 4.2):**

An associative memory consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Key Register** — holds the search argument (the bit pattern being searched for).
2. **Storage Array** — contains $N$ words, each of $m$ bits.
3. **Comparators** — one per word. Each comparator receives the key and the corresponding stored word and outputs a MATCH (logic 1) if all $m$ bits match.
4. **Priority Encoder** — receives the $N$ MATCH signals and produces the address of the highest-priority matching word.
5. **Match Register / Output** — indicates whether a match was found, and if so, at which address.

**Operation (read):**
- Load the search key into the Key Register.
- All comparators fire in *parallel*.
- If exactly one comparator asserts MATCH, the encoder outputs that word's address, and the matching word can be read out.

**Two applications:**

1. **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** in virtual memory systems — used to translate virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers in a single cycle.
2. **Network Routers** use Ternary CAM (TCAM) to perform high-speed packet-header lookups (e.g., longest-prefix matching in IP routing tables).

---

**(b)** *A memory system has 16 address lines and 8 data lines. Calculate:*
   *   *(i) Total capacity in bits, bytes, and kilobytes.*
   *   *(ii) The address range of the memory.*
   *   *(iii) How many extra address lines are required to double the capacity?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 16$ address lines, $m = 8$ data lines.

**(i) Total capacity:**

- Number of words: $N = 2^{16} = 65,\!536$ words.
- Capacity in bits: $C_{bits} = 2^{16} \times 8 = 524,\!288$ bits.
- Capacity in bytes: $C_{bytes} = 524,\!288 \,/\, 8 = 65,\!536$ bytes.
- Capacity in KB: $C_{KB} = 65,\!536 \,/\, 1024 = 64$ KB.

**(ii) Address range:**
The smallest address is $0$ and the largest is $2^{16} - 1 = 65,\!535$.

$$
\text{Address Range} = 0 \text{ to } 65,\!535 \quad \text{(decimal)}
$$

In hexadecimal: `0000H` to `FFFFH`.

**(iii) Lines required to double capacity:**
Current capacity is $2^{16} \times 8 = 2^{19}$ bits. Doubling requires $2^{20} \times 8 = 2^{20}$ words.

$$
2^{16} \times 2 = 2^{17}
$$

So we need $n_{new} = 17$ address lines. The number of *extra* address lines is:

$$
\Delta n = 17 - 16 = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** Adding *just one* address line doubles the addressable capacity (because capacity grows *exponentially* with the number of address lines).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $T_1$ term** in the average-access-time formula. Students often write $T_{avg} = h \cdot T_1 + (1-h) \cdot T_2$ directly *without* simplifying, and lose a mark if the question asks for the "canonical" form $T_{avg} = T_1 + (1-h) T_2$.
> 2. **Confusing access time and cycle time.** Always state clearly which is which in your answer.
> 3. **Using the wrong base** for capacity conversion. $1 \text{ KB} = 1024$ bytes in KTU numerical problems, *not* 1000. (Computer-science context.)
> 4. **Omitting the address-range question.** Examiners expect the range to be written explicitly as $0$ to $2^n - 1$ — not just the *number* of words.
> 5. **No diagram for the associative-memory question.** A block diagram is essentially *mandatory* for full marks; a textual description alone is worth at most 4 of the 7 marks.
> 6. **Units missing** — write **ns, $\mu$s, ms, bytes, KB, MB** explicitly. Marks are routinely deducted for unitless numerical answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memory System: Basic Concepts**

- A **memory system** is a *hierarchy* of devices, not a single chip.
- The hierarchy (top→bottom): **Registers → Cache (SRAM) → Main Memory (DRAM) → Secondary (Disk/SSD) → Tertiary (Tape/Optical)**.
- The hierarchy is justified by the **principle of locality** (temporal + spatial).
- **SRAM** uses 6 transistors per bit, no refresh, fast, expensive → used for cache.
- **DRAM** uses 1T + 1C per bit, needs refresh, slower, cheap → used for main memory.
- **ROM variants:** Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM (UV-erasable) → EEPROM (electrically erasable) → Flash (block-erasable EEPROM).
- **Associative Memory (CAM)** searches by *content*, not *address*; access time is independent of $N$.
- **Capacity formula:** $C = 2^n \times m$ bits, where $n$ = address lines, $m$ = data lines.
- **Average access time:** $T_{avg} = T_1 + (1 - h) \cdot T_2$ — *memorize this canonical form*.
- **Hit ratio $h$** = (number of hits) / (total accesses); **miss ratio** = $1 - h$.
- **Speedup** with cache = $T_2 \,/\, T_{avg}$.
- Doubling the number of address lines **quadruples the capacity** ($2 \times$ lines → $4 \times$ capacity).
- $1 \text{ KB} = 1024$ bytes, $1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \text{ KB}$, $1 \text{ GB} = 1024 \text{ MB}$ in KTU numerical problems.
- **Address range** is always $0$ to $2^n - 1$.
- Always **draw a block diagram** for any associative-memory question — it is worth at least 3 of 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semiconductor RAMs, Memory System Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT304 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Semiconductor RAMs, Memory System Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Semiconductor Memory: The Formal Definition

**Semiconductor Memory** refers to the on-chip and off-chip digital storage devices fabricated using semiconductor materials (primarily **Silicon** — Si) in which binary data is stored using electronic circuits (transistors and capacitors) rather than magnetic or optical media. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, semiconductor memory is formally classified along two primary axes:

- **Volatility axis** → Volatile (loses data on power-off) vs. Non-Volatile (retains data without power).
- **Access mechanism axis** → Random Access Memory (RAM, equal access time to any location) vs. Sequential Access Memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "RAM" in this context is a *historical misnomer*. It does **not** imply Read-Write capability alone. Both SRAM and DRAM are technically "RAM," but ROM variants (PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash) are also random-access. The dividing line is **volatility**, not access pattern.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Three-Tier Library

Imagine a vast **University Library** with the following tiers:

| Tier | Analogy | Real Hardware |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Tier 1: Your Desk** | A few books open at once — instant access | **CPU Registers** (~ 1 ns, ~bytes) |
| **Tier 2: Your Backpack** | A small set of frequently used books | **Cache (SRAM)** (~ 5–10 ns, KB–MB) |
| **Tier 3: Library Stacks** | The entire collection — slow to fetch | **Main Memory (DRAM)** (~ 50–100 ns, GB) |
| **Tier 4: Off-Site Archive** | Rarely accessed backup | **Secondary Storage (HDD/SSD)** (~ ms, TB) |

> **The "Memory Hierarchy" is simply the engineering strategy of placing faster, smaller, more expensive memory close to the CPU and slower, larger, cheaper memory farther away.**

## 1.3 SRAM (Static RAM) — The "Light Switch" Memory

**SRAM** stores each bit in a **6-transistor (6T) bistable latch** made of two cross-coupled inverters. As long as power is supplied, the latch self-perpetuates its state — like a **light switch that stays in the last position you flicked it to**.

> [!NOTE]
